failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when the team is stuck too early on one frame or one answer.

failure_modes.mode: brainstorming vs confirmation-bias
failure_modes.description: Idea generation can become fake divergence when evaluation leaks in and only confirms the group’s current direction.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate divergent and convergent phases strictly: no critique, ranking, or feasibility filtering during idea generation.
failure_modes.source_quote: brainstorming vs confirmation-bias

failure_modes.mode: brainstorming vs anchoring
failure_modes.description: The first idea can anchor the session so strongly that later options only orbit around it instead of widening the possibility space.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require each participant to propose at least one idea that contradicts the first proposal; explicit counter-anchoring breaks the orbit.
failure_modes.source_quote: brainstorming vs anchoring

premortem_questions.description: Are we still genuinely widening the option set, or have we already started evaluating and defending favorites?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when divergence is explicitly separated from evaluation.

premortem_questions.description: What assumption is making the first idea feel like the natural reference point for everything else?
premortem_questions.source_quote: brainstorming vs anchoring

premortem_questions.description: What hidden alternative would matter if we forced ourselves to go beyond the current obvious set?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when idea volume is genuinely valuable because the search space is still underexplored.

heuristics.description: Separate divergence from evaluation so idea generation stays open long enough to produce real alternatives.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when divergence is explicitly separated from evaluation.

heuristics.description: Push for volume only when the search space is genuinely underexplored and hidden alternatives still matter.
heuristics.source_quote: Works when idea volume is genuinely valuable because the search space is still underexplored.

heuristics.description: Reset the session if the first answer has already become the implicit reference point.
heuristics.source_quote: brainstorming vs anchoring

danger_when: Evaluation is creeping into generation and selectively developing only ideas that confirm the current direction.
The search space is already well explored and additional divergence would mostly delay commitment.
brainstorming.name: Brainstorming
reframing_when: The question has already converged on a small set of options when the problem space has not been adequately explored — judgment has been applied too early, killing generative thinking.
The question treats the first satisfactory option as the answer (satisficing) when deliberately generating a larger quantity of alternatives would reveal structurally different approaches.
The question frames the situation as requiring evaluation ('which of these options?') when it actually requires generation ('what options exist that we have not considered?').
The question suppresses wild or unconventional framings because they feel impractical, when those framings contain structural insights that can be tamed into practical alternatives.
brainstorming.reframing_move: Before evaluating options, ask: 'Have we generated enough options to be confident we have found the right problem framing, or are we judging too early?' — the brainstorming reframe separates generation from evaluation and insists on quantity before quality.

select_when: A complex problem needs to be decomposed into manageable branches before the team can solve and recombine it coherently.
Workstreams are proliferating and the next risk is incoherent synthesis unless the merge rule is made explicit.
A high-stakes recommendation needs both rigorous analysis and a clear integration path back to one decision.
The team must move from scattered issue work toward a structured whole without losing the causal logic of each branch.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when teams branch aggressively but never define the merge rule
failure_modes.description: Branching fails when teams decompose aggressively but never define how the work will be recombined into one answer.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define the merge protocol before branching: specify the format, criteria, and owner responsible for recombination into a single answer.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams branch aggressively but never define the merge rule

failure_modes.mode: Maslow's Hammer (The Brittle Branch)
failure_modes.description: The model breaks when familiar frameworks are applied mechanically to a genuinely novel problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the decomposition against a genuinely novel aspect of the problem; if the branch structure was borrowed from a past project without adaptation, challenge it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Maslow's Hammer (The Brittle Branch)

failure_modes.mode: The Curse of Knowledge
failure_modes.description: Merge quality collapses when experts communicate in abstraction and peripheral data instead of distilling the core truth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require each branch owner to distill their output to the core finding before merge; abstraction and peripheral detail kill integration quality.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Curse of Knowledge

premortem_questions.description: What is the merge rule before we create more branches?
premortem_questions.source_quote: never define the merge rule

premortem_questions.description: Are these branches MECE enough that they expose the full problem without multiplying overlap?
premortem_questions.source_quote: MECE Decomposition

premortem_questions.description: What would show that we are forcing a familiar branch frame onto a novel problem?
premortem_questions.source_quote: apply familiar frameworks routinely and unquestioningly

heuristics.description: Decompose the problem into MECE branches before solving.
heuristics.source_quote: MECE Decomposition

heuristics.description: Solve each branch rigorously, but keep the future merge criterion visible while the work is still separable.
heuristics.source_quote: the Merge phase defines what emerges from that interaction

heuristics.description: Merge by explaining the answer plainly enough that the audience can follow the reasoning, not just admire the analysis.
heuristics.source_quote: write down an explanation of the concept in plain English

danger_when: Teams are branching aggressively without any credible merge rule, so more decomposition would only increase fragmentation.
The problem is genuinely novel and a familiar branch frame is being imposed too mechanically for comfort or speed.
branch-solve-merge.name: Branch Solve Merge

select_when: A small local decision could propagate through a tightly connected system and create outsized downstream effects.
First-order reasoning is too shallow because second- and third-order consequences dominate the real risk.
Leaders need to scan for cascade paths, not just isolated events or immediate outcomes.
The system is sensitive to initial conditions, sequencing, or amplification effects.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when small interventions may have large downstream effects.
failure_modes.description: A decision can look safe locally while still creating dangerous downstream effects once it propagates through the broader system.
failure_modes.mitigation: Trace the plausible transmission path from the local decision through the broader system; if the propagation mechanism cannot be named, the concern is speculation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when small interventions may have large downstream effects.

failure_modes.mode: Works when first-order reasoning is too shallow.
failure_modes.description: First-order reasoning misses real risk when second- and third-order consequences matter more than the immediate result.
failure_modes.mitigation: Explicitly map second- and third-order consequences before accepting a first-order-only risk assessment.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when first-order reasoning is too shallow.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Cascade Thinking Turns into Mysticism.
failure_modes.description: Cascade thinking becomes noise when people invoke butterfly-effect language without naming a plausible transmission path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require a named mechanism for any claimed cascade; invoking butterfly-effect language without a plausible path is not analysis.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Cascade Thinking Turns into Mysticism.

premortem_questions.description: If this small move goes wrong, through which specific chain of dependencies, delays, or feedback loops does the damage spread?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Visualizing All the Dominoes.

premortem_questions.description: Which second- or third-order consequence would matter most if the obvious first-order outcome turns out to be the least important one?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The model compels us to engage in second-order thinking.

premortem_questions.description: What evidence would prove that our cascade story is grounded in a real transmission path rather than vague fear?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger When Cascade Thinking Turns into Mysticism.

heuristics.description: Trace consequence chains before declaring a local optimization safe.
heuristics.source_quote: An action at one point will invariably ramify to create an effect at a distant one.

heuristics.description: Prioritize the few cascade paths that matter most instead of trying to model every variable in the system.
heuristics.source_quote: The goal is to be a catalyst for action, not to be complete and comprehensive.

heuristics.description: Name the plausible transmission path or stop using butterfly-effect language.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger When Cascade Thinking Turns into Mysticism.

danger_when: Butterfly-effect language is being used to justify vague fear or grandiosity without naming a plausible transmission path.
The team is trying to model every variable and is sliding into analysis paralysis instead of prioritizing the highest-leverage cascade paths.
butterfly-effect.name: Butterfly Effect

select_when: A high-stakes decision requires explicit downside sizing, scenario comparison, or sensitivity analysis rather than intuition alone.
The uncertainty parameters are estimable enough that cost-benefit logic, decision trees, or reference classes can improve the choice.
The quality of the decision process matters more than trying to guarantee a good outcome in an uncertain environment.
The work needs a structured way to compare bets, losses, and reversibility before committing capital, time, or reputation.

failure_modes.mode: Is the worst case bad enough?
failure_modes.description: Overweighting the base case can hide severe downside outcomes that matter more than the average scenario.
failure_modes.mitigation: Simulate outcomes one or two standard deviations from the mean; ask whether the worst case is survivable, not just whether the base case is attractive.
failure_modes.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

failure_modes.mode: Reference Class Forecasting
failure_modes.description: Reference-class errors can make a decision look safer or riskier because the current case was matched to the wrong historical structure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Scrutinize reference-class fit: line up the assumptions that make the historical comparison valid and test each against the current case.
failure_modes.source_quote: Reference Class Forecasting

failure_modes.mode: Sensitivity Analysis
failure_modes.description: Sensitivity-blind analysis can create false confidence by hiding how much the conclusion changes when key inputs move.
failure_modes.mitigation: Run sensitivity analysis on every key input; if a small change in one assumption flips the conclusion, the analysis has earned less confidence than it claims.
failure_modes.source_quote: Sensitivity Analysis

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What You Have to Believe Analysis

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: Which variable, probability, or payoff would have to move for this decision to stop being attractive?
premortem_questions.source_quote: performing a sensitivity analysis helps to test the stability of the conclusion

heuristics.description: Use decision trees or cost-benefit analysis when a choice depends on explicit branches, tradeoffs, and probabilities.
heuristics.source_quote: Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) and Decision Trees

heuristics.description: Run scenario analysis or simulation before a large commitment so the team can see the spread of plausible outcomes, not just the central case.
heuristics.source_quote: Scenario Analysis and Simulations

heuristics.description: Treat the choice as a bet and evaluate the process quality separately from whether luck later produces a good or bad outcome.
heuristics.source_quote: Decisions as Bets

danger_when: The team is treating calculable models as a substitute for judgment in a setting where the core drivers are still too unknown or unstable.
Quantification is becoming decorative certainty and is not changing the actual choice, hedge, or exposure limit.
calculated-risk-taking.name: Calculated Risk Taking

select_when: Repeated decisions are vulnerable to in-the-moment inconsistency and need a stable pre-committed boundary.
Option overload is degrading judgment quality and the first need is to narrow the field before evaluating details.
A team needs a stable decision rule instead of improvising each case from scratch.
Incident or case handling may require separating fundamentally different problem classes before standardization.

failure_modes.mode: universal process is tempting but incidents are structurally heterogeneous
failure_modes.description: A universal process can fail simply because different incident types were never separated before standardization.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate incident types before standardizing the process; a universal procedure that ignores category differences will fail the edge cases.
failure_modes.source_quote: universal process is tempting but incidents are structurally heterogeneous

failure_modes.mode: labels are assigned from salient traits instead of distributional evidence
failure_modes.description: Representativeness can assign a case to the wrong bucket based on salient traits instead of evidence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge representativeness assignments with evidence: check whether the salient trait that drove categorization is actually predictive of the outcome.
failure_modes.source_quote: labels are assigned from salient traits instead of distributional evidence

failure_modes.mode: Category Lock-In
failure_modes.description: Category lock-in turns yesterday's useful buckets into today's blinders.
failure_modes.mitigation: Revisit categories periodically; yesterday's useful buckets become today's blinders when the environment changes.
failure_modes.source_quote: Category Lock-In

premortem_questions.description: What evidence would show these cases are not one category but several, requiring different response logic?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What evidence would show the incident types in scope are not one category but two or more

premortem_questions.description: Which criteria belong in separate MECE branches before we start evaluating options?
premortem_questions.source_quote: MECE

premortem_questions.description: What inherited bucket is replacing real decomposition of the underlying mechanism?
premortem_questions.source_quote: inherited buckets replace decomposition of underlying mechanisms

heuristics.description: Use category decisions as advance fences against in-the-moment impulse and exception making.
heuristics.source_quote: onetime, advance choice about what options you can and cannot choose

heuristics.description: Break the problem down with logic trees before writing the rule that will govern future cases.
heuristics.source_quote: Problem Disaggregation using Logic Trees

heuristics.description: Force a category summary sentence after grouping so the bucket earns its place.
heuristics.source_quote: Grouping and Summarizing Process

danger_when: Yesterday's buckets are being preserved after the underlying causal structure has changed.
A universal playbook is being imposed on structurally different cases that first need category validation.
category-decisions.name: Category Decisions

select_when: A problem is attracting a fast blame story and the team needs to resist assigning causation before the structure is actually understood.
The current diagnosis may be confusing symptom, proximate trigger, and root cause because the first explanation feels morally or emotionally satisfying.
An incident or outcome needs structural cause tracing before responsibility, remediation, or redesign decisions are made.
The next move should separate hidden drivers from the most narratively available explanation.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When a Fast Blame Story Replaces Causal Inquiry
failure_modes.description: A satisfying blame story can arrive before the real causal structure is understood.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate symptom, proximate trigger, and root cause explicitly before accepting any blame story; satisfying explanations arrive fastest but are often wrong.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When a Fast Blame Story Replaces Causal Inquiry

failure_modes.mode: distinguish symptoms, proximate causes, and root causes
failure_modes.description: The model fails when symptom, proximate trigger, and root cause get collapsed into one emotionally available explanation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use structured root-cause techniques (fishbone, five whys) to resist collapsing multiple causal layers into one emotionally available explanation.
failure_modes.source_quote: distinguish symptoms, proximate causes, and root causes

failure_modes.mode: resist premature attribution, not responsible action forever
failure_modes.description: Attribution resistance becomes avoidance when the team keeps suspending judgment after the causal evidence is already sufficient.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision threshold: once sufficient causal evidence exists, commit to an attribution rather than letting resistance become avoidance.
failure_modes.source_quote: resist premature attribution, not responsible action forever

premortem_questions.description: Are we explaining the outcome with the most available story or with the best-supported cause chain?
premortem_questions.source_quote: the first story is not always the right cause

premortem_questions.description: What is symptom, what is proximate trigger, and what is root cause here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: distinguish symptoms, proximate causes, and root causes

premortem_questions.description: What incentives or structures might be generating the behavior we are tempted to personalize?
premortem_questions.source_quote: look at incentives and structures, not just visible actors

heuristics.description: Use root-cause discipline before assigning responsibility so the team does not confuse explanation with blame.
heuristics.source_quote: causal inquiry should precede confident attribution

heuristics.description: Run Five Whys or similar cause-tracing methods to push past the first narratively satisfying answer.
heuristics.source_quote: Five Whys

heuristics.description: Check whether incentives and system conditions are producing behavior that looks purely personal at first glance.
heuristics.source_quote: look at incentives and structures

danger_when: The team is using anti-blame language to avoid naming an obvious causal driver, accountable party, or concrete design flaw.
The situation already has good causal evidence and now needs action rather than another loop of attribution skepticism.
causal-attribution-resistance.name: Causal Attribution Resistance

select_when: A complex problem must be decomposed into smaller linked steps before any trustworthy answer is possible.
The team needs to slow down intuition and make the reasoning path explicit enough to inspect for gaps.
A plan depends on ordered intermediate checks rather than a one-shot conclusion.
The current failure mode is jumping from prompt to answer without disciplined intermediate reasoning.

failure_modes.mode: decomposition of a complex problem into a sequence of smaller steps
failure_modes.description: Jumping to a conclusion without the intermediate reasoning steps hides where the error actually entered.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require the intermediate reasoning steps to be visible so that the point of error can be identified and corrected.
failure_modes.source_quote: decomposition of a complex problem into a sequence of smaller steps

failure_modes.mode: loss of big picture / analysis paralysis
failure_modes.description: A reasoning chain can become so long and self-referential that it loses the big picture and stalls action.
failure_modes.mitigation: Impose a length discipline: if the reasoning chain grows self-referential, step back and restate the core question the chain was supposed to answer.
failure_modes.source_quote: loss of big picture / analysis paralysis

failure_modes.mode: Implementation Gap
failure_modes.description: The implementation gap appears when the chain explains what to think but not how to act on it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Close the implementation gap by translating each conclusion step into a concrete action; a chain that explains what to think but not how to act is incomplete.
failure_modes.source_quote: Implementation Gap

premortem_questions.description: Which step in this reasoning chain, if wrong, would collapse the conclusion even if the rest sounded plausible?
premortem_questions.source_quote: sequence of smaller, logically connected steps

premortem_questions.description: What is the one thing the audience should remember if we acted on this chain today?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If my audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?

premortem_questions.description: How does the reasoning we are doing right now solve the problem, and is it the most important thing to do next?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what I'm doing solve the problem?

heuristics.description: Decompose the hard problem into linked sub-steps before asking for the final answer.
heuristics.source_quote: structured sequence

heuristics.description: Use a one-day answer to keep the chain connected to the larger conclusion.
heuristics.source_quote: The One-Day Answer

heuristics.description: Write the action plan down and read it aloud so the reasoning path has an implementation check.
heuristics.source_quote: Plan for Action, Not Just Analysis

danger_when: The chain is becoming verbose theater that does not improve the answer, evidence bar, or next action.
The problem is simple enough that forcing a long reasoning chain would mostly create delay and noise.
chain-of-thought.name: Chain Of Thought

select_when: A case depends on several premises in sequence and one weak link could collapse the whole conclusion.
Stakeholders need an auditable trail that shows how each claim was established rather than relying on expert confidence alone.
The team should scale commitment only after checking whether foundational assumptions, dependencies, and thresholds actually hold.
The current reasoning is too compressed, so the next useful move is to verify the make-or-break links one step at a time.

failure_modes.mode: Flawed Foundation
failure_modes.description: A flawed foundation can survive upward because teams fail to challenge weak assumptions at the earliest link.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge assumptions at the earliest link: a flawed foundation will survive all later checks if the first step is treated as given.
failure_modes.source_quote: Flawed Foundation

failure_modes.mode: Verification can turn into theater when extra checks and sign-offs create the appearance of rigor wi...
failure_modes.description: Verification can turn into theater when extra checks and sign-offs create the appearance of rigor without improving truth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Tie each verification step to a decision it should change; if no check would alter the plan, the verification is theater.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Verification Theater Replaces Judgment: The model is misapplied when teams keep adding checks, documents, or sign-offs that create an appearance of rigor without improving the actual truth of the chain.

failure_modes.mode: Be prepared to walk away from a solution and restart the process if refinement fails.
failure_modes.description: System rigidity can appear when a sequential proof process is treated as mechanically fixed even though the work needs iteration or reset.
failure_modes.mitigation: Allow iteration within the sequential proof process: treat verification as a living structure that adapts when evidence demands revisiting earlier steps.
failure_modes.source_quote: Be prepared to walk away from a solution and restart the process if refinement fails.

premortem_questions.description: Which links are make-or-break enough that the whole conclusion collapses if they fail?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Priority is given to critical, "make or break" hypotheses that, if proven wrong, would collapse the entire hypothesis pyramid.

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask “What would you have to believe?” to reveal the necessary assumptions in a scenario.

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: For projects, ask: “Is the worst case bad enough?”.

heuristics.description: Break the main claim into smaller conditions that can be validated bottom-up before recommitting to the top-line answer.
heuristics.source_quote: The verification happens bottom-up: analyses, facts, and data are gathered to prove or disprove the elementary hypotheses, and only once these are validated can the problem solver "climb back up the pyramid" to confirm the leading hypothesis.

heuristics.description: Gather just enough fact-based evidence to prove or disprove the next link instead of boiling the ocean.
heuristics.source_quote: The principle mandates gathering just enough facts to prove or disprove a hypothesis.

heuristics.description: Keep the logic and data inspectable so others can audit the chain instead of trusting a conclusion by authority.
heuristics.source_quote: allowing a system to be easily examined for bias

danger_when: Verification is becoming theater through extra checks and sign-offs that do not materially improve the truth of the chain.
The situation needs flexible social adaptation more than a rigid sequential proof process, so over-formalization would crowd out judgment.
chain-of-verification.name: Chain Of Verification

select_when: The environment is unstable enough that simple linear prediction is giving false confidence.
Minor disturbances, feedback, and path dependence make precise long-range forecasting unreliable.
Decision quality depends more on robustness and adaptive monitoring than on exact prediction.
The system appears orderly at a distance but is highly sensitive underneath.

failure_modes.mode: The literal term is most closely associated with sensitive dependence on initial conditions.
failure_modes.description: Linear forecasting creates false confidence when the system is actually sensitive, nonlinear, and hard to predict over long horizons.
failure_modes.mitigation: Replace linear forecasts with scenario ranges; in sensitive nonlinear systems, robustness and adaptive monitoring matter more than point predictions.
failure_modes.source_quote: The literal term is most closely associated with sensitive dependence on initial conditions.

failure_modes.mode: Complex outcomes cannot be fully predicted from their component parts regardless of analytical effort.
failure_modes.description: Teams waste effort chasing exact prediction in environments where robustness and adaptive monitoring matter more.
failure_modes.mitigation: Shift effort from prediction to resilience: design for adaptation, bounded exposure, and fast monitoring rather than chasing exact forecasts.
failure_modes.source_quote: Complex outcomes cannot be fully predicted from their component parts regardless of analytical effort.

failure_modes.mode: The model is misapplied when it becomes a reason to avoid structured analysis or action.
failure_modes.description: Chaos language becomes fatalism when people use it to imply that no understanding or intervention is possible.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require a concrete intervention or monitoring action from any chaos-theory framing; fatalism that implies no understanding is possible is not analysis.
failure_modes.source_quote: The model is misapplied when it becomes a reason to avoid structured analysis or action.

premortem_questions.description: Where are we confusing forecast precision with actual control over a nonlinear environment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Sensitive dependence on initial conditions.

premortem_questions.description: Which signals or thresholds should we monitor continuously because long-range certainty is unrealistic here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The purpose is to recognize the necessity of anticipating second- and third-order consequences in complex systems.

premortem_questions.description: What simple robustness move still helps even if exact prediction fails?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The true utility ... lies in highlighting profound interrelationships within complex systems.

heuristics.description: Prefer robustness, monitoring, and reversibility to precise long-range forecasts in chaotic conditions.
heuristics.source_quote: The model highlights sensitive dependence on initial conditions.

heuristics.description: Treat exact prediction claims with suspicion when the system is highly nonlinear and path dependent.
heuristics.source_quote: Irreducibility ... complex outcomes cannot be fully predicted from their component parts.

heuristics.description: Do not let chaos become an excuse for giving up on diagnosis or intervention quality.
heuristics.source_quote: The model is dangerous when it turns complexity into a reason for inaction.

danger_when: Teams are using chaos rhetoric to imply nothing can be understood or improved.
A bounded mechanical process with stable variables is being treated as chaotic when simpler diagnosis would suffice.
chaos-theory.name: Chaos Theory

select_when: The downside of being wrong is large enough that false confidence matters more than missing an adjacent opportunity.
A team must decide whether its intuition is actually earned by repeated exposure, fast feedback, and real error correction in this domain.
Scarce attention needs a perimeter, so saying no to adjacent opportunities is more important than sounding broadly capable.
The decision depends on whether judgment is genuinely calibrated here rather than on whether the team can tell a persuasive story.

failure_modes.mode: The Illusion of Understanding
failure_modes.description: False confidence outside the perimeter can hide unknown unknowns until the decision is already exposed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map the boundary explicitly and require external validation before committing to decisions outside it; unknown unknowns live just past the perimeter.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Illusion of Understanding

failure_modes.mode: Treating the circle as a permanent identity label can turn expertise into stagnation instead of a bo...
failure_modes.description: Treating the circle as a permanent identity label can turn expertise into stagnation instead of a boundary that updates with evidence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Treat the circle as a living boundary that updates with evidence and practice, not a permanent identity label.
failure_modes.source_quote: "stay in your circle" becomes an excuse to avoid adjacent learning, partnerships, or evidence that the current edge is no longer real.

failure_modes.mode: Maslow’s Hammer (The Law of the Instrument)
failure_modes.description: Maslow's hammer can make practitioners force familiar tools onto problems that sit outside their real competence.
failure_modes.mitigation: When the problem feels familiar but sits outside your real competence, bring in a practitioner from the relevant domain instead of stretching the familiar tool.
failure_modes.source_quote: Maslow’s Hammer (The Law of the Instrument)

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What would you have to believe?"

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Is the worst case bad enough?"

premortem_questions.description: What variables matter in this situation and why?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What variables matter in this situation and why?"

heuristics.description: Define the real perimeter by identifying what you understand and what you do not understand.
heuristics.source_quote: You have to know what you understand and what you don’t understand.

heuristics.description: Break oversized scopes into bite-size chunks that can actually be solved within current capability and resources.
heuristics.source_quote: the first action is to break it down into "bite-size chunks" that can realistically be solved within the allotted time and resources

heuristics.description: Use plain-English explanation as a perimeter test: if you cannot explain the concept simply, the circle is thinner than you think.
heuristics.source_quote: The Feynman Technique

danger_when: Circle of competence is being treated as a permanent identity label instead of a boundary that can expand, decay, or shift with evidence.
Staying inside the circle is being used as an excuse to avoid adjacent learning, partnerships, or evidence that the current edge is no longer real.
circle-of-competence.name: Circle Of Competence

select_when: External volatility is dominating attention, but the team still controls sequencing, communication, staffing, guardrails, or scope.
The next step depends on separating what is directly owned, what can be influenced, and what must be monitored rather than solved immediately.
Execution speed matters more than narrative completeness, so the work needs action buckets instead of one undifferentiated worry cloud.
The plan is stuck because people are mixing controllable levers with uncontrollable conditions.

failure_modes.mode: Failure Mode: False helplessness
failure_modes.description: False helplessness gives away agency by treating influenceable problems as if they are wholly external.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the classification: ask what specific action the team could take to influence the outcome; if an action exists, the item is not purely external.
failure_modes.source_quote: Failure Mode: False helplessness

failure_modes.mode: Failure Mode: Control illusion
failure_modes.description: Control illusion mistakes activity for leverage and assumes the team can directly move a system mostly constrained by external actors or feedback loops.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish influence from direct control: focus effort on the highest-leverage moves available rather than assuming the team can force outcomes in constrained systems.
failure_modes.source_quote: Failure Mode: Control illusion

failure_modes.mode: Failure Mode: Static categorization
failure_modes.description: Static categorization locks items into one bucket and never revisits the classification after new data or changed incentives.
failure_modes.mitigation: Revisit the categorization when new data arrives or incentives change; static buckets decay as the environment shifts.
failure_modes.source_quote: Failure Mode: Static categorization

premortem_questions.description: Which item did we mark uncontrollable without evidence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Which item did we mark uncontrollable without evidence?"

premortem_questions.description: Where are we assuming control we do not actually have?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Where are we assuming control we do not actually have?"

premortem_questions.description: What one action would increase our influence on the highest-impact external factor?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What one action would increase our influence on the highest-impact external factor?"

heuristics.description: Split the situation into direct control, indirect influence, and no-control buckets before generating actions.
heuristics.source_quote: split them into three buckets: direct control, indirect influence, and no control

heuristics.description: Create actions only for the control and influence buckets and monitoring rules for the rest.
heuristics.source_quote: Create actions only for the first two buckets and monitoring rules for the third.

heuristics.description: Reclassify the buckets when new information or incentives change what is influenceable.
heuristics.source_quote: what was uncontrollable yesterday may become influenceable after new information or changed incentives

danger_when: The model is being used to shrink responsibility by classifying difficult stakeholder, system, or incentive problems as outside control before influence has been tested.
Local control is being optimized so narrowly that broader feedback loops or slow indirect pathways are being ignored.
circle-of-control.name: Circle Of Control

select_when: The team is making consequential judgments under pressure, ambiguity, or strong prior beliefs that can quietly distort perception.
Decisions need explicit debiasing moves such as outside views, disconfirming evidence, or structured challenge instead of trust in intuition alone.
Unexamined confidence is more dangerous than adding a little extra friction to the reasoning process.

failure_modes.mode: System 1 can quietly take over and build a coherent story from limited evidence, leading the team to...
failure_modes.description: System 1 can quietly take over and build a coherent story from limited evidence, leading the team to mistake fluency for truth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the evidence behind any conclusion that feels fluent and obvious; System 1 confidence tracks story quality, not evidence completeness.
failure_modes.source_quote: System 1 is generally useful and appropriate for navigating daily life most of the time, allowing us to be guided by impressions and feelings.

failure_modes.mode: if the only tool you have is a hammer, to treat everything as if it were a nail
failure_modes.description: Teams can treat familiar frameworks as if they are always the right tool, substituting a handy model for the real structure of the problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test multiple frameworks against the same problem; if only one was considered, substitution bias may have smuggled in the wrong model.
failure_modes.source_quote: if the only tool you have is a hammer, to treat everything as if it were a nail

failure_modes.mode: high cognitive ability does not attenuate the bias blind spot
failure_modes.description: Smart people can become better at narrative defense than at truth-seeking, creating an intelligence trap instead of protection from bias.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require explicit reasoning chains from smart contributors; intelligence amplifies rationalization as much as it amplifies truth-seeking.
failure_modes.source_quote: high cognitive ability does not attenuate the bias blind spot

premortem_questions.description: What sources of information have we missed or minimized because they do not fit the story we already prefer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What sources of information could I have missed or minimized on the way to reaching my belief?

premortem_questions.description: If an outside view replaced our inside view here, what base rates or denominator checks would it force us to confront?
premortem_questions.source_quote: To fight biases rooted in the inside view decisions must be anchored to the outside view

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe for the opposite thesis to be true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.description: Force an outside-view check before committing to a plan that feels intuitively compelling.
heuristics.source_quote: decisions must be anchored to the outside view

heuristics.description: Use thesis, antithesis, and synthesis to make disconfirming evidence part of the process rather than a late objection.
heuristics.source_quote: use team norms like the Dialectic Standard (thesis, antithesis, synthesis)

heuristics.description: Document what was known at decision time so hindsight does not rewrite the learning afterward.
heuristics.source_quote: Use the Knowledge Tracker to separate what was known at the time of the decision from what was subsequently learned.

danger_when: Bias language is being used as a rhetorical weapon against opponents while the speaker avoids inspecting their own incentives, evidence, and assumptions.
The group wants a generic warning label instead of a concrete debiasing move tied to the actual decision structure.
cognitive-biases.name: Cognitive Biases

select_when: A public, ego-identified commitment has been made and subsequent evidence challenges it — the risk is motivated belief revision rather than honest updating.
A team is deep into a failing initiative and contrary evidence is being reframed, minimized, or explained away rather than genuinely weighted.
A decision-maker's stated reasoning is suspiciously well-aligned with the conclusion that protects their prior commitment.
Evidence avoidance is occurring — feedback mechanisms, review channels, or external assessments are being deprioritized without explicit justification.
Post-hoc rationalization is being offered as the original reasoning — the justification was constructed after the commitment, not before.

failure_modes.mode: Escalation of commitment
failure_modes.description: Escalation of commitment generates cumulative lock-in through a chain of small rationalizations — each dissonance-resolution step makes the next sunk-cost investment easier to justify and the next contrary signal easier to dismiss.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build in explicit reversal triggers and stop-loss thresholds at the start so the team has pre-committed exit criteria before rationalizations accumulate.
failure_modes.source_quote: Escalation of commitment

failure_modes.mode: Evidence avoidance
failure_modes.description: Evidence avoidance deprioritizes the feedback mechanisms, review meetings, and external assessments that would generate the dissonance-intensifying contrary signals.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect feedback mechanisms: schedule external reviews and evidence checkpoints that generate the dissonance signal instead of letting the team avoid it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Evidence avoidance

failure_modes.mode: Group dissonance amplification
failure_modes.description: Group dissonance amplification creates social pressure that recruits each team member's personal dissonance reduction toward the team's committed position, silencing genuine dissent.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate individual from group dissonance by collecting independent assessments before group discussion so social pressure cannot recruit personal rationalization.
failure_modes.source_quote: Group dissonance amplification

premortem_questions.description: If this same project were brought fresh today with the data we now have, would we approve it — or are we continuing because stopping would feel like admitting an error?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this same decision process had produced a good outcome, would we evaluate it differently?

premortem_questions.description: What signals are we currently monitoring that would indicate the plan is failing — and are those signals actually being reported and weighted?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Create structural information channels that cannot be blocked by motivated avoidance

premortem_questions.description: Are we staying this course because the evidence supports it, or because changing course would constitute a public admission of error?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Decouple decision quality from self-concept

heuristics.description: Establish explicit kill criteria before committing resources: pre-define the conditions that would trigger termination, so that the exit decision is made by the earlier, less-dissonant self.
heuristics.source_quote: Apply explicit kill criteria before committing resources

heuristics.description: Separate the proposer from the evaluator: the person who championed the commitment should not be the sole evaluator of contrary evidence — dissonance will ensure the evaluation is motivated.
heuristics.source_quote: Protect devil's advocates from social consequences

heuristics.description: Celebrate public updating: when leaders visibly change their position based on evidence, they demonstrate that intellectual updating is rewarded rather than penalized — directly countering the identity-threat that powers dissonance.
heuristics.source_quote: Reward evidence of good judgment, not fidelity to prior positions

danger_when: Genuine honest reconsideration has occurred and the position update is evidence-driven rather than dissonance-motivated — the model should not pathologize real belief updating.
The model is being used to dismiss any position-maintenance as rationalization without examining whether the evidence genuinely warrants the maintained position.
cognitive-dissonance.name: Cognitive Dissonance
cognitive-dissonance.output_type: motivated belief revision detection and escalation warning

select_when: Confidence is high but the explanation still feels under-specified and the team may be missing something important.
Capability, evidence, or understanding must be audited explicitly before commitment.
The problem may reflect false certainty, missing perspective, or unrecognized blind spots rather than missing effort.
Prevention matters more than cleaning up hidden assumptions after damage appears.

failure_modes.mode: bias blind spot
failure_modes.description: The bias blind spot can make sophisticated people less willing to see their own missing knowledge.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use the Feynman technique: explain the system in plain language and mark every point where the explanation fails as a genuine gap, not just a label.
failure_modes.source_quote: bias blind spot

failure_modes.mode: illusion of explanatory depth
failure_modes.description: Illusions of explanatory depth make people think they understand a system more deeply than they actually do.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test for illusion of explanatory depth by asking the team to walk through the mechanism step by step; overconfidence collapses when specificity is required.
failure_modes.source_quote: illusion of explanatory depth

failure_modes.mode: gap-mapping becomes a performance ritual
failure_modes.description: Gap-mapping can become ritualized labeling that never changes the plan, evidence bar, or exposure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Tie each identified gap to a concrete change in the plan, evidence bar, or exposure; if no decision changes, the gap-mapping is ritual.
failure_modes.source_quote: gap-mapping becomes a performance ritual

premortem_questions.description: What would need to be true for this idea to work, and which assumption in that chain is least grounded?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would need to be true for this idea to work?

premortem_questions.description: Which capability narrative are we accepting because of rank or authority rather than objective evidence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: rank substitutes for objective competency evidence

premortem_questions.description: What unknown unknown is most likely to embarrass us because we have not tried to surface it yet?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Unknown Unknowns

heuristics.description: Use the Feynman technique to force plain-language explanation and expose where understanding collapses.
heuristics.source_quote: The Feynman Technique

heuristics.description: Use self-quizzing and calibration to separate fluency from actual mastery.
heuristics.source_quote: Metacognitive Self-Quizzing & Calibration

heuristics.description: Convert risks from unknown to known states with an explicit knowns-and-unknowns matrix.
heuristics.source_quote: Knowns & Unknowns Matrix

danger_when: Gap mapping has become a performance ritual that labels uncertainty without changing the plan or evidence bar.
The team is endlessly diagnosing knowledge gaps instead of moving to the next discriminating test.
cognitive-gaps-assessment.name: Cognitive Gaps Assessment

select_when: Novices must absorb several unfamiliar concepts at once and would otherwise confuse the task because too many moving parts arrive together.
A presentation, workflow, lesson, or prompt can be sequenced so attention stays on the next useful chunk instead of the entire problem space.
Stress, ambiguity, or time pressure is already consuming working-memory capacity and decision quality depends on reducing avoidable mental load.
The communication or operating path should be redesigned so the most important distinctions stick instead of being buried in excess detail.

failure_modes.mode: Risk: The Illusion of Knowing (Overconfidence Bias)
failure_modes.description: Fluency can be mistaken for mastery when a workflow feels easy but the operator has not really learned the underlying distinction.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test mastery under novel conditions: fluency in the routine workflow does not prove the operator understands the underlying distinctions.
failure_modes.source_quote: Risk: The Illusion of Knowing (Overconfidence Bias)

failure_modes.mode: Risk: False Pattern Recognition (Availability Heuristic/Substitution Bias)
failure_modes.description: False pattern recognition can push a novel problem into a familiar frame simply because the familiar frame is cognitively cheaper.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge the first frame with a second one from a different discipline; if the novel problem was shoved into a familiar bucket, cognitive cheapness won.
failure_modes.source_quote: Risk: False Pattern Recognition (Availability Heuristic/Substitution Bias)

failure_modes.mode: Risk: Under-structuring Complex Problems (Decision Fatigue)
failure_modes.description: Stress and complexity can sap working memory and leave the team under-structuring the problem until bad decisions feel like the only available move.
failure_modes.mitigation: Reduce extraneous load by chunking and scaffolding so working memory is freed for the genuine complexity the problem requires.
failure_modes.source_quote: Risk: Under-structuring Complex Problems (Decision Fatigue)

premortem_questions.description: Which step in this simplified workflow feels clearer only because it hides the difference between two incident classes?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which step in this simplified workflow feels clearer only because it hides the difference between two incident classes, and what wrong containment, escalation, or ownership move would that false clarity trigger first?

premortem_questions.description: Which distinction did we erase in the name of simplification, and what wrong branch or escalation move becomes more likely once that cue is gone?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which distinction did we erase in the name of simplification, and what wrong branch, owner, or escalation move becomes more likely once that cue is gone?

premortem_questions.description: Where does this reduced-load playbook remove the cue that tells responders to switch to a different owner, tool path, or escalation branch?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Where does this reduced-load playbook remove the cue that tells responders to switch to a different owner, tool path, or escalation branch, and which incident type would be misrouted because that distinction is no longer visible under pressure?

heuristics.description: Chunk and scaffold complex material so the learner carries one small load at a time instead of the whole pile at once.
heuristics.source_quote: Chunking and Scaffolding

heuristics.description: Use worked examples to teach the schema before forcing the learner into heavy means-ends problem solving.
heuristics.source_quote: Use of Worked Examples

heuristics.description: Force simplification with plain-English explanation and analogy so unnecessary load falls away.
heuristics.source_quote: The Feynman Technique (Simplification and Analogy)

danger_when: Simplification would remove the causal structure, nuance, or thresholds needed to make a real decision rather than merely creating a feeling of clarity.
Expert users are being forced through novice scaffolding that adds friction, redundancy, or boredom instead of improving performance.
cognitive-load-theory.name: Cognitive Load Theory

select_when: Several forces are interacting so the outcome is larger or stranger than any single driver would predict alone.
The team needs to explain lollapalooza upside or downside by tracing aligned incentives, pressures, and system interactions.
A single-cause story feels too weak because multiple factors are compounding each other.
A recommendation should identify which combination of forces matters, not just which isolated variable matters most.

failure_modes.mode: combinatorial effects that create lollapalooza consequences
failure_modes.description: Treating a lollapalooza outcome as if it had one cause hides the interacting forces that actually made it possible.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map the interacting forces explicitly: if the outcome required more than one factor, label each and trace how they combined rather than crediting a single cause.
failure_modes.source_quote: combinatorial effects that create lollapalooza consequences

failure_modes.mode: I have seen this one before, it is an X problem
failure_modes.description: False pattern recognition makes a novel interacting system look like a familiar one-cause problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge any one-cause explanation of a surprising outcome by asking which additional conditions were necessary for it to happen.
failure_modes.source_quote: I have seen this one before, it is an X problem

failure_modes.mode: motivated reasoning
failure_modes.description: Motivated reasoning can force the team to explain away interactions that threaten a preferred story.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the evidence for each interacting force before allowing a narrative synthesis; motivated reasoning hides behind the combined story.
failure_modes.source_quote: motivated reasoning

premortem_questions.description: Which forces are aligned here, and what happens if one of the supposedly minor drivers is removed from the combination?
premortem_questions.source_quote: multiple factors or forces, when aligned and interacting

premortem_questions.description: Are we seeing the whole interacting system, or only the most vivid contributor?
premortem_questions.source_quote: multiple factors shape almost every system

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions are we making when we claim these drivers will compound rather than cancel each other out?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.description: Map interacting pressures across disciplines before naming the dominant mechanism.
heuristics.source_quote: ignore jurisdictional boundaries and synthesize the best ideas

heuristics.description: Use symphony-style synthesis to see how multiple elements produce an outsized whole.
heuristics.source_quote: symphony

heuristics.description: Prefer cross-checking compound drivers with feedback and second-order consequence analysis before acting.
heuristics.source_quote: second-order effects

danger_when: The situation is being forced into a dramatic synergy story before the interacting mechanisms have been identified clearly.
One dominant driver already explains the outcome and adding more factors would mostly create narrative fog.
combinatorial-effects.name: Combinatorial Effects

select_when: Execution requires persistence across a long sequence of small decisions that would otherwise unravel under distraction or drift.
The commitment is tied to a reversible experiment, disciplined cadence, or identity-based habit that benefits from staying power.
The main risk is under-follow-through rather than over-escalation, and the team has explicit stop rules to revisit the commitment.
A person or group is defending a prior commitment, identity, or promise more strongly than the evidence now justifies.
Escalation, rigidity, or self-justification is blocking reversal even though the original assumptions have weakened.
The real issue is not lack of information but the social or psychological cost of admitting the earlier choice may have been wrong.
The team needs to inspect how small public commitments are shaping larger downstream behavior.

failure_modes.mode: Escalation after the decision has already lost its best branch
failure_modes.description: Escalation after the decision has already lost its best branch can keep effort flowing into a dead path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define explicit exit criteria and reversal triggers before commitment so the team has pre-agreed conditions for changing course.
failure_modes.source_quote: Escalation after the decision has already lost its best branch

failure_modes.mode: Entrenchment and Inertia
failure_modes.description: Entrenchment and inertia can make the team defend prior posture long after the evidence moved.
failure_modes.mitigation: Schedule regular re-evaluation checkpoints that force the team to justify continued investment from current evidence, not from prior momentum.
failure_modes.source_quote: Entrenchment and Inertia

failure_modes.mode: Self-Justification & Rationalization
failure_modes.description: Self-justification can turn the conscious self into a press secretary for earlier decisions instead of a seeker of truth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Ask what evidence would cause a reversal and whether anyone has looked for it; the press-secretary instinct defends decisions rather than seeking truth.
failure_modes.source_quote: Self-Justification & Rationalization

failure_modes.mode: Bias Blind Spot
failure_modes.description: Bias blind spot can keep people convinced they are less trapped by commitment than everyone else even while their story hardens.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use structured red-team exercises that explicitly challenge the prior commitment; awareness alone does not dissolve the bias.
failure_modes.source_quote: Bias Blind Spot

failure_modes.mode: Groupthink / Tribalism
failure_modes.description: Groupthink and tribal loyalty can make consistency with the group feel safer than updating on reality.
failure_modes.mitigation: Collect individual positions before group discussion to prevent tribal consistency pressure from overriding independent judgment.
failure_modes.source_quote: Groupthink / Tribalism

premortem_questions.description: What evidence would make us reverse course rather than defend the original commitment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If this path is failing quietly, what would we notice first?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: Are we protecting the goal, or just protecting the story that we were right to start?
premortem_questions.source_quote: commitment and consistency

premortem_questions.description: What would I do if I had no sunk costs invested in this outcome?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would I do if I had no sunk costs invested in this outcome?

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions underlie the framework I am using?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumptions underlie the framework I am using?

heuristics.description: Separate goal-setting, diagnosis, and design so commitment to one step does not pre-commit the next.
heuristics.source_quote: perform the steps one at a time and in order

heuristics.description: Use outside challenge to test whether the current path still deserves commitment.
heuristics.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.description: Treat small commitments as leverage carefully because they can ratchet identity and posture faster than evidence updates.
heuristics.source_quote: commitment and consistency

heuristics.description: Treat abandoning a bad idea as learning progress rather than as weakness so reversal becomes psychologically possible.
heuristics.source_quote: making mistakes is okay, but failing to learn from them is unacceptable

heuristics.description: Force an antithesis before the group synthesizes, so loyalty to prior commitment does not silently set the answer.
heuristics.source_quote: The Dialectic Standard

heuristics.description: Assume you are wrong long enough to search for the flaw in the commitment story before defending it.
heuristics.source_quote: Find Your Own Flaws

danger_when: Prior public commitment, sunk cost, or ego investment is becoming more important than updating on new evidence.
The case demands flexibility and fast updating, but the commitment structure would make reversal socially or politically expensive.
The model is being used to mock consistency itself instead of distinguishing disciplined persistence from self-deceptive lock-in.
commitment-bias.name: Commitment Bias

select_when: The real question is who should do what, not merely how hard everyone should work inside the current role map.
Small differences in skill, speed, or opportunity cost can compound into materially different outcomes across people or teams.
Responsibilities or partnerships can be redesigned around complementary strengths without losing coordination.
The current setup keeps work with the original owner even though another person or function has the higher-leverage fit.

failure_modes.mode: legacy role allocation blocks higher-leverage specialization
failure_modes.description: Legacy role allocation can keep work with the wrong owner even when marginal efficiency has shifted.
failure_modes.mitigation: Revisit role allocation when marginal efficiency shifts; legacy assignments persist through inertia even after the comparative advantage has moved.
failure_modes.source_quote: legacy role allocation blocks higher-leverage specialization

failure_modes.mode: Cognitive Entrenchment
failure_modes.description: Over-specialization can create inflexibility and blind teams to novelty outside their habitual frame.
failure_modes.mitigation: Guard against over-specialization by maintaining minimum competence in adjacent domains so the team can recognize novelty outside its habitual frame.
failure_modes.source_quote: Cognitive Entrenchment

failure_modes.mode: avoid building minimum competence in adjacent skills
failure_modes.description: Comparative advantage can be misused as an excuse to avoid building minimum adjacent competence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish comparative advantage from convenient avoidance: the model justifies focus, not an excuse to ignore gaps that create systemic fragility.
failure_modes.source_quote: avoid building minimum competence in adjacent skills

premortem_questions.description: Who should own this work if we reallocated by marginal efficiency instead of by tradition?
premortem_questions.source_quote: who should do what

premortem_questions.description: What alternative perspective outside my specialty would expose a better role split?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What alternative perspectives, outside of my area of expertise

premortem_questions.description: Which non-core skills still need baseline competence even if they are not our comparative edge?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Permanent Weakness

heuristics.description: Map the zone of genius and complementary strengths before assigning ownership.
heuristics.source_quote: Zone of Genius

heuristics.description: Use the Pareto principle to focus effort on the work with the highest return on talent.
heuristics.source_quote: Pareto Principle

heuristics.description: Design roles so the organization becomes more than the sum of specialized parts.
heuristics.source_quote: more than the sum of its parts

danger_when: Specialization logic is being used to excuse permanent weakness in adjacent skills that still require minimum competence.
The environment has changed enough that old role allocations no longer match the real work, but the model is being used to defend the legacy map.
comparative-advantage.name: Comparative Advantage

select_when: Institutional design, incentives, and power structure matter more than the stated ideology or surface narrative.
Governance systems or policy responses must be compared without assuming one familiar national frame fits all cases.
Strategic interpretation depends on seeing how formal rules and informal behavior interact across different system designs.
The analyst needs structural comparison across systems rather than superficial analogy from one visible label.

failure_modes.mode: comparison collapses into superficial analogy
failure_modes.description: Surface analogy can make unlike systems look equivalent just because they share one visible institution or label.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge surface analogies by mapping the underlying institutional mechanisms; shared labels can mask fundamentally different causal structures.
failure_modes.source_quote: comparison collapses into superficial analogy

failure_modes.mode: interpret cross-system evidence in ways that confirm their preferred political framework
failure_modes.description: Inside-view or motivated reasoning can make analysts interpret cross-system evidence through a preferred domestic frame.
failure_modes.mitigation: Discipline inside-view bias by requiring at least one alternative interpretation grounded in the other system's own logic, not just the domestic frame.
failure_modes.source_quote: interpret cross-system evidence in ways that confirm their preferred political framework

failure_modes.mode: connecting everything with everything
failure_modes.description: System maps can become so sprawling that everything connects to everything and no actionable comparison remains.
failure_modes.mitigation: Limit the system map to actionable comparison dimensions; if everything connects to everything, the analysis has lost its diagnostic leverage.
failure_modes.source_quote: connecting everything with everything

premortem_questions.description: What structural difference between these systems would most likely break the analogy we are currently relying on?
premortem_questions.source_quote: formal rules and informal behavior interact across different system designs

premortem_questions.description: Which make-or-break institutional assumption are we importing from one regime that should be tested instead?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If the outside view on comparable systems is sparse, are we mistaking uncertainty for stable probability?
premortem_questions.source_quote: sparse comparables are treated as stable probabilities

heuristics.description: Compare systems through institutional structure, incentives, and power interactions instead of surface narrative.
heuristics.source_quote: see interrelationships rather than things

heuristics.description: Use system mapping and causal analysis to separate machine-level patterns from case-level events.
heuristics.source_quote: machine level

heuristics.description: Run explicit comparative hypotheses so cross-system evidence can falsify a preferred interpretation.
heuristics.source_quote: disaggregation and hypotheses

danger_when: Comparison is collapsing into shallow analogy that treats unlike systems as equivalent because they share one visible institution.
The system map has grown so large that everything is connected to everything and no actionable comparison remains.
comparative-political-systems-analysis.name: Comparative Political Systems Analysis

select_when: Many agents are adapting at once, so the environment behaves more like a living system than a fixed machine.
Feedback, delays, and incentives keep reshaping the situation, including the effects of the intervention itself.
Improving the whole depends on understanding where a small change can alter learning loops, coordination, or adaptation.
The team keeps describing parts correctly but still misses the behavior emerging from their interaction.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When “The System Is Complex” Becomes an Alibi.
failure_modes.description: Complexity can become an alibi when the team talks about the system without naming concrete leverage points, experiments, or ownership.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require every complexity framing to name concrete leverage points, experiments, or owners; complexity without action items is an alibi.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When “The System Is Complex” Becomes an Alibi.

failure_modes.mode: The Bureaucratic Mindset: Applying mechanical or linear beliefs to social systems is counterproductive.
failure_modes.description: Mechanical or bureaucratic thinking can misdesign social systems by treating them like static machines with fixed parts.
failure_modes.mitigation: Design social systems with feedback loops, adaptation mechanisms, and agency in mind rather than treating them as static machines with fixed parts.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Bureaucratic Mindset: Applying mechanical or linear beliefs to social systems is counterproductive.

failure_modes.mode: The Overwhelming Nature of Complexity
failure_modes.description: Adaptive-system analysis can drown in overload when too many variables are tracked without deciding which loop or boundary matters most.
failure_modes.mitigation: Limit the scope of adaptive-system analysis to the loops and boundaries that drive the decision; tracking everything drowns the signal.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Overwhelming Nature of Complexity

premortem_questions.description: What would have to be true for this system view to explain the behavior we are seeing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: Which feedback loop, delay, or incentive are we leaving out that could change how this intervention behaves after we act?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when feedback, delays, and incentives keep reshaping the environment.

premortem_questions.description: Are we telling the story of the search, or can we state the core system loop clearly enough for someone else to act on it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Am I telling the story of the search (the complex, winding analysis) or the story of the solution (the clear, simple outcome)?

heuristics.description: Use a five-step adaptive loop that separates goals, problems, diagnosis, design, and doing so the system can learn instead of thrash.
heuristics.source_quote: The 5-Step Iterative Process

heuristics.description: Map the system visually so interactions, boundaries, and causal loops become explicit instead of remaining implicit assumptions.
heuristics.source_quote: Systems Mapping and Visualization

heuristics.description: Expose the assumptions behind the current system view before acting so the team knows which beliefs are carrying the intervention.
heuristics.source_quote: A key heuristic is to use the “What would you have to believe?” question to spell out all the implicit assumptions in a perspective.

danger_when: Complexity language is being used as an alibi for not naming concrete leverage points, experiments, or ownership.
The model is being used to admire the system's complexity instead of deciding what variable, boundary, or loop actually matters next.
complex-adaptive-systems.name: Complex Adaptive Systems

select_when: A team is drifting into needless complexity or shallow oversimplification instead of holding to the essential core of the problem.
People are boiling the ocean because they have not prioritized what actually matters.
Decision quality depends on disciplined simplicity that preserves causal structure without decorative complication.
Communication or planning is becoming dense enough to overload working memory and hide the real decision.

failure_modes.mode: It requires the practitioner to relentlessly prioritize and be a master of exclusion.
failure_modes.description: Teams waste time and cognitive energy when they keep adding detail without clarifying the essential core of the problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Cut detail until the essential core of the problem is clear; every additional layer must earn its place by changing the decision.
failure_modes.source_quote: It requires the practitioner to relentlessly prioritize and be a master of exclusion.

failure_modes.mode: The human brain has limits on its working memory.
failure_modes.description: Communication can fail when simplification removes the causal structure people need, or when unnecessary intricacy overloads working memory.
failure_modes.mitigation: Match simplification to the audience: ensure the causal structure people need to act is preserved while extraneous intricacy is removed.
failure_modes.source_quote: The human brain has limits on its working memory.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the model is used to adopt inaccurate, oversimplified models.
failure_modes.description: Complexity-bias-resistance becomes crude reduction when simplicity is used to erase nuance instead of to protect what matters.
failure_modes.mitigation: Guard against crude reduction: simplicity that erases nuance is as dangerous as complexity that buries it; the goal is clarity, not minimalism.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the model is used to adopt inaccurate, oversimplified models.

premortem_questions.description: What is the essential core of this problem, and what detail is decorative rather than decision-bearing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Focus resources only on the essential core of the problem.

premortem_questions.description: Which part of our explanation is too compressed to preserve the real causal structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The model works against unnecessary intricacy or superficial oversimplification.

premortem_questions.description: Where are we boiling the ocean because we have not defined what output the work is supposed to produce?
premortem_questions.source_quote: This prevents the wasteful effort of boiling the ocean.

heuristics.description: Simplify until the core is clear, but stop before the causal structure is destroyed.
heuristics.source_quote: The disciplined commitment to simplicity and clarity.

heuristics.description: Use ruthless prioritization to decide what belongs in the reasoning path and what should be excluded.
heuristics.source_quote: Be a master of exclusion.

heuristics.description: Treat needless complexity and lazy oversimplification as twin errors rather than as opposites where one side is always safe.
heuristics.source_quote: Actively working against the natural human inclination to either introduce needless complexity or adopt inaccurate, oversimplified models.

danger_when: The push for simplicity would erase decision-critical nuance, edge cases, or causal structure.
Complexity-bias-resistance is being misused as a slogan for crude reduction rather than disciplined prioritization.
complexity-bias-resistance.name: Complexity Bias Resistance

select_when: Small repeated gains can accumulate because progress is preserved rather than reset each cycle.
The main payoff unfolds across time, so short-run visibility understates long-run effect.
The decision must balance growing the upside with protecting the base that growth depends on.
Repeated improvements, trust, or knowledge can stack into later optionality if the engine stays intact.

failure_modes.mode: conjunctive character
failure_modes.description: Plans with conjunctive structure can produce unwarranted optimism because every step must go right.
failure_modes.mitigation: Break conjunctive plans into independent probability assessments for each step and multiply them; the compound probability is almost always lower than intuition suggests.
failure_modes.source_quote: conjunctive character

failure_modes.mode: ignore fragility in incentives, quality, retention, or operational resilience
failure_modes.description: Growth can fail to compound when hidden fragility in quality or incentives erodes the engine.
failure_modes.mitigation: Monitor the compounding engine for hidden quality or incentive decay that erodes the base before the next round of growth.
failure_modes.source_quote: ignore fragility in incentives, quality, retention, or operational resilience

failure_modes.mode: Don't boil the ocean
failure_modes.description: Boiling the ocean can waste effort instead of stacking the highest-leverage gains.
failure_modes.mitigation: Apply the Pareto principle: stack the highest-leverage gains first rather than boiling the ocean with equal effort across all fronts.
failure_modes.source_quote: Don't boil the ocean

premortem_questions.description: What base are we protecting so gains can keep stacking instead of resetting?
premortem_questions.source_quote: protecting downside is as important as amplifying upside

premortem_questions.description: What single failure could erase years of accumulated progress?
premortem_questions.source_quote: single component failure

premortem_questions.description: How does this work advance the problem instead of merely creating activity?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what you're doing solve the problem?

heuristics.description: Stack repeated improvements where gains persist into the next cycle.
heuristics.source_quote: results generated in one cycle become the base for generating results in the next cycle

heuristics.description: Use structured iterative loops so learning compounds rather than restarting from scratch.
heuristics.source_quote: setting goals, identifying problems, diagnosing root causes, designing solutions, and implementing them

heuristics.description: Focus time on highly leveraged activities that compound output over time.
heuristics.source_quote: Highly Leveraged Activities

danger_when: The team is celebrating a growth curve while ignoring fragility in quality, incentives, or retention that could erase the base.
The plan assumes persistence alone will compound even though rework or churn keeps resetting progress.
compounding.name: Compounding

select_when: A forecast, estimate, or recommendation is about to determine commitment size, but the confidence behind it has not been made explicit.
The team is speaking with precise-sounding certainty even though the evidence base, sample depth, or regime stability is still weak.
A branch model, dashboard, or spreadsheet looks decision-grade, but the real question is how much trust the underlying measurement has earned.
