The loudest or most experienced voice may be directionally right, but the cost of untested overconfidence is high enough to justify explicit boundary conditions.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when teams confuse quantified language with calibrated judgment.
failure_modes.description: Calibration can fail when quantified language looks rigorous even though the underlying evidence is still weak or unstable.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the evidence beneath any precise-sounding confidence claim; quantified language can look rigorous even when the data is weak or unstable.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams confuse quantified language with calibrated judgment.

failure_modes.mode: Overconfidence can hide behind dashboards or model outputs that appear precise before the data-gener...
failure_modes.description: Overconfidence can hide behind dashboards or model outputs that appear precise before the data-generating process is trustworthy.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate model precision from data trustworthiness: dashboards and outputs inherit the quality of their inputs, not the other way around.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when dashboards or model outputs are being treated as decision-grade before the data-generating process is trusted.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when calibration talk replaces commitment.
failure_modes.description: Calibration talk can become a substitute for commitment when teams keep widening the caveats instead of defining an action threshold.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set an action threshold: calibration talk that only widens caveats without defining a decision point is avoidance, not rigor.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when calibration talk replaces commitment.

premortem_questions.description: On a scale of 0 to 100%, how confident are you?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask for a numerical measure of confidence in the claim (e.g., "On a scale of 0 to 100%, how confident are you?").

premortem_questions.description: Would you be really shocked if the outcome fell outside this upper or lower bound?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Would you be really shocked if the outcome fell outside this upper or lower bound?

premortem_questions.description: Which confidence claim in this plan is acting like measurement even though it comes from a thin, filtered, or regime-shifted sample?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which confidence claim in this plan is acting like measurement even though it comes from a thin, filtered, or regime-shifted sample, and what evidence would widen the range enough to change the commitment?

heuristics.description: State boundary conditions and confidence ranges explicitly so the recommendation carries the uncertainty it has actually earned.
heuristics.source_quote: Consultants must avoid the foolishness of "pretending we can be certain of things that are difficult to predict". Instead of projecting absolute confidence, the calibrated strategist shares the boundary conditions under which their recommendations hold true.

heuristics.description: Interrogate the method used to justify confidence, not just the reasons that support the claim.
heuristics.source_quote: Ask what method they used to judge the quality of their reasons, then focus the conversation on assessing that methodology.

heuristics.description: Shrink commitment size to the confidence level actually earned rather than to the upside story being told.
heuristics.source_quote: The key discipline is sizing the move to the confidence level actually earned rather than to the upside story being told.

danger_when: The model is being used to add ranges and caveats without naming the action threshold that would still trigger a real decision.
Percentages or confidence intervals are being presented as if quantified language itself proved disciplined judgment.
confidence-calibration.name: Confidence Calibration

select_when: A team is converging on a favored answer before the evidence has been fully tested.
Disconfirming evidence keeps getting reframed, minimized, or pushed to the edge of the discussion.
The decision is emotionally, politically, or reputationally charged enough that people are likely to defend a conclusion instead of testing it.
A recommendation packet is built around visible wins while failed cases, churn, or rejected signals were not counted with equal weight.

failure_modes.mode: Bias Blind Spot Amplification
failure_modes.description: Bias blind spot amplification lets the team audit everyone else's evidence standards while sparing its own.
failure_modes.mitigation: Turn the diagnostic lens inward first: audit your own evidence standards with the same rigor you apply to others.
failure_modes.source_quote: Bias Blind Spot Amplification

failure_modes.mode: Premature Simplification
failure_modes.description: Premature simplification can harden an early story before contradictory evidence has been tested.
failure_modes.mitigation: Delay story-hardening until contradictory evidence has been actively sought and tested; premature simplification is the gateway to confirmation lock-in.
failure_modes.source_quote: Premature Simplification

failure_modes.mode: Flawed Hypothesis Testing
failure_modes.description: Flawed hypothesis testing turns the search into confirmation work instead of genuine falsification.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require genuine falsification: define what evidence would disprove the hypothesis and look for it before declaring the hypothesis confirmed.
failure_modes.source_quote: Flawed Hypothesis Testing

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe for this conclusion to be true, and which of those assumptions is weakest?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: What disconfirming evidence would change our mind if it were true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Find disconfirming evidence

premortem_questions.description: Which failed cases, churn cases, or null results are we currently excluding from the packet?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Search for information that would prove you wrong, not just information that confirms you are right.

heuristics.description: Use the Darwinian approach by trying to kill weak ideas before defending favored ones.
heuristics.source_quote: Darwinian Approach

heuristics.description: Ask what you would have to believe so implicit assumptions become inspectable instead of sacred.
heuristics.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.description: Adopt the scientific mindset by treating the claim as a hypothesis that must survive disconfirmation pressure.
heuristics.source_quote: Adopt the Scientific Mindset

danger_when: The review is already structured around explicit falsification, equal treatment of confirming and disconfirming evidence, and adversarial challenge.
The model is being used only to accuse other people of bias without tightening the team's own evidence standards, incentives, or sacred assumptions.
confirmation-bias.name: Confirmation Bias
reframing_when: The question is framed to seek validating evidence for a position already taken — the search for information is asymmetric, looking for reasons to confirm rather than reasons to doubt.
The question treats the absence of disconfirming evidence as confirmation, ignoring that the frame itself determines what evidence gets noticed — survivorship bias in the evidence search.
The question suppresses counterfactuals by treating the current trajectory as the natural one, making alternatives feel like deviations that need justification rather than live options.
The question weighs evidence asymmetrically — favorable information is taken at face value while unfavorable information is scrutinized, debunked, or dismissed as exceptional.
confirmation-bias.reframing_move: Before accepting the evidence base as given, ask: 'What evidence would I expect to see if this frame were wrong, and have I looked for it with the same energy I used to confirm it?' — the disconfirmation reframe forces symmetric evidence search.

select_when: Success depends on a chain of individually plausible steps that all must occur for the plan to work.
A proposal sounds convincing step by step, but the overall odds are being inferred from the plausibility of each component.
The team is evaluating a launch, integration, or transformation whose result depends on coordinated sequential execution.
The decision needs an outside-view check on how compound failure risk accumulates across a multi-stage plan.

failure_modes.mode: Unwarranted Optimism
failure_modes.description: Unwarranted optimism can make a multi-step plan look safer than the compounded odds justify.
failure_modes.mitigation: Decompose the multi-step plan into individual probabilities and compute the joint: the compounded odds are almost always lower than the narrative makes them feel.
failure_modes.source_quote: Unwarranted Optimism

failure_modes.mode: Anchoring Bias
failure_modes.description: Anchoring on one plausible step can hide how fragile the full sequence really is.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge anchoring on the most plausible single step by asking what must also go right for the full sequence to hold.
failure_modes.source_quote: Anchoring Bias

failure_modes.mode: Lazy Thinking (System 1 Reliance)
failure_modes.description: Lazy System 1 acceptance lets a smooth narrative substitute for explicit probability discipline.
failure_modes.mitigation: Force explicit probability discipline: require the team to assign and multiply probabilities rather than accepting a smooth narrative as evidence of likelihood.
failure_modes.source_quote: Lazy Thinking (System 1 Reliance)

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: What specific assumptions must all hold for this chain to succeed?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: Which step breaks first if one dependency slips, and what happens to the rest of the plan then?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what you're doing solve the problem? How does it advance your thinking?

heuristics.description: Use reference class forecasting before trusting the inside story of a multi-step success case.
heuristics.source_quote: Reference Class Forecasting

heuristics.description: Run worst-case scrutiny on the chain instead of only arguing from the happy path.
heuristics.source_quote: Worst-Case Scrutiny

heuristics.description: Decompose the plan into MECE steps so hidden dependencies and stacked assumptions become inspectable.
heuristics.source_quote: Decomposition and MECE

danger_when: The case is dominated by one or two obvious bottlenecks rather than a long conjunctive chain, so probability arithmetic would add false precision.
The structure is actually disjunctive, redundant, or fail-safe, where success does not require every step to go right.
conjunction-fallacy.name: Conjunction Fallacy

select_when: A team is losing clarity because too many goals, variables, or stakeholders are competing for attention at once.
Success depends on explicit boundaries around scope, acceptable trade-offs, and what the current effort will deliberately exclude.
Creative or analytical work needs a forcing function that narrows the space enough for quality decisions and movement.
The real problem is not lack of ideas but lack of boundaries strong enough to focus effort and reduce overload.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when old constraints remain unquestioned after the economics, technology, or stakeholder environment has changed.
failure_modes.description: Stale constraints can quietly mislead action when the environment changed but the boundary never did.
failure_modes.mitigation: Revisit constraints on a schedule: ask whether each boundary still reflects the current environment or has fossilized from a prior context.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when old constraints remain unquestioned after the economics, technology, or stakeholder environment has changed.

failure_modes.mode: Constraint design can fail by being so loose that it creates false confidence or so tight that it bl...
failure_modes.description: Constraint design can fail by being so loose that it creates false confidence or so tight that it blocks learning.
failure_modes.mitigation: Calibrate constraint tightness to the decision: too loose creates false confidence, too tight blocks learning; the constraint should match the exposure.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when constraints are written so loosely that they create false confidence in control, or so tightly that they choke off signal, adaptation, or learning.

failure_modes.mode: Over-engineering Constraints
failure_modes.description: Over-engineered boundaries can create useless accuracy and analysis paralysis instead of action.
failure_modes.mitigation: Prefer simple, actionable constraints over elaborate boundary architectures that produce analysis paralysis instead of enabling action.
failure_modes.source_quote: Over-engineering Constraints

premortem_questions.description: How does what you’re doing solve the problem?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what you’re doing solve the problem?

premortem_questions.description: Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?

premortem_questions.description: If this was cut, would anything be lost?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this was cut, would anything be lost?

heuristics.description: Define constraints explicitly in the problem statement so the scope is realistic before analysis starts.
heuristics.source_quote: Problem Statement Definition

heuristics.description: Make everything earn its place so only information serving the primary goal survives.
heuristics.source_quote: Make Everything Earn Its Place

heuristics.description: Chunk the work into bite-sized pieces so the boundary helps the mind focus instead of overload.
heuristics.source_quote: Cognitive Load Management (Chunking)

danger_when: Old constraints are remaining unquestioned after the economics, technology, or stakeholder environment has changed.
Constraints are being written so loosely that they create false confidence in control, or so tightly that they choke off signal, adaptation, or learning.
constraints.name: Constraints
reframing_when: The question treats a human-chosen boundary as an immutable wall when that constraint could be relaxed, removed, or redefined — the frame/lens is being mistaken for reality.
The question has no explicit constraints and is therefore too vague to produce actionable answers — adding the right boundary would sharpen the inquiry.
The question includes constraints that were inherited from a prior context but no longer apply — organizational or technical boundaries have shifted but the question hasn't.
The question asks 'how do we do everything?' when the real question is 'what should we cut?' — the absence of a don't-do constraint leads to boiling the ocean.
constraints.reframing_move: Test the walls: ask 'which of these constraints are real (physics, law, budget) and which are assumed (organizational habit, precedent, fear)?' — then reframe the question with only the real constraints, opening the space that the assumed ones were closing.

select_when: Performance, learning, or trust depends on giving people information they can act on rather than vague reassurance.
The goal is to improve future behavior while preserving enough relationship quality that the feedback will actually be heard.
A team needs correction that is specific, evidence-backed, and tied to standards rather than personal attack.
An issue will keep repeating unless the discussion moves from the case-at-hand to the underlying machine or process.

failure_modes.mode: Defensive/Adversarial Response
failure_modes.description: Defensiveness can overwhelm the learning if the feedback lands as adversarial threat instead of as respectful correction.
failure_modes.mitigation: Frame feedback as a shared problem to solve rather than an adversarial judgment; respectful framing reduces defensiveness and protects the learning signal.
failure_modes.source_quote: Defensive/Adversarial Response

failure_modes.mode: Machine vs. Case-at-Hand
failure_modes.description: Micromanagement can keep the conversation stuck in the case-at-hand instead of improving the process that produced it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Elevate feedback from the specific case to the process that produced it; the goal is systemic improvement, not micromanagement of one incident.
failure_modes.source_quote: Machine vs. Case-at-Hand

failure_modes.mode: Vague or Abstract Feedback
failure_modes.description: Vague feedback can protect comfort but fail to give the receiver anything concrete enough to change.
failure_modes.mitigation: Make feedback concrete and behavioral: vague encouragement or criticism gives the receiver nothing specific enough to change.
failure_modes.source_quote: Vague or Abstract Feedback

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If someone came to me asking my opinion about this kind of decision, what would I need to know to give good advice?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If someone came to me asking my opinion about this kind of decision, what would I need to know to give good advice?

premortem_questions.description: What process or machine condition produced this outcome, not just what happened in this one case?
premortem_questions.source_quote: machine level

heuristics.description: Use the 4 I's so feedback moves from concrete observation to impact, invitation, and implications instead of stalling at vague criticism.
heuristics.source_quote: The 4 I’s Framework

heuristics.description: Challenge directly while caring personally so the receiver gets both specificity and enough support to stay engaged.
heuristics.source_quote: Radical Candor

heuristics.description: Shift the discussion to the machine level when repetition suggests the system is producing the error, not just the person.
heuristics.source_quote: Machine vs. Case-at-Hand

danger_when: Feedback is technically accurate but so poorly timed, context-free, or disrespectful that defensiveness will overwhelm learning.
The moment mainly requires decision clarity, boundary enforcement, or role authority rather than a collaborative learning conversation.
constructive-feedback-models.name: Constructive Feedback Models

select_when: A visible metric shift, pattern, or association is being treated as proof of what is driving the outcome.
The team is about to act on a symptom because the presenting variable is measurable, even though the causal mechanism is still uncertain.
Interventions are expensive or hard to reverse, so acting on the wrong driver would waste time, budget, or trust.
Diagnosis needs to separate proximate causes from root causes instead of confusing the latest event with the true generative mechanism.

failure_modes.mode: The most common anti-pattern is mistaking correlation for causation.
failure_modes.description: Teams can act on a visible association while missing the underlying causal mechanism that actually needs to change.
failure_modes.mitigation: Trace the causal mechanism: ask what specific process connects the correlated variables and whether intervening on one will actually change the other.
failure_modes.source_quote: The most common anti-pattern is mistaking correlation for causation.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when experimentation is impossible but certainty is still claimed.
failure_modes.description: Causal claims can become overconfident when the evidence is observational, confounded, or incomplete.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the causal claim against confounders: identify at least one plausible alternative explanation and check whether the evidence discriminates between them.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when experimentation is impossible but certainty is still claimed.

failure_modes.mode: Root-cause work can fail when the team stops at the proximate event instead of tracing the machine t...
failure_modes.description: Root-cause work can fail when the team stops at the proximate event instead of tracing the machine that produced it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Push past the proximate event: use fishbone or five-whys to trace the system that produced the correlation rather than stopping at the visible association.
failure_modes.source_quote: Focusing solely on the case-at-hand (the correlation or outcome) without diagnosing the machine (the causal structure) leads to micromanagement and prevents true learning.

premortem_questions.description: What mechanism would have to exist for this factor to cause the outcome we claim?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Before one can logically conclude that causation is present, one must explicitly formulate hypotheses about the mechanisms by which one factor causes the other.

premortem_questions.description: Which confounding variables have we identified and measured instead of leaving them to chance?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Analysts must actively identify and measure all potential confounding variables in addition to those specified in the hypotheses to control for spurious correlations.

premortem_questions.description: Are we solving the root cause, or only the symptom that happened immediately before the failure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: This involves striving to separate the proximate cause (what happened immediately before) from the root cause (the true underlying driver).

heuristics.description: Use a fishbone diagram to map multiple candidate causes before committing to a single explanation.
heuristics.source_quote: The Fishbone (or Ishikawa) Diagram is a practical tool used to determine causation more effectively.

heuristics.description: Apply the five whys to move past surface symptoms and keep drilling toward the original driver.
heuristics.source_quote: The "Five Whys" Technique

heuristics.description: Choose analytical tools based on whether the job is to understand causal drivers or merely to predict a future state.
heuristics.source_quote: The most important defining question at the outset is whether you are primarily trying to understand the drivers of causation ... or if you are primarily trying to predict a future state.

danger_when: The evidence base is observational, confounded, or incomplete, but the recommendation is still being framed as if causality were already settled.
The work only needs a quick associative signal for exploration, but the team is pretending that full causal proof is available before learning starts.
correlation-vs-causation.name: Correlation Vs Causation

select_when: One realized outcome is hiding the actual quality of the decision process and the missing branches need to be recovered.
Learning depends on exploring omitted paths, assumptions, or conditions rather than merely retelling the observed result.
Pre-mortems, scenario trees, or retrospectives need sharper causal pressure before the team locks in a conclusion.
Leaders need to expose realistic alternative futures before committing to a plan that currently feels inevitable.

failure_modes.mode: Overfitting the Outcome
failure_modes.description: Outcome overfitting can make the past feel inevitable and hide the actual quality of the original decision.
failure_modes.mitigation: Judge the decision by the quality of the process and evidence at the time, not by the outcome; overfitting to results hides the actual decision logic.
failure_modes.source_quote: Overfitting the Outcome

failure_modes.mode: WYSIATI (What You See Is All There Is)
failure_modes.description: WYSIATI can trap the team in the available story instead of searching for the missing branch or missing information.
failure_modes.mitigation: Actively search for the missing branch: ask what information was absent or what alternative was never explored, rather than accepting the available story.
failure_modes.source_quote: WYSIATI (What You See Is All There Is)

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Counterfactuals Become Fiction Instead of Discipline
failure_modes.description: Counterfactuals become dangerous when they drift into fiction untethered from plausible causal structure and context.
failure_modes.mitigation: Anchor counterfactuals to plausible causal structure: require that every 'what if' trace a mechanism that could have actually operated.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Counterfactuals Become Fiction Instead of Discipline

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe to accept this conclusion?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What would you have to believe (WTWHTB)?"

premortem_questions.description: What other plausible paths deserve attention right now before we lock in this plan?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what other plausible paths deserve attention right now

premortem_questions.description: If this project has already failed spectacularly, what branches or assumptions did we miss?
premortem_questions.source_quote: imagine that the endeavor has failed spectacularly

heuristics.description: Mandate a devil's advocate or dialectic standard so every dominant story meets an explicit antithesis.
heuristics.source_quote: Mandate the Devil’s Advocate/Dialectic Standard

heuristics.description: Require independent judgments before discussion so the first view does not anchor the full branch set.
heuristics.source_quote: Decorrleate Errors (Independent Judgment)

heuristics.description: Run a pre-mortem before commitment so the team works backward from failure and exposes hidden branches.
heuristics.source_quote: Conduct a Pre-Mortem

danger_when: The team is spinning imagined alternatives that are untethered from plausible causal structure, base rates, or the real decision context.
The group lacks the emotional willingness to question its preferred story, so counterfactuals would become defensive theater instead of learning.
counterfactual-reasoning.name: Counterfactual Reasoning
reframing_when: The question treats the current situation as the only plausible outcome without exploring what alternative paths were available at the decision point — the past feels inevitable but wasn't.
The question accepts a single causal story without asking what conditions would need to have been different for the outcome to change — hidden assumptions about causation go untested.
The question asks 'what should we do next?' without first asking 'what would we have to believe for our current path to be the right one?' — implicit assumptions remain unspelled.
The question frames success or failure as proof that the decision process was right or wrong, without recovering the missing branches that make the original choice intelligible.
counterfactual-reasoning.reframing_move: Force the question to recover its missing branches: instead of asking about the path taken, ask 'what alternative paths existed at the decision point, and what would have had to be true for each to be the better choice?' — the WTWHTB analysis makes hidden assumptions concrete and testable.

select_when: Legacy assumptions or offerings still function by inertia even though the surrounding conditions have changed materially.
Meaningful reinvention requires abandoning sunk-cost logic before better options become visible.
Innovation requires disciplined replacement of obsolete logic, not just incremental polishing.
The team needs to destroy wrong ideas early so it can build a more truthful and executable alternative.

failure_modes.mode: novelty theater
failure_modes.description: Teams can celebrate novelty while failing to prove the replacement is more useful or executable.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require proof that the replacement is more useful or executable than the incumbent before celebrating novelty.
failure_modes.source_quote: novelty theater

failure_modes.mode: I have seen this one before, it is an X problem
failure_modes.description: False pattern recognition can protect the old frame because it still feels familiar and previously successful.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge the old frame explicitly: name the assumption that has changed, not just the feeling that something new is needed.
failure_modes.source_quote: I have seen this one before, it is an X problem

failure_modes.mode: implementation is treated purely as a fundamentally rational process
failure_modes.description: Ignoring the human dimension turns rational reinvention slides into failed implementation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Account for the human dimension: map stakeholder resistance, retraining needs, and transition costs alongside the rational case for reinvention.
failure_modes.source_quote: implementation is treated purely as a fundamentally rational process

premortem_questions.description: What would need to be true for this supposedly disruptive replacement to actually work better than the incumbent logic?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would need to be true for this idea to work?

premortem_questions.description: Which prior commitment is blocking us from killing a wrong idea quickly enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: public prior strategy blocks timely reinvention

premortem_questions.description: Are we replacing obsolete logic or just performing boldness for ourselves?
premortem_questions.source_quote: clear evidence that the replacement is more truthful

heuristics.description: Get really good at destroying your own wrong ideas early so evidence can redirect the work.
heuristics.source_quote: really good at destroying your own wrong ideas

heuristics.description: Do not boil the ocean; gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the hypothesis.
heuristics.source_quote: DON'T BOIL THE OCEAN

heuristics.description: Use what-you-have-to-believe analysis to make reinvention assumptions concrete before investing behind them.
heuristics.source_quote: what you have to believe

danger_when: Destruction has become novelty theater without evidence that the replacement is more truthful, useful, or executable.
The organization is celebrating boldness while ignoring the human and implementation system needed for the new design to work.
creative-destruction.name: Creative Destruction
reframing_when: The question frames improvement as incremental optimization of the existing system when the situation actually calls for asking whether the existing system should be replaced entirely.
The question treats the incumbent approach, product, or institution as the permanent frame, ignoring that disruption from below or outside is the historical norm, not the exception.
The question escalates commitment to defending the current position rather than asking what would replace it if it were swept away — the creative destruction that would follow is unseen.
The question assumes that stability and continuity are the default, when the natural lifecycle of systems includes displacement by more adaptive successors.
creative-destruction.reframing_move: Before optimizing the current system, ask: 'If this system were going to be replaced by something structurally different, what would that replacement look like — and should we be building it instead?' — the creative destruction reframe makes the replacement scenario thinkable.

select_when: Viability is threshold-dependent, so adoption, liquidity, trust, or participation only works after activity crosses a minimum density.
A few isolated wins will not compound unless they connect into a self-reinforcing system.
The real decision is whether momentum can become self-sustaining inside a defined area, network, or segment rather than whether the idea sounds attractive in general.
The plan needs a concrete answer to what density, scale, or seeding move is required before the system becomes durable.

failure_modes.mode: False Pattern Recognition
failure_modes.description: False pattern recognition can force a threshold explanation onto a problem whose real structure does not match critical-mass dynamics.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test whether the problem actually has threshold dynamics before applying a critical-mass frame; not every growth curve has a tipping point.
failure_modes.source_quote: False Pattern Recognition

failure_modes.mode: Underestimating Uncertainty
failure_modes.description: Anchoring on one target threshold can understate how uncertain the path to durable mass really is.
failure_modes.mitigation: Size the uncertainty around the threshold: anchoring on one target number can understate how contingent and fragile the path to durable mass really is.
failure_modes.source_quote: Underestimating Uncertainty

failure_modes.mode: Strategic Misrepresentation
failure_modes.description: Strategic misrepresentation can make a team overstate current momentum or likely threshold reach just to secure resources.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit momentum claims against verifiable data; strategic misrepresentation about proximity to threshold is common when resources are at stake.
failure_modes.source_quote: Strategic Misrepresentation

premortem_questions.description: Is this merely an analogy, or is the underlying structure truly the same?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is this merely an analogy, or is the underlying structure truly the same?

premortem_questions.description: What variables, beyond the threshold size, have we failed to account for?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What variables, beyond the threshold size, have we failed to account for, and what happens if the outcome is two standard deviations from our estimate?

premortem_questions.description: If this project fails, why did it happen?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this project fails, why did it happen?

heuristics.description: Ask what density of the key resource is needed to reach the critical mass point in the relevant area.
heuristics.source_quote: What density of [resource] is needed to reach the critical mass point in a given area?

heuristics.description: Test the geographic or contextual boundary of the mass instead of assuming all local supply counts equally.
heuristics.source_quote: How far away can two tools be to count toward the same critical mass point in that area?

heuristics.description: If the threshold is unreachable as designed, change the model or seed the system so the threshold becomes reachable.
heuristics.source_quote: Can you tweak the business model so that this critical mass point is reachable or easier to reach?

danger_when: Early launch energy or one-off seeding is being mistaken for durable threshold security before the reinforcement loop is actually stable.
Critical mass is being used as a handy threshold story even though the underlying structure does not really fit a threshold dynamic.
critical-mass.name: Critical Mass

select_when: Important judgments depend on separating evidence from story, authority, or emotional plausibility.
First impressions, status, or internal pressure could dominate the conclusion unless disciplined skepticism slows the team down.
A problem needs decomposition, challenge, and synthesis rather than immediate action or rhetorical certainty.
The quality of reasoning itself is the constraint on decision quality.

failure_modes.mode: Analysis Paralysis (The Hamlet Trap)
failure_modes.description: Analysis paralysis can turn careful inquiry into a delay machine that never cashes out in action.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision deadline that forces analysis to cash out in a recommendation; careful inquiry without a forcing function becomes delay.
failure_modes.source_quote: Analysis Paralysis (The Hamlet Trap)

failure_modes.mode: Maslow’s Hammer (Law of the Instrument)
failure_modes.description: Maslow's hammer can make the analyst force reality to fit a favorite framework instead of questioning the fit.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the analytical frame against at least one alternative before committing to it; Maslow's hammer fits reality to the favorite framework.
failure_modes.source_quote: Maslow’s Hammer (Law of the Instrument)

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Critical Thinking Becomes Detachment Theater
failure_modes.description: Critical thinking fails when it becomes detachment theater that avoids judgment, accountability, and clear recommendation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require every critical analysis to conclude with a clear judgment, owner, and next step; detachment without accountability is theater.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Critical Thinking Becomes Detachment Theater

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what you have to believe

premortem_questions.description: Are we anchoring to the outside view enough, or only protecting our inside-view story?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Anchor decisions to the "outside view"

heuristics.description: Adopt a dialectic standard so every thesis meets an antithesis before synthesis.
heuristics.source_quote: Adopt a Dialectic Standard

heuristics.description: Take an academic selfie before evaluating others so personal bias is visible before judgment hardens.
heuristics.source_quote: The Academic Selfie

heuristics.description: Prioritize the make-or-break hypotheses first so skepticism stays tied to decisions that can change the answer.
heuristics.source_quote: Prioritize Critical Hypotheses

danger_when: Critical thinking is becoming detachment theater, where nuance and skepticism are used to avoid taking a position or making a recommendation.
Slow analysis is crowding out action so completely that the work is sliding into analysis paralysis.
critical-thinking.name: Critical Thinking

select_when: The same message must survive translation across cultures, functions, or status differences without losing the intended action.
Misunderstanding is likely to come from unspoken norms, different politeness expectations, or mismatched assumptions about directness.
Implementation depends not just on being right, but on being understood, trusted, and taken seriously by audiences with different frames of reference.
A complex idea needs to be retuned for the audience's actual cognitive load, vocabulary, and social context.

failure_modes.mode: False Pattern Recognition / Substitution Bias
failure_modes.description: False pattern recognition can make a familiar communication frame look right even when the audience's actual context is different.
failure_modes.mitigation: Validate the audience's actual context before applying a familiar communication frame; surface similarity can mask deep differences in interpretation.
failure_modes.source_quote: False Pattern Recognition / Substitution Bias

failure_modes.mode: Egocentric Bias / Spotlight Effect
failure_modes.description: Egocentric bias can make the sender assume the audience shares the same frame of reference, knowledge level, and interpretation rules.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check for egocentric projection: explicitly state what the audience's frame of reference, knowledge level, and interpretation rules are likely to be.
failure_modes.source_quote: Egocentric Bias / Spotlight Effect

failure_modes.mode: Decision Paralysis
failure_modes.description: Too much detail can cause decision paralysis by forcing the audience to work harder than the message is worth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Calibrate detail to the audience's decision load; excessive context forces them to work harder than the message is worth.
failure_modes.source_quote: Decision Paralysis

premortem_questions.description: What is my audience's frame of reference, and how will this message be interpreted through that lens?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What is my audience's frame of reference, and how will this message be interpreted through that lens?

premortem_questions.description: Have we been explicit about how our recommendations will be implemented, and have we secured buy-in from those responsible for the doing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Have we been explicit about how our recommendations will be implemented, and have we secured buy-in from those responsible for the doing?

premortem_questions.description: What existing models am I applying here, and are the underlying assumptions met in this new context?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What existing models am I applying here, and are the underlying assumptions met in this new context?

heuristics.description: Use perspective taking, initiative, nonverbal sensitivity, and goals together so communication is designed for the actual audience context.
heuristics.source_quote: The PING Approach

heuristics.description: Check whether the conversation is practical, emotional, or social before you decide how direct or detailed the message should be.
heuristics.source_quote: Practical ... Emotional ... Social

heuristics.description: Put the governing thought up front and keep only the information needed for the audience to understand and act.
heuristics.source_quote: BLUF ... governing thought

danger_when: Cultural models are being used as stereotype shortcuts instead of prompts to investigate the actual audience and local norms.
Translation is optimizing for harmony so aggressively that it hides disagreement, urgency, or real trade-offs.
cross-cultural-communication-frameworks.name: Cross Cultural Communication Frameworks
reframing_when: The question assumes a shared communication style (direct vs indirect, individual vs collective, low-context vs high-context) when the stakeholders' communication norms differ and the frame reflects only one set of norms.
The question diagnoses a 'communication problem' or 'alignment issue' when the real gap is between different cultural frames about what constitutes a problem, a priority, or a good outcome.
The question frames resistance or silence as disagreement or disengagement when in certain cultural contexts these signals mean something structurally different.
The question exports high-context or low-context assumptions to an audience that operates on the other end of the spectrum, making the question itself illegible to the people it is meant to help.
cross-cultural-communication-frameworks.reframing_move: Before diagnosing a communication or alignment problem, ask: 'Are the parties actually disagreeing, or are they operating with different cultural frames about what the question is and how to engage with it?' — the cross-cultural reframe separates content disagreement from frame mismatch.

select_when: People from different backgrounds are interpreting the same behavior through incompatible expectations about trust, hierarchy, conflict, or commitment.
A decision-maker needs a comparative lens rather than a personal intuition about how others will read the same action.
Coordination quality depends on distinguishing cultural context clues from purely individual preference.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can mistake framework labels for verified facts about individuals and stop investigating the r...
failure_modes.description: Teams can mistake framework labels for verified facts about individuals and stop investigating the real relationship.
failure_modes.mitigation: Treat framework labels as hypotheses about individuals, not facts; investigate the real relationship rather than stopping at the cultural category.
failure_modes.source_quote: The model fails when cultural dimensions are treated as fixed labels for individuals rather than probabilistic context clues that still need verification in the real relationship.

failure_modes.mode: if the only tool you have is a hammer, to treat everything as if it were a nail
failure_modes.description: A familiar cultural frame can become Maslow's hammer, torturing the situation to fit the model instead of testing whether the assumptions hold.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test whether the cultural frame actually fits the situation; if the model requires torturing the data, the frame is wrong for this case.
failure_modes.source_quote: if the only tool you have is a hammer, to treat everything as if it were a nail

failure_modes.mode: Adaptation to one norm set can hide the opportunity costs of alternative coordination strategies.
failure_modes.description: Adaptation to one norm set can hide the opportunity costs of alternative coordination strategies.
failure_modes.mitigation: Evaluate the opportunity costs of adapting to one cultural norm set; alternative coordination strategies may be available.
failure_modes.source_quote: cultural-dimensions-theory conflicts with opportunity-cost (opportunity cost) when adaptation to one norm set masks foregone outcomes in alternative contexts.

premortem_questions.description: What part of this interpretation is a verified signal from the relationship, and what part is only a cultural hypothesis?
premortem_questions.source_quote: fixed labels for individuals rather than probabilistic context clues

premortem_questions.description: What assumption embedded in this cultural frame might be violated by the current situation?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumptions embedded in this model might be violated by the current situation

premortem_questions.description: What concrete coordination commitment are we postponing by keeping the cultural analysis too broad?
premortem_questions.source_quote: broad cultural branching postpones concrete coordination commitments

heuristics.description: Use cultural dimensions as comparative hypotheses, not as labels that end the inquiry.
heuristics.source_quote: probabilistic context clues that still need verification

heuristics.description: Test multiple frames before deciding which cultural interpretation actually explains the behavior.
heuristics.source_quote: Test Multiple Frames

heuristics.description: Translate cultural insight into a concrete coordination move instead of stopping at descriptive nuance.
heuristics.source_quote: leaders must anticipate how trust, hierarchy, conflict, or commitment may be read differently across groups

danger_when: The model is being used to stereotype individuals instead of generating testable context clues.
Broad cultural branching is postponing concrete coordination choices that now need commitment.
cultural-dimensions-theory.name: Cultural Dimensions Theory

select_when: The same facts are being interpreted through different human worlds shaped by norms, status, pressure, or local context.
Adoption matters as much as analysis, and a recommendation will fail unless it is translated into the language and motivations of the people who must act.
Leaders need to separate stereotype from situated truth and understand whether behavior is driven by role, culture, pressure, or history.
A diverse group or cross-context team needs better perspective taking before it can coordinate effectively.

failure_modes.mode: Motivated Reasoning
failure_modes.description: Motivated reasoning can turn cultural interpretation into a story that protects the observer's desired conclusion rather than what is true.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate observation from interpretation: document what you saw before layering on cultural meaning, and check whether motivated reasoning is shaping the story.
failure_modes.source_quote: Motivated Reasoning

failure_modes.mode: Confirmation Bias / Groupthink
failure_modes.description: Confirmation bias and groupthink can keep a team reading new observations through home-culture expectations.
failure_modes.mitigation: Seek perspectives from within the culture you are interpreting; confirmation bias and groupthink favor home-culture expectations.
failure_modes.source_quote: Confirmation Bias / Groupthink

failure_modes.mode: Cognitive Entrenchment
failure_modes.description: Cognitive entrenchment can make prior experience feel like cultural wisdom even when the context is genuinely different.
failure_modes.mitigation: Treat prior cross-cultural experience as a starting point, not a conclusion; cognitive entrenchment can make old experience feel like wisdom in a new context.
failure_modes.source_quote: Cognitive Entrenchment

premortem_questions.description: Are we arguing with one another, or are we seeking to understand each other's perspectives to figure out what is true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are we arguing with one another, or are we seeking to understand each other’s perspectives to figure out what is true?

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If this decision fails disastrously, what specific cultural assumption or unrecognized organizational context will be the root cause?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this decision fails disastrously, what specific cultural assumption or unrecognized organizational context will be the root cause?

heuristics.description: Force perspective taking until you can describe the other side's view compellingly enough that they would recognize it.
heuristics.source_quote: Force Perspective Taking

heuristics.description: Use dialectic norms so cultural interpretation is challenged before it becomes the group's default explanation.
heuristics.source_quote: Employ the Dialectic Standard

heuristics.description: Boil the situation down to a simple why before expanding into cultural explanation so you do not hide the real operating issue behind abstraction.
heuristics.source_quote: Can I boil this idea down to two sentences

danger_when: Cultural understanding is being used as a polite label for untested assumptions about motives, resistance, or alignment.
The problem is being reduced to culture talk when the real issue is a concrete incentive, role, or process failure that should be named directly.
cultural-intelligence.name: Cultural Intelligence
reframing_when: The question assumes its framing is culturally neutral when the values, priorities, and problem definitions embedded in it reflect a specific cultural lens that other stakeholders would not share.

The question treats one stakeholder group's norms as universal — 'obviously the priority is efficiency' or 'of course we optimize for individual choice' — when cultural context determines which frame is appropriate.
The question frames cross-cultural friction as a communication problem when it is actually a framing problem — the parties define the problem differently because their cultural frames differ.
The question exports a Western, corporate, or disciplinary frame to a context where that frame creates more problems than it solves.
cultural-intelligence.reframing_move: Before treating the current framing as shared, ask: 'Whose cultural frame shaped this question, and how would the problem look different through the cultural lens of the people most affected?' — the cultural intelligence reframe reveals frame-level ethnocentrism.

select_when: The first answer is probably shallow and the real problem is still underdefined.
Better questions could change the frame by exposing hidden assumptions, causes, or opportunities.
Inquiry still serves a concrete decision, design move, or learning objective rather than free-floating fascination.
The work needs deeper cause-finding, assumption stress-testing, or empathic discovery rather than a quick surface explanation.

failure_modes.mode: The first explanation provided is often insufficient and misleading.
failure_modes.description: The first explanation can be dangerously shallow when teams stop at proximate causes instead of digging toward the true cause.
failure_modes.mitigation: Ask 'why' one more time when the first explanation arrives quickly; shallow proximate causes are satisfying but often wrong.
failure_modes.source_quote: The first explanation provided is often insufficient and misleading.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Curiosity Forgets the Decision It Is Supposed to Serve.
failure_modes.description: Inquiry can become wasteful when it keeps widening the search space after uncertainty is already reduced enough to act.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision threshold for when inquiry should stop: once uncertainty is reduced enough to act, further widening the search space is waste.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Curiosity Forgets the Decision It Is Supposed to Serve.

failure_modes.mode: Boiling the Ocean
failure_modes.description: Fact-gathering can turn into boiling the ocean when the team keeps collecting material without a clear hypothesis or decision need.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require a clear hypothesis before gathering more material; fact-collection without a question to answer is boiling the ocean.
failure_modes.source_quote: Boiling the Ocean

premortem_questions.description: What if the first answer is mostly wrong because the real problem is still underdefined?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when the first answer is probably shallow.

premortem_questions.description: What better question would change the frame by exposing a hidden assumption, cause, or opportunity?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when better questions can change the frame.

premortem_questions.description: How does this line of inquiry solve the problem or advance the next decision, rather than just expanding the search?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what you're doing solve the problem? How does it advance your thinking?

heuristics.description: Use repeated why-questions to move from surface causes toward the root cause instead of stopping at the first plausible story.
heuristics.source_quote: The Five Whys Technique

heuristics.description: Use Socratic questioning to stress-test assumptions and make the reasoning explicit enough to inspect.
heuristics.source_quote: The Socratic Method

heuristics.description: Structure inquiry around relevant variables, a specific next-step question, and a clear decision so curiosity stays useful.
heuristics.source_quote: Structured Deep Thinking

danger_when: Exploration is widening the search space after the relevant uncertainty has already been reduced enough to act.
The team is collecting more facts without a clear purpose, next-step question, or decision it is supposed to serve.
curiosity.name: Curiosity
reframing_when: The question has already settled on what is interesting or important about the situation, closing off exploratory inquiry that might redefine the problem space entirely.
The question treats unknowns as threats to be managed rather than as signals pointing toward where the real leverage lies — curiosity about what is not yet understood would open new frames.
The question assumes the problem is well-defined and ready for solution-mode when the situation actually calls for open-ended exploration to discover what the right question is.
The question frames learning as a cost ('we don't have time to investigate') when the information gap itself is the highest-risk element in the decision.
curiosity.reframing_move: Before converging on a solution, ask: 'What are we most curious about in this situation, and what would change if we followed that curiosity before deciding?' — the curiosity reframe reopens the problem space by treating unknowns as opportunities rather than obstacles.

select_when: An expert is communicating with, teaching, or designing for an audience with significantly less domain exposure.
Onboarding, training, or documentation is being produced by subject-matter experts for novice users.
A product, interface, or process is being designed by specialists who cannot reliably simulate the experience of a first-time user.
Communication is producing confusion or mis-comprehension that the communicator cannot explain — they cannot see what is unclear.
Feedback from stakeholders or customers repeatedly surfaces misaligned understanding despite expert explanations.

failure_modes.mode: Invisible compression
failure_modes.description: Invisible compression causes the expert to skip logical steps that feel trivially obvious, leaving the audience without the premises needed to follow the conclusion.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the explanation on a genuine novice; if they cannot follow the logical steps, the expert has skipped premises that feel obvious but are not.
failure_modes.source_quote: Invisible compression

failure_modes.mode: Vocabulary mismatch
failure_modes.description: Vocabulary mismatch embeds technical terms that carry precise meaning for the expert and approximate or absent meaning for the novice, with neither party registering the divergence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Replace or define technical terms using the audience's vocabulary; precision that only the expert can parse is noise to everyone else.
failure_modes.source_quote: Vocabulary mismatch

failure_modes.mode: Recursive curse
failure_modes.description: Recursive curse produces a simplified version still calibrated for the expert's knowledge level — 'simplified' relative to expert discourse, not relative to the novice's starting point.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify that any 'simplified' version is calibrated to the audience's knowledge level, not to a slightly less expert version of the expert.
failure_modes.source_quote: Recursive curse

premortem_questions.description: What step am I taking for granted here that my audience would need spelled out — and can I identify where in this explanation I first assumed shared context?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Articulate the Steps You're Skipping

premortem_questions.description: Which words in this explanation would a smart non-specialist not understand, or understand differently than I mean?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Every piece of expert communication should be scanned for terms that carry compressed meaning

premortem_questions.description: Can the person I've just briefed explain this back in their own words — not mine — without using the same terms I used?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Feedback-loop absence: polite nodding and superficial agreement are misread as understanding

heuristics.description: Apply the Feynman standard: if the concept cannot be explained in plain language without jargon to someone with no prior exposure, the communication is still inside the curse.
heuristics.source_quote: Use the Feynman Standard

heuristics.description: Apply the Stranger Test: before finalizing any communication, ask whether a smart person who has never encountered this topic would understand it — not whether another expert would.
heuristics.source_quote: The Stranger Test

heuristics.description: Observe actual novice users directly — don't predict their confusion. Expert predictions of novice experience suffer from the same curse they are trying to correct.
heuristics.source_quote: Include genuine novice users in design and testing processes. Observe, don't ask.

danger_when: Communicating with peers who share the same knowledge base — in that context the curse does not operate and calibrating down wastes time.
The model is being used to excuse novice confusion as inevitably the communicator's fault — sometimes the content is genuinely difficult and requires study, not simplified communication.
curse-of-knowledge.name: Curse Of Knowledge
curse-of-knowledge.input_type: expert knowledge to be communicated or applied
curse-of-knowledge.output_type: communication gap identification and novice-calibrated revision

select_when: The problem is adversarial, fast-changing, and capable of cascading across technical, human, and governance layers at once.
Defenders must model attacker incentives, failure chains, and cross-domain consequences rather than only enumerate controls.
Security decisions need to be pressure-tested against realistic scenarios instead of static compliance or architecture diagrams alone.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can optimize for appearing secure instead of being secure when stakeholder incentives are misa...
failure_modes.description: Teams can optimize for appearing secure instead of being secure when stakeholder incentives are misaligned with the measured outcome.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish appearing secure from being secure: audit whether the measured outcomes align with actual threat mitigation, not just compliance optics.
failure_modes.source_quote: cybersecurity-thinking-models conflicts with incentives (incentives) when stakeholder incentives are not aligned with the security outcome being measured, so defenders optimize for appearing secure rather than being secure.

failure_modes.mode: Threat modeling can become decorative if it never touches asset specificity, ownership, or operation...
failure_modes.description: Threat modeling can become decorative if it never touches asset specificity, ownership, or operational failure chains.
failure_modes.mitigation: Anchor threat modeling to asset specificity, ownership, and operational failure chains; decorative models that never touch real attack surfaces are waste.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams fetishize threat models, simulations, or mental frameworks but skip asset specificity, attack surface reality, or operational ownership.

failure_modes.mode: Security decisions can become static and underfit reality when they are built from compliance diagra...
failure_modes.description: Security decisions can become static and underfit reality when they are built from compliance diagrams rather than realistic adversarial scenarios.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build security decisions from realistic adversarial scenarios rather than static compliance diagrams; threat landscapes shift faster than checklists.
failure_modes.source_quote: Best used when security decisions must be pressure-tested against realistic scenarios instead of static compliance or architecture diagrams alone.

premortem_questions.description: Which stakeholder gains if this plan underperforms quietly rather than fails visibly?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Every plan has a stakeholder whose incentives are not fully aligned with the outcome you are measuring.

premortem_questions.description: What failure chain crosses technical, human, and governance layers if an attacker chooses the most inconvenient path?
premortem_questions.source_quote: cascading across technical, human, and governance layers at once

premortem_questions.description: Are we modeling realistic attacker incentives or only listing controls we already know how to talk about?
premortem_questions.source_quote: model attacker incentives, failure chains, and cross-domain consequences

heuristics.description: Threat-model the plan against adversarial incentives before treating the architecture as sufficient protection.
heuristics.source_quote: adversarial, fast-changing

heuristics.description: Use scenario simulation to test ownership gaps and hidden dependencies, not just control lists.
heuristics.source_quote: test ideas at the speed of compute and anticipate outcomes with unprecedented clarity

heuristics.description: Name the misaligned actor and fallback path before claiming the plan is robust.
heuristics.source_quote: Name that party, describe what they stand to gain, and identify which dependency on them has no fallback.

danger_when: Threat-model language is being fetishized while asset specificity, attack-surface reality, and operational ownership stay vague.
Scenario work is creating false certainty about rare threats while everyday control failures remain underexamined.
cybersecurity-thinking-models.name: Cybersecurity Thinking Models

select_when: Large, noisy evidence sets need to be turned into a bounded reasoning process with explicit hypotheses, tests, and decision-relevant outputs.
Teams must separate question framing, measurement design, modeling, and communication so analysis does not collapse into dashboard theater.
Competing explanations need to be compared against observed data rather than settled by authority, intuition, or anecdote.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can build precise models around the wrong target when convenient proxies stand in for the real...
failure_modes.description: Teams can build precise models around the wrong target when convenient proxies stand in for the real causal question.
failure_modes.mitigation: Validate that the measured proxy is causally connected to the real question; precise models around the wrong target produce confident wrong answers.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when convenient proxy metrics are treated as the underlying reality, producing precise analysis around the wrong target.

failure_modes.mode: Analysis can degrade into dashboard theater if framing, measurement, modeling, and communication are...
failure_modes.description: Analysis can degrade into dashboard theater if framing, measurement, modeling, and communication are never separated.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate framing, measurement, modeling, and communication as distinct steps; collapsing them produces dashboard theater instead of insight.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works best when teams must separate question framing, measurement design, modeling, and communication so analysis does not collapse into dashboard theater.

failure_modes.mode: Metric optimization can suppress stakeholder-context signals that explain why the measured pattern i...
failure_modes.description: Metric optimization can suppress stakeholder-context signals that explain why the measured pattern is shifting.
failure_modes.mitigation: Integrate stakeholder context into the analysis loop; metric optimization that ignores why the pattern is shifting will miss the explanation.
failure_modes.source_quote: data-science-reasoning-framework conflicts with empathy (empathy) when metric optimization suppresses stakeholder-context signals that explain behavior shifts.

premortem_questions.description: What causal question are we actually trying to answer before we decide what to measure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: modeling before agreeing on the causal question or business decision

premortem_questions.description: Which proxy are we treating as reality, and what behavior might it be hiding?
premortem_questions.source_quote: convenient proxy metrics are treated as the underlying reality

premortem_questions.description: What competing explanation would still fit the observed data if our preferred story were wrong?
premortem_questions.source_quote: competing explanations need to be compared against observed data

heuristics.description: Separate framing, measurement, modeling, and communication before you call the work analysis.
heuristics.source_quote: separate question framing, measurement design, modeling, and communication

heuristics.description: Write the causal question first so modeling effort stays tied to a decision-relevant target.
heuristics.source_quote: causal question or business decision

heuristics.description: Force competing explanations to survive contact with the same observed data.
heuristics.source_quote: competing explanations need to be compared against observed data

danger_when: Data volume is substituting for problem clarity and the team is modeling before agreeing on the causal question or business decision.
Convenient proxy metrics are being treated as the underlying reality.
data-science-reasoning-framework.name: Data Science Reasoning Framework

select_when: A failure is real but the cause is ambiguous, distributed, or hidden behind several interacting components.
The team can form explicit hypotheses, isolate variables, and test the system instead of arguing from intuition.
Reliability matters enough that vague explanations are more dangerous than the time required to trace the true defect path.
The work is conflating symptom, diagnosis, and solution design, so it needs a disciplined root-cause pass before action.

failure_modes.mode: Debugging can optimize noise when the team has not agreed on the actual failure condition.
failure_modes.description: Debugging can optimize noise when the team has not agreed on the actual failure condition.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define the actual failure condition first; debugging without an agreed-on target optimizes noise.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when people start debugging before agreeing on the actual failure condition, so they optimize noise, symptoms, or tools instead of the broken behavior.

failure_modes.mode: Teams jump into analysis too early and lose clarity on the output or problem structure they actually...
failure_modes.description: Teams jump into analysis too early and lose clarity on the output or problem structure they actually need.
failure_modes.mitigation: Structure the problem before diving into analysis: clarify the expected output, the actual output, and the boundary between them.
failure_modes.source_quote: A common anti-pattern is the human inclination to jump immediately into analysis or modeling without first being clear about the needed output or the underlying problem structure.

failure_modes.mode: Frameworks can become shackles when familiar analytical cuts are reused even though their assumption...
failure_modes.description: Frameworks can become shackles when familiar analytical cuts are reused even though their assumptions do not fit the case.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge familiar analytical cuts when their assumptions do not fit the current case; frameworks become shackles when reused without adaptation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Routinely applying analytical frameworks without questioning their inherent assumptions leads to the "hammer-in-search-of-a-nail" problem.

premortem_questions.description: What's the one-day answer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What's the one-day answer?

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The concept of "What would you have to believe?" is a tool for constructive confrontation used to debug underlying assumptions.

premortem_questions.description: Are we discussing the machine level, the case-at-hand level, or blurring the two?
premortem_questions.source_quote: When a problem occurs, discussion must happen at two levels: the machine level ... and the case-at-hand level.

heuristics.description: Disaggregate the problem into logical parts so the diagnostic cut exposes insight rather than just creating more categories.
heuristics.source_quote: Good disaggregation aims to find a cut through the problem that exposes insight.

heuristics.description: Use hypothesis-driven diagnosis to gather only the facts needed to prove or disprove the current explanation.
heuristics.source_quote: This approach prevents teams from attempting to "boil the ocean" by gathering only enough facts required to prove or disprove the hypothesis.

heuristics.description: Keep diagnosis sequenced before solution design so symptom pressure does not crowd out root-cause tracing.
heuristics.source_quote: Successful debugging requires performing these steps one at a time and in order, as blurring the steps interferes with uncovering true problems.

danger_when: People have not agreed on the actual failure condition, so debugging would optimize noise, symptoms, or tools instead of broken behavior.
The problem is genuinely novel and needs abductive or creative generation before deductive debugging frameworks can help.
debugging-strategies.name: Debugging Strategies

select_when: A decision has explicit branches, trigger conditions, or kill criteria that can be made visible before resources are committed.
The branching logic has to be explicit before commitment because the wrong branch would create irreversible downside once a threshold, approval, or counterparty response lands.
Thresholds, approvals, or countermoves can be expressed clearly enough that the tree sharpens action rather than merely documenting options.
The next action depends on naming the branch that activates if one condition fails.

failure_modes.mode: A tree can look complete while still missing the threshold, dependency break, or second-order conseq...
failure_modes.description: A tree can look complete while still missing the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that kills the favored branch.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the tree for missing thresholds, dependency breaks, or second-order consequences that could kill the favored branch.
failure_modes.source_quote: Branches are modeled neatly enough to look complete, but the tree does not name the threshold, dependency break, or second-order consequence that would terminate the favored path before scale.

failure_modes.mode: Placing excessive emphasis on the mean outcome (the base case) and insufficient weight on outcomes far from the mean.
failure_modes.description: Mean-outcome thinking can hide distribution risk and send the team down the wrong branch.
failure_modes.mitigation: Replace mean-outcome thinking with distribution-aware assessment: simulate the range of outcomes for each branch to expose hidden risk.
failure_modes.source_quote: Placing excessive emphasis on the mean outcome (the base case) and insufficient weight on outcomes far from the mean.

failure_modes.mode: Confirmation bias or Maslow's hammer can shape the tree around the analyst's preferred hypothesis in...
failure_modes.description: Confirmation bias or Maslow's hammer can shape the tree around the analyst's preferred hypothesis instead of the real structure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the tree structure was shaped by the analyst's preferred hypothesis; confirmation bias can prune branches before they are tested.
failure_modes.source_quote: the modeler risks unconsciously defining branches to confirm an existing hypothesis or using the only tool they are familiar with

failure_modes.mode: the external condition that would force a different choice ... was never modeled
failure_modes.description: A missing state-of-the-world branch can make the recommendation look stable only because the decisive condition was never modeled.
failure_modes.mitigation: Explicitly model the state-of-the-world conditions that could change the recommendation; a missing branch makes the tree look stable by omission.
failure_modes.source_quote: the external condition that would force a different choice ... was never modeled

premortem_questions.description: Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which branch in this tree looks viable only because we have not named the threshold that kills it, the dependency that can fail first, or the second-order consequence that makes an early win a later loss?

premortem_questions.description: Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which threshold assumption would reverse which branch is correct, and what evidence would tell us we have already crossed it before sunk effort locks us into the wrong path?

premortem_questions.description: Which branch in this tree looks precise on paper but still leaves the operator without a usable decision rule when the key signal is noisy, late, or contested?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which branch in this tree looks precise on paper but still leaves the operator without a usable decision rule when the key signal is noisy, late, or contested, and what simpler branch instruction would they actually need at the moment of choice?

premortem_questions.description: What state of the world would force this decision onto a different branch immediately, and where is that branch represented in the tree?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What state of the world would force this decision onto a different branch immediately, where is that branch represented in the tree

heuristics.description: Prune low-impact or low-change branches after disaggregating the problem.
heuristics.source_quote: Prioritization and Pruning: After disaggregating a problem into branches, the model enables prioritization by helping users determine what not to work on.

heuristics.description: Force each option to carry explicit preferences, payoffs, and probabilities.
heuristics.source_quote: Effective use requires explicitly defining and integrating three components for each option: Preferences, Payoffs, Probabilities.

heuristics.description: Embed explicit branch logic so the system can name when action A should beat action B.
heuristics.source_quote: Embedding Explicit Logic: For high-stakes decisions or automated systems, the Decision Tree helps explicitly define the rules for choosing action A over action B.

heuristics.description: Define branch-kill thresholds in advance so the team knows exactly which observed condition ends the favored path instead of improvising under pressure.
heuristics.source_quote: the threshold that kills it

danger_when: Payoffs or probabilities are so unstable that a neat tree would create false precision and confidence rather than real guidance.
Strong feedback loops, path dependence, or interdependencies make the branches less independent than the diagram implies.
decision-trees.name: Decision Trees
reframing_when: The question collapses a multi-branch decision into a yes/no binary when the real structure involves distinct options with different payoffs and probabilities.
The question hides the option structure by pre-selecting which alternatives exist — making the tree explicit reveals branches the question suppressed.
The question skips the threshold question ('should we do this at all?') and jumps to 'how should we do this?' — the tree's first branch is missing.
decision-trees.reframing_move: Make the decision tree explicit: draw the branches the question suppresses, including the 'do nothing' and 'do something else entirely' branches — the question often becomes 'which branch?' rather than 'should we?'

select_when: The problem is too tangled to reason about as a whole and teams keep mixing symptoms, causes, owners, and options in one blob.
Progress depends on identifying which branches are causal, which are descriptive, and which are actually actionable.
The breakdown should hand off into priorities, owners, sequencing, and the few branches worth pursuing rather than ending as a neat tree on paper.
High-volume information needs to be organized into a structure that reveals the key drivers instead of leaving facts isolated and unusable.

failure_modes.mode: Boiling the Ocean
failure_modes.description: Boiling the ocean can turn decomposition into exhaustive branch collection that obscures the answer instead of sharpening it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Stop branching when the next level of detail no longer changes the conclusion; exhaustive decomposition obscures instead of sharpens.
failure_modes.source_quote: Boiling the Ocean

failure_modes.mode: Framing Error/False Pattern Recognition
failure_modes.description: A familiar framework can impose a false pattern on a novel problem and send the tree down the wrong cut.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge the framework's fit before applying it: if the cut was borrowed from a different problem type, it may impose a false pattern.
failure_modes.source_quote: Framing Error/False Pattern Recognition
