failure_modes.mode: Confirmation Bias (Motivated Decomposition)
failure_modes.description: Motivated decomposition can structure the tree to justify the preferred answer instead of discovering the true drivers.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the tree structure leads to the truth or toward a preferred answer; motivated decomposition hides behind logical-looking branches.
failure_modes.source_quote: Confirmation Bias (Motivated Decomposition)

premortem_questions.description: What is the single, core purpose we are trying to achieve with this decomposition?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What is the single, core purpose we are trying to achieve with this decomposition?

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions are embedded in this chosen framework or logic tree?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumptions are embedded in this chosen framework/logic tree?

premortem_questions.description: Are we breaking down symptoms, or are we identifying the key drivers that are truly under our control?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are we breaking down symptoms, or are we identifying the key drivers that are truly under our control?

heuristics.description: Use mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive branches so the parts stay distinct while covering the whole.
heuristics.source_quote: MECE Principle (Mutually Exclusive, Collectively Exhaustive)

heuristics.description: Build logic trees and issue pyramids so claims, analyses, and supporting branches stay structurally connected.
heuristics.source_quote: Logic Trees and Issue Pyramids

heuristics.description: Use plain-English explanation to reveal whether the current decomposition actually makes the concept manageable.
heuristics.source_quote: The Feynman Technique

danger_when: A familiar framework is being forced onto a novel problem just because it is the easiest cut available.
The tree is creating false certainty by making the structure look tidy even though the causal landscape may still be wrong.
decomposition.name: Decomposition
reframing_when: The question treats a complex, multi-dimensional problem as a single monolithic decision when breaking it into components would reveal that different parts have different optimal frames.
The question feels overwhelming or intractable because the frame is too coarse-grained — decomposition into sub-questions would make hidden structure visible.

The question conflates multiple independent dimensions (cost, quality, speed, risk) into a single judgment call when separating them would reveal that the apparent dilemma dissolves at the component level.
The question is stuck because the asker is trying to optimize everything at once instead of identifying which sub-problem is actually the binding constraint.
decomposition.reframing_move: Before answering the question as a whole, ask: 'What are the independent sub-questions hidden inside this question, and does the dilemma survive when we address each one separately?' — the decomposition reframe reveals that apparent deadlocks often dissolve at a finer grain.

select_when: Actions and consequences are separated in time, so a naive short-term read could mistake no result yet for no effect.
A system contains feedback loops, capacity constraints, or implementation lags that could trigger overreaction if ignored.
The team needs to distinguish between a pause that improves judgment and a delay that merely avoids commitment or ownership.
Performance is being judged too quickly even though the real signal depends on delayed feedback.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can treat delayed feedback as proof that an intervention failed and overcorrect before the sys...
failure_modes.description: Teams can treat delayed feedback as proof that an intervention failed and overcorrect before the system has time to respond.
failure_modes.mitigation: Account for system response time before judging an intervention; overcorrecting before delayed feedback arrives creates unnecessary oscillation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams treat delayed feedback as proof that an intervention failed, then overcorrect before the system has had time to respond.

failure_modes.mode: Waiting can become romanticized reflection when the next useful move is already clear and the real c...
failure_modes.description: Waiting can become romanticized reflection when the next useful move is already clear and the real cost is execution drift.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set an explicit wait threshold: if the next useful move is already clear, waiting is avoidance dressed as reflection.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when people romanticize waiting and reflection even though the next useful move is already clear and the real cost is execution drift.

failure_modes.mode: The Planning Fallacy (Failing to Anticipate Delay)
failure_modes.description: Plans can quietly fail because people underestimate how often multi-step work will slip behind its intended timing.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build delay buffers into multi-step plans; underestimating how often work slips behind its intended timing is the default human error.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Planning Fallacy (Failing to Anticipate Delay)

premortem_questions.description: Are we reading no visible result yet as no effect, even though the system is known to respond with a lag?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when actions and consequences are separated in time, so naive short-term reading would mistake "no result yet" for "no effect".

premortem_questions.description: Is this pause improving judgment, or is it just avoiding commitment or ownership?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best used when the team must distinguish between a pause that improves judgment and a delay that merely avoids commitment or ownership.

premortem_questions.description: If one essential lag or capacity constraint is missing from our mental model, how would that distort the correction we are about to make?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Analyzing delayed feedback helps managers understand why their corrective actions might be too aggressive or too timid, leading to oscillation or drift in system performance.

heuristics.description: Map the delayed feedback path before changing course so you know when a response should realistically become visible.
heuristics.source_quote: Recognizing the location and length of time delays is key to successful system intervention.

heuristics.description: Chunk complex work into paced stages so delay becomes a learning aid instead of a source of overload.
heuristics.source_quote: Since working memory is a limited resource, complex topics must be broken down temporally, focusing on steps or stages in a sequence.

heuristics.description: Set aggressive but achievable deadlines when the true problem is self-generated sprawl rather than useful incubation time.
heuristics.source_quote: Setting aggressive (but achievable) deadlines forces prioritization and helps free the mind from the sustained background stress of a distant deadline.

danger_when: People are romanticizing waiting and reflection even though the next useful move is already clear and the real cost is execution drift.
The model is being used to excuse planning drag instead of to understand timing, lagged feedback, or paced learning.
delays.name: Delays

select_when: Performance improvement requires specific reps, structured feedback loops, and error correction rather than vague effort.
The bottleneck is a weak sub-skill, poor diagnostic habit, slow recognition pattern, or inconsistent execution routine.
Progress needs to be measurable over time against a tighter benchmark, coach review, or replayable standard.
The team knows the target capability but still lacks a disciplined way to build it reliably.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when practice is intense but not reality-linked.
failure_modes.description: Practice can become fake progress when intense drills are disconnected from the real failure modes and feedback conditions of live performance.
failure_modes.mitigation: Connect drills to the actual failure modes and feedback conditions of live performance; practice that never encounters real-world friction is fake progress.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when practice is intense but not reality-linked.

failure_modes.mode: Flawed Model Adoption
failure_modes.description: A flawed mental model can become more dangerous when repetition entrenches the error instead of exposing it to corrective feedback.
failure_modes.mitigation: Pair repetition with corrective feedback: a flawed mental model becomes more dangerous when entrenched through practice without correction.
failure_modes.source_quote: Flawed Model Adoption

failure_modes.mode: Premature Application
failure_modes.description: A well-practiced tool can get overapplied to novel problems simply because it is the one the practitioner has most rehearsed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit whether the most-rehearsed tool is being applied outside its domain; familiarity from practice can create Maslow's hammer.
failure_modes.source_quote: Premature Application

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions are implicit in the framework or drill I am currently using, and are those assumptions met here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumptions are implicit in the frameworks I am currently using, and are those assumptions met in this new context?

premortem_questions.description: What key perspective from outside my practiced domain am I neglecting that might expose a blind spot?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What key perspective (from a discipline outside my domain of expertise) am I neglecting that might uncover a blind spot in my thinking?

premortem_questions.description: Can I explain this concept simply enough that a novice would understand it, and if not, where are my knowledge gaps?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Can I explain this concept simply enough that a five-year-old would understand it? If not, where are my knowledge gaps?

heuristics.description: Define improvement against a tighter benchmark, coach review, or replayable standard so practice stays measurable over time.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when progress must be measurable over time.

heuristics.description: Break capability into sub-skills so drills target the actual weak component instead of vague overall effort.
heuristics.source_quote: Works when the bottleneck is sub-skill quality.

heuristics.description: Pull in outside views and alternative frames so repeated practice sharpens reality contact instead of entrenching one favored model.
heuristics.source_quote: Embrace the Outside View.

danger_when: Practice is intense but disconnected from the real failure modes, stakes, or feedback conditions of live performance.
A genuinely novel problem is being forced into the most practiced drill because that routine is the tool already at hand.
deliberate-practice.name: Deliberate Practice

select_when: Easy fluency is being mistaken for real understanding and the team needs retrieval, effort, or friction to expose whether the knowledge will hold up later.
The goal is transfer under new conditions rather than short-term smooth performance on the easiest version of the task.
Support can be calibrated to stretch capability without dropping the learner or operator into confusion and overload.
The work requires disciplined focus and productive discomfort to build durable competence instead of shallow familiarity.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when fluency would otherwise be mistaken for mastery.
failure_modes.description: Easy fluency can create a false sense of mastery when the learner has not had to retrieve, struggle, or transfer the skill under pressure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test mastery under transfer conditions: easy fluency during practice does not prove the learner can retrieve the skill under pressure or in a new context.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when fluency would otherwise be mistaken for mastery.

failure_modes.mode: Best when support can be calibrated rather than removed entirely.
failure_modes.description: Challenge can become counterproductive when support is removed so aggressively that the learner is overwhelmed instead of stretched.
failure_modes.mitigation: Calibrate challenge to the learner's current edge: removing support so aggressively that they are overwhelmed defeats the purpose of productive struggle.
failure_modes.source_quote: Best when support can be calibrated rather than removed entirely.

failure_modes.mode: The Danger of Abstraction
failure_modes.description: Abstract difficulty can confuse rather than strengthen learning when people never get the concrete building blocks they still need.
failure_modes.mitigation: Provide concrete building blocks before abstract difficulty; confusion from missing foundations is not the same as productive retrieval effort.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Danger of Abstraction

premortem_questions.description: What part of this learning path feels smooth only because we have not required retrieval or transfer yet?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when fluency would otherwise be mistaken for mastery.

premortem_questions.description: Where did we remove support so far that the challenge stopped being productive and started becoming overload?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when support can be calibrated rather than removed entirely.

premortem_questions.description: Which concrete building block is still missing beneath the abstraction we are asking people to struggle through?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The Danger of Abstraction

heuristics.description: Use retrieval, effort, and friction deliberately so understanding is tested for durability rather than assumed from fluency.
heuristics.source_quote: Most useful when fluency would otherwise be mistaken for mastery.

heuristics.description: Calibrate support so the task stretches capability without dropping people into confusion or shutdown.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when support can be calibrated rather than removed entirely.

heuristics.description: Preserve enough concrete structure that the difficulty strengthens transfer instead of becoming empty hardship.
heuristics.source_quote: Works when the challenge is productive precisely because it strengthens transfer.

danger_when: Difficulty is being added without enough structure, support, or sequencing to keep the challenge productive rather than overwhelming.
Abstract challenge is replacing the concrete building blocks people still need in order to understand the task at all.
desirable-difficulties.name: Desirable Difficulties

select_when: Delivery speed and reliability have to coexist, and the bottleneck is integration friction rather than lack of ideas.
Failures are being created by handoffs, untested merges, delayed feedback, brittle release steps, or poor coordination between builders and operators.
The system needs a living operating loop that shortens the distance between build, observe, diagnose, and adjust.
Local throughput gains may be degrading end-to-end service, so the work needs system-level rather than step-local reasoning.

failure_modes.mode: Maslow's Hammer (Law of the Instrument)
failure_modes.description: Maslow's-hammer style framework use can optimize the wrong delivery problem because the team mistakes a familiar pipeline story for the real system.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge whether the familiar pipeline story fits the current delivery problem; Maslow's hammer can optimize the wrong bottleneck.
failure_modes.source_quote: Maslow's Hammer (Law of the Instrument)

failure_modes.mode: Boiling the Ocean
failure_modes.description: Boiling-the-ocean analysis can delay the feedback loop that should have revealed the next operational constraint earlier.
failure_modes.mitigation: Ship the smallest feedback loop that reveals the next operational constraint; boiling-the-ocean analysis delays the signal the pipeline should provide.
failure_modes.source_quote: Boiling the Ocean

failure_modes.mode: Danger when local pipeline optimization hides system fragility
failure_modes.description: Local pipeline optimization can quietly increase rollback pain, debugging latency, or production brittleness.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit local pipeline optimizations for downstream side effects: faster deploys that increase rollback pain or debugging latency are not net improvements.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when local pipeline optimization hides system fragility

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: Which output can we measure now to detect whether faster local flow is actually degrading end-to-end reliability?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What key output can I measure now to detect failure early?

heuristics.description: Use a build-observe-diagnose-adjust loop so learning happens continuously instead of after a large release batch.
heuristics.source_quote: shortening the distance between build, observe, diagnose, and adjust

heuristics.description: Map handoffs, merge friction, and brittle release steps explicitly before optimizing tool speed or throughput numbers.
heuristics.source_quote: handoffs, untested merges, delayed feedback, brittle release steps

heuristics.description: Use the five-step sequence of goals, problems, diagnosis, design, and implementation so delivery fixes do not skip root-cause work.
heuristics.source_quote: Success requires doing all five steps well, one at a time, and in order

danger_when: Pipeline optimization is being treated as an abstract slogan without specific failure paths, rollback realities, or operator constraints.
The team is using speed metrics to hide system fragility, debugging latency, or rollback pain.
devops-and-continuous-integration.name: Devops And Continuous Integration

select_when: A strong claim needs an explicit counterclaim before the team can trust the conclusion.
The current reasoning is too one-sided, and progress depends on holding opposing perspectives long enough to produce a better synthesis.
A decision would improve if contradiction were treated as a disciplined input rather than as social friction to be suppressed.
The next useful move is to force thesis, antithesis, and synthesis instead of letting one frame dominate by momentum.

failure_modes.mode: Dialectical reasoning fails when teams mistake endless contrarianism for real synthesis work.
failure_modes.description: Dialectical reasoning fails when teams mistake endless contrarianism for real synthesis work.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require synthesis: every dialectical round must produce a concrete integration, not just another contrary view; contrarianism without synthesis is waste.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams confuse productive dialectic with endless contrarianism and never translate tension into a bounded decision or next step.

failure_modes.mode: Analysis Paralysis (The Hamlet Trap)
failure_modes.description: The model can slide into analysis paralysis when every perspective is surfaced but none is integrated into action.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision deadline that forces perspectives into a synthesized recommendation; surfacing alternatives without integrating them is analysis paralysis.
failure_modes.source_quote: Analysis Paralysis (The Hamlet Trap)

failure_modes.mode: counterintuitive and emotionally challenging
failure_modes.description: Dialectic can be emotionally avoided when the team resists confronting the weaknesses in its own position.
failure_modes.mitigation: Create psychological safety for confronting weaknesses in the team's own position; dialectic fails when the antithesis is emotionally avoided.
failure_modes.source_quote: counterintuitive and emotionally challenging

premortem_questions.description: What is the strongest antithesis to our current thesis?
premortem_questions.source_quote: thesis, antithesis, synthesis

premortem_questions.description: What decision or next step should this tension produce instead of just extending debate?
premortem_questions.source_quote: translate tension into a bounded decision or next step

premortem_questions.description: Which weakness in our own reasoning are we emotionally resisting because it threatens the current story?
premortem_questions.source_quote: the emotional part instinctively resists

heuristics.description: Use thesis, antithesis, and synthesis as a default reasoning norm for important claims.
heuristics.source_quote: The Dialectic Standard

heuristics.description: Force the team to articulate the strongest counterposition before it earns synthesis.
heuristics.source_quote: Every proposition must automatically generate its counter-proposition

heuristics.description: Translate contradiction into a bounded integration step so dialectic ends in better judgment rather than endless motion.
heuristics.source_quote: bounded decision or next step

danger_when: The team is confusing productive dialectic with endless contrarianism and never converting tension into a bounded decision.
The question now needs execution rather than another round of debate, and dialectical rigor would mostly add delay.
dialectical-reasoning.name: Dialectical Reasoning
reframing_when: The question presents a single thesis without generating its antithesis — the dialectic standard (thesis, antithesis, synthesis) has not been applied to the question itself.
The question asks 'is X true?' or 'should we do X?' without asking 'what is the strongest counter-argument to X, and what would a synthesis of both look like?'
The question is being asked in a context where conflicting perspectives exist but only one is represented in the framing — the question has already taken a side.
The question seeks a single clean answer when the situation contains genuine tensions that should be held and navigated, not resolved — the question is collapsing a dialectic into a binary.
dialectical-reasoning.reframing_move: Generate the counter-question: for any question X, ask 'what is the strongest question that contradicts or challenges X?' — then seek the synthesis question that holds both in productive tension. The WTWHTB question ('what would you have to believe?') is the operationalized version of this move.

select_when: The team is either collapsing to the first workable idea or endlessly ideating without a real selection moment.
The problem is novel enough to need broad option generation but consequential enough to require disciplined narrowing afterward.
Creative exploration and critical evaluation are both necessary, but harmful when mixed at the same time.

failure_modes.mode: Premature evaluation can anchor the team to the first promising idea and kill originality before the...
failure_modes.description: Premature evaluation can anchor the team to the first promising idea and kill originality before the search space opens.
failure_modes.mitigation: Enforce strict phase separation: no evaluation, ranking, or feasibility critique during idea generation; premature convergence kills the search space.
failure_modes.source_quote: Premature Convergence (Anti-pattern): Cutting short the divergent phase by evaluating ideas too quickly run the risk of hampering originality.

failure_modes.mode: Analysis Paralysis (Over-reliance on CT)
failure_modes.description: Endless convergent analysis can stall action and turn disciplined narrowing into paralysis.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a convergence deadline: disciplined narrowing has a time limit, after which the team must commit to action rather than continuing to refine.
failure_modes.source_quote: Analysis Paralysis (Over-reliance on CT)

failure_modes.mode: People can think they are balancing both modes while actually mixing them so badly that neither idea...
failure_modes.description: People can think they are balancing both modes while actually mixing them so badly that neither ideation nor evaluation works.
failure_modes.mitigation: Explicitly label which mode is active in each meeting segment; mixing divergent and convergent thinking invisibly ruins both.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when participants think they are being balanced but are actually switching modes so chaotically that neither ideation nor evaluation gets done well.

premortem_questions.description: Are we still generating options, or did one early idea silently become the reference point for everything else?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If a compelling solution comes to mind during the question generation phase, it can act as an anchor.

premortem_questions.description: What would change if we asked what we could do before asking what we should do?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What could you do? promotes the expansive exploration of divergent thinking. What should you do? often leads to zeroing in on the most obvious solutions.

premortem_questions.description: Have we earned the right to converge, or are we only choosing because ambiguity is uncomfortable?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The problem is novel enough to need broad option generation but consequential enough to require disciplined narrowing afterward.

heuristics.description: Separate ideation from evaluation so the group can widen possibilities before ranking them.
heuristics.source_quote: Separate Ideation and Evaluation

heuristics.description: Use How Might We framing to keep the problem open long enough for real alternatives to appear.
heuristics.source_quote: articulate the problem as a How Might We question

heuristics.description: Force a clear convergence moment after divergence so the process ends in selection rather than option hoarding.
heuristics.source_quote: teams then reconvene to narrow them down to a few scenarios to explore further

danger_when: Participants are switching modes so chaotically that neither ideation nor evaluation is being done well.
The problem already has a clear bounded answer and extra divergence would only delay execution.
divergent-vs-convergent-thinking.name: Divergent Vs Convergent Thinking

select_when: Novices or newly promoted operators are gaining responsibility in a domain where confidence can outrun actual competence.
Self-assessment needs to be compared to objective tests, external review, or observed performance instead of being taken at face value.
A team needs to separate apparent fluency or charisma from real operating skill before making a trust decision.
The risk is that people do not know enough to notice their own mistakes or blind spots.

failure_modes.mode: Individuals with limited knowledge or low competence tend to significantly overestimate their abilities.
failure_modes.description: Low-competence actors can overestimate their ability because they lack the skill required to notice their own mistakes.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use objective tests and calibration exercises rather than self-assessment; low-competence actors lack the skill to notice their own mistakes.
failure_modes.source_quote: Individuals with limited knowledge or low competence tend to significantly overestimate their abilities.

failure_modes.mode: Demonstrating slightly more knowledge can cause the audience to assume that person is a guru.
failure_modes.description: Organizations can mistake fluency, confidence, or slight knowledge advantage for real competence and give too much trust too early.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify competence through demonstrated performance in realistic conditions, not through confidence, fluency, or slight knowledge advantage.
failure_modes.source_quote: Demonstrating slightly more knowledge can cause the audience to assume that person is a guru.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the Dunning-Kruger label is used as a lazy dismissal of someone else's view.
failure_modes.description: Dunning-Kruger becomes a lazy insult when the label replaces calibration, coaching, objective tests, or evidence-bearing review.
failure_modes.mitigation: Replace the Dunning-Kruger label with concrete calibration data: coaching, evidence-bearing review, and skill tests are more useful than insults.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the Dunning-Kruger label is used as a lazy dismissal of someone else's view.

premortem_questions.description: What objective test, external review, or observable performance would falsify this person's self-assessment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Self-assessment can be compared to objective tests, external review, or observed performance instead of being taken at face value.

premortem_questions.description: Are we trusting confidence and fluency, or are we actually checking operating skill under real conditions?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Teams need to separate apparent fluency or charisma from real operating skill.

premortem_questions.description: How might status, experience, or prior success be creating the same illusion of understanding in our experts?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger when experts assume the bias belongs only to novices.

heuristics.description: Never trust self-assessment alone in a high-consequence domain.
heuristics.source_quote: The DKE is powerful in contexts where self-assessment by novices is often useless.

heuristics.description: Use calibration, external review, and performance evidence to separate real competence from overconfidence.
heuristics.source_quote: Calibration: regularly use objective instruments like quizzes, practice tests, or external review processes.

heuristics.description: Treat Dunning-Kruger as a prompt for better evidence and coaching, not as a shortcut for dismissing people.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when the Dunning-Kruger label is used as a lazy dismissal.

danger_when: The label is being used as a lazy dismissal of someone else's view instead of prompting better calibration, evidence, or coaching.
Experts are treating Dunning-Kruger as a novice-only problem and ignoring how status or past success can create the same illusion of understanding.
dunning-kruger-effect.name: Dunning Kruger Effect

select_when: An experienced team keeps reaching for the same proven frame even though the situation feels structurally different.
The first solution arrived suspiciously fast and there is reason to think expertise is blinding the group to alternatives.
A problem requires reframing, cross-domain analogies, or deliberate interruption of the dominant mental template.
People are assuming they have seen this one before without pressure-testing that assumption.

failure_modes.mode: An existing or previously learned solution prevents an individual from perceiving alternative solutions.
failure_modes.description: A familiar solution path can block better alternatives because the first successful frame becomes hard to unsee.
failure_modes.mitigation: Before committing to the first solution that works, explicitly search for at least one alternative; a familiar path can block better options.
failure_modes.source_quote: An existing or previously learned solution prevents an individual from perceiving alternative solutions.

failure_modes.mode: Expertise can become a trap when people assume they have seen this problem before and stop testing o...
failure_modes.description: Expertise can become a trap when people assume they have seen this problem before and stop testing other frames.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge the assumption that this is a known problem: test whether the current case actually matches the conditions where the old solution succeeded.
failure_modes.source_quote: Experienced problem solvers often rely on mental models that have worked previously, but these frames can be misleading for genuinely novel problems.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge.
failure_modes.description: Anti-fixation rhetoric becomes waste when teams reject all prior patterns instead of identifying which pattern is stale and why.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish stale patterns from useful ones: anti-fixation rhetoric that rejects all prior experience is as wasteful as blind repetition.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge.

premortem_questions.description: What if the reason this answer arrived so fast is that we are reusing a familiar template rather than seeing the actual structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: When a person begins thinking about a problem in one direction, that fixation can blind them to the full range of alternatives.

premortem_questions.description: What different frame, analogy, or outside-domain lens would expose a solution path we are currently missing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Avoiding the Einstellung effect means testing multiple frameworks.

premortem_questions.description: What part of our prior knowledge is genuinely useful here, and what part is just making the current case look familiar?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The danger is allowing one’s existing toolkit to dictate the solution.

heuristics.description: Interrupt the dominant template before committing when the problem feels novel despite a suspiciously familiar answer.
heuristics.source_quote: The model is most useful when an experienced team keeps reaching for the same proven frame even though the situation feels structurally different.

heuristics.description: Switch modes or step away briefly when focused effort is reinforcing the same stale path.
heuristics.source_quote: The path to overcoming the effect is to turn the focus away from the subject.

heuristics.description: Keep helpful pattern recognition, but force a fresh test of whether this case actually satisfies the old frame's assumptions.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge instead of distinguishing helpful pattern recognition from stale pattern lock-in.

danger_when: Avoid-fixation language is becoming a reason to reject all prior knowledge instead of distinguishing helpful pattern recognition from stale lock-in.
The team already has strong evidence that the familiar pattern truly fits and is using anti-fixation rhetoric to keep reopening a settled question.
einstellung-effect.name: Einstellung Effect
reframing_when: The question keeps producing variations of the same answer because the asker's expertise has installed a default frame that blocks alternative formulations — the solution arrived suspiciously fast.
The question is locked into one disciplinary lens ('this is a marketing problem', 'this is a cost problem') when cross-domain reframing would expose a structurally different problem definition.
The question assumes a known problem type ('we've seen this before') without testing whether the situation is genuinely novel and the familiar frame is misleading.

The question frames improvement as refinement of the existing approach when the real leverage comes from switching modes entirely — from focused mode to diffuse mode, from optimization to exploration.
einstellung-effect.reframing_move: Interrupt the expert default: instead of asking 'how do we improve X within the current frame?', ask 'what would we do if we had never seen this kind of problem before and had to start from the structure of the situation rather than from prior solutions?' — the de-fixation reframe forces cross-domain alternatives before expertise narrows the field.

select_when: Roles, markets, or constraints are shifting fast enough that fixed boundaries and rigid playbooks become liabilities.
A team must adapt tactics or skill deployment while preserving a stable objective and quality bar.
The environment rewards flexible deployment across functional edges rather than narrow static ownership.
A system must fit hard constraints dynamically by surfacing only what is most relevant to the current task.

failure_modes.mode: elasticity becomes shapelessness
failure_modes.description: Cross-functional stretch can become shapelessness when no one owns the critical work.
failure_modes.mitigation: Assign clear ownership for critical work: cross-functional stretch without accountability creates shapelessness.
failure_modes.source_quote: elasticity becomes shapelessness

failure_modes.mode: there is nothing for details to hang on to
failure_modes.description: Breadth without trunk-and-branch depth creates superficiality that cannot carry real decisions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Maintain trunk-and-branch depth even while building breadth; superficiality that cannot carry real decisions is not adaptability.
failure_modes.source_quote: there is nothing for details to hang on to

failure_modes.mode: if the search pulls in irrelevant or low-quality data bits
failure_modes.description: Too much context or low-quality retrieval confuses the system instead of helping it adapt to constraints.
failure_modes.mitigation: Calibrate context and retrieval quality: too much information or poorly matched material confuses rather than enables adaptation.
failure_modes.source_quote: if the search pulls in irrelevant or low-quality data bits

premortem_questions.description: What must stay invariant here, and what can safely stretch across functional or formatting boundaries?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what can stretch and what must stay invariant

premortem_questions.description: Are we using elasticity to fit hard constraints, or to disguise missing expertise or ownership?
premortem_questions.source_quote: mask missing depth

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions in our current frame make it hard to stretch into this new context honestly?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumptions embedded in my current framework might be misleading

heuristics.description: Use self-interrogation to pull out executable tactics rather than vague confidence statements.
heuristics.source_quote: Can I make a great pitch?

heuristics.description: Summarize and surface only the most relevant context when the environment imposes hard processing limits.
heuristics.source_quote: only the most relevant data

heuristics.description: Build elastic skill deployment on top of real underlying competence instead of replacing it.
heuristics.source_quote: fixed skills are punished, elastic skills are prized

danger_when: Elasticity is becoming shapelessness and stretching roles until nobody owns the critical work.
Cross-functional adaptability is being used to hide missing depth or the absence of a true expert.
elasticity.name: Elasticity

select_when: The whole-system behavior cannot be inferred by scoring the parts independently.
Interaction effects, timing, or combinations matter more than component quality alone.
Leaders need to design conditions and constraints rather than script a fixed outcome path.
A novel system is producing patterns that only become visible once multiple forces interact.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when the whole cannot be inferred from the parts alone.
failure_modes.description: Teams miss real system behavior when they judge each component separately and never inspect the interaction effects creating the whole.
failure_modes.mitigation: Inspect interaction effects between components: judging each part separately will miss the emergent behavior that matters.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when the whole cannot be inferred from the parts alone.

failure_modes.mode: Works when interaction effects matter more than component quality.
failure_modes.description: Incremental part optimization can fail because the result is being produced by relationships between parts rather than by one weak component.
failure_modes.mitigation: Optimize for the relationships and feedback loops that produce the result, not for individual components in isolation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when interaction effects matter more than component quality.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When “Let It Emerge” Becomes an Excuse for No Design.
failure_modes.description: Emergence becomes an excuse for drift when teams avoid setting the minimal structure and feedback needed for healthy pattern formation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set minimal structure and feedback mechanisms: emergence is not an excuse for drift; healthy pattern formation needs guardrails.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When “Let It Emerge” Becomes an Excuse for No Design.

premortem_questions.description: What behavior only appears when these parts interact that we would miss if we scored them one by one?
premortem_questions.source_quote: To understand emergence, one must adopt systems thinking ... determine what emerges from that interaction.

premortem_questions.description: Which conditions, constraints, or feedback loops must be designed up front so better patterns can emerge instead of chaos?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when leaders must design conditions rather than script outcomes.

premortem_questions.description: Where are we mistaking implementation flexibility for permission to improvise without a supporting structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The focus on emergence can be misapplied as 'busking it'.

heuristics.description: Map interactions before optimizing components when the system-level result looks larger than the sum of the parts.
heuristics.source_quote: Emergence ... where complex outcomes arise from the interaction of simpler, distinct parts.

heuristics.description: Design enabling conditions and learning loops instead of pretending the full path can be scripted in advance.
heuristics.source_quote: Implementation is often more emergent than amenable to a blueprint.

heuristics.description: Use emergence to explain interaction-driven novelty, not to excuse absent design discipline.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger When “Let It Emerge” Becomes an Excuse for No Design.

danger_when: Teams are using let-it-emerge language as an excuse for having no structure, constraints, or feedback loops.
The task actually requires a fixed sequential process where diagnosis, design, and execution must stay separate.
emergence.name: Emergence

select_when: The technical answer is not enough because trust, morale, conflict, or perceived fairness will shape whether the decision lands.
Stakeholder emotions carry real information about risk, resistance, motivation, or hidden constraints.
Leadership requires influencing people through difficult change without losing candor or accountability.
The problem depends on self-management, empathy, or relationship handling more than on raw analytical horsepower alone.

failure_modes.mode: The Nice Guy Trap
failure_modes.description: Emotional intelligence can be misread as niceness, leaving hard but necessary truth unsaid.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish empathy from avoidance: emotional intelligence includes delivering hard truths, not just maintaining comfort.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Nice Guy Trap

failure_modes.mode: without validating what others actually feel or need
failure_modes.description: Teams can project confidence about others' feelings without validating what is actually going on.
failure_modes.mitigation: Validate emotional reads before acting on them: projecting confidence about others' feelings without checking creates false certainty.
failure_modes.source_quote: without validating what others actually feel or need

failure_modes.mode: omitting the emotional dimension
failure_modes.description: Communication fails when emotion is omitted and only information or action is specified.
failure_modes.mitigation: Include the emotional dimension alongside information and action in every important communication; omitting it causes messages to misfire.
failure_modes.source_quote: omitting the emotional dimension

premortem_questions.description: What emotion is present here, and what do I think is causing it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Identifying the emotion and naming the cause

premortem_questions.description: Are we packaging this message for analytical review only, or for the emotional response that drives action?
premortem_questions.source_quote: create wanting

premortem_questions.description: What specific feeling do we want the audience to leave with?
premortem_questions.source_quote: emotion

heuristics.description: Use emotional granularity instead of vague labels so the real issue becomes more actionable.
heuristics.source_quote: emotional granularity

heuristics.description: Use empathy maps to surface pains, gains, and hidden motivations before acting.
heuristics.source_quote: Empathy Map

heuristics.description: Validate by naming both the emotion and the likely cause, not by generic reassurance.
heuristics.source_quote: Two-Step Validation

danger_when: Empathy is being treated as a substitute for standards, evidence, or hard conversations.
The team assumes it has read the emotional landscape correctly without validating what others actually feel or need.
emotional-intelligence.name: Emotional Intelligence

select_when: Hidden user, stakeholder, or teammate constraints will determine whether a solution is trusted, adopted, or resisted.
Observation, interviews, or reflection are being used to challenge assumptions rather than merely decorate a decision that is already set.
A message, service, or intervention must fit another person's actual mental model, language, fear, and motivation.
The work would improve if perspective-taking surfaced tensions between what people say, do, fear, and hope.

failure_modes.mode: Egocentric Bias / Inside View
failure_modes.description: A team can stay trapped in the inside view and misdefine the problem because it never really enters the stakeholder's world.
failure_modes.mitigation: Enter the stakeholder's world before defining the problem: an inside-view definition without empathic validation misses what actually matters to them.
failure_modes.source_quote: Egocentric Bias / Inside View

failure_modes.mode: The Empathy Compromise
failure_modes.description: Emotional empathy can compromise strategic clarity if feeling with someone starts overriding legitimate self-protection.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate emotional understanding from strategic capitulation: feeling with someone does not mean abandoning your own legitimate interests.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Empathy Compromise

failure_modes.mode: The Curse of Knowledge
failure_modes.description: Messages can miss entirely when expertise is communicated without enough connection to the audience's actual fears, hopes, and language.
failure_modes.mitigation: Translate expertise into the audience's language, fears, hopes, and framing; a message built from the expert's vocabulary alone will miss its target.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Curse of Knowledge

premortem_questions.description: What tensions, contradictions, or disconnects exist between what the user says and what they do?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What tensions, contradictions, or disconnects exist between what the user says and what they do?

premortem_questions.description: What do I want the audience to think, feel, and do?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What do I want the audience to Think, Feel, and Do?

premortem_questions.description: Why should I care from the audience's point of view rather than from my own expert perspective?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Why should I care?

heuristics.description: Observe, interview, and immerse before defining the problem so the user perspective can reframe the work.
heuristics.source_quote: Design Thinking (Empathize Phase)

heuristics.description: Use empathy maps to separate what people think, feel, say, do, fear, and hope before inferring what they need.
heuristics.source_quote: Empathy Maps

heuristics.description: Use empathetic reflection as a guess, not a diagnosis, so the other person can confirm or correct the read.
heuristics.source_quote: Is that right?

danger_when: Emotional identification is starting to compromise strategic clarity or crowd out the need to protect legitimate interests.
The model is being used to simulate care or intimacy without the observation, reflection, or validation work needed to make understanding real.
empathy.name: Empathy
reframing_when: The question frames the situation from a single actor's perspective, treating other stakeholders' experiences as externalities rather than as structurally relevant information that would change the problem definition.
The question conflates the means (a specific intervention) with the end (the human outcome it serves) because the decision-maker hasn't inhabited the perspective of the people affected.
The question assumes rational self-interest explains all parties' behavior when emotional, relational, or contextual factors are actually driving the dynamics.
The question treats disagreement or resistance as obstacles to be overcome rather than as signals containing information about what the frame is missing.
empathy.reframing_move: Before deciding from your own vantage point, ask: 'If I fully inhabited the perspective of the person most affected by this decision, what problem would I see?' — the empathy reframe reveals frame distortions that are invisible from the decision-maker's position.

select_when: Ownership, authorship, or incumbency may be making an asset, idea, or status quo feel more valuable than it would to an outsider.
A negotiation, divestment, or priority decision is being warped by identity, history, or control rather than objective utility alone.
The team needs to test whether attachment to what it already has is hiding opportunity cost or suppressing rational switching.
The next move should force an outside-view valuation instead of letting possession decide the price.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Ownership Logic Hides Opportunity Cost
failure_modes.description: Ownership can keep teams defending what they already have without seriously comparing it to the best available alternative today.
failure_modes.mitigation: Force a zero-base comparison: ask whether the team would acquire this asset today at its current cost if they did not already own it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Ownership Logic Hides Opportunity Cost

failure_modes.mode: Failure to Quit or Divest
failure_modes.description: Leaders can fail to quit, divest, or switch because ownership inflates the perceived value of the status quo.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build in explicit quit, divest, or switch checkpoints; ownership inflates perceived value and makes exit feel like loss even when it is gain.
failure_modes.source_quote: Failure to Quit or Divest

failure_modes.mode: Motivated Reasoning in Valuation
failure_modes.description: Motivated reasoning can justify an owned asset's value with narrative confidence rather than neutral facts.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require neutral facts — not narrative confidence — to justify holding an owned asset; motivated reasoning is the endowment effect's enforcement mechanism.
failure_modes.source_quote: Motivated Reasoning in Valuation

premortem_questions.description: If we did not already own this asset, idea, or project, how much would we pay to acquire it today?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If I did not currently own this asset, how much would I be willing to pay

premortem_questions.description: What facts, independent of our history or feeling of ownership, support this valuation?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What facts, independent of my history or feeling of ownership, support this valuation?

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions about value would prove wrong if this transaction or hold decision failed?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this transaction were to fail, what assumptions about value did I hold that proved incorrect?

heuristics.description: Ask whether it is true value or just ownership talking before defending a held asset or plan.
heuristics.source_quote: Do I really value this, or is it just the ownership talking?

heuristics.description: Anchor first to the outside view so the market or neutral benchmark disciplines the inside view.
heuristics.source_quote: Anchor to the Outside View

heuristics.description: Write the valuation or choice down in explicit terms so emotion has to compete with a fact-based plan.
heuristics.source_quote: Draft a Plan and Write Out Thoughts

danger_when: The issue is already being evaluated through a credible outside-view or neutral benchmark and additional ownership-bias framing would add little.
The model is being used as a loose story about attachment without any real effort to compare today's best available alternative.
endowment-effect.name: Endowment Effect

select_when: Behavior is being shaped more by selection pressure, incentives, fear, or repeated demands than by stated intent.
The real question is what the system is rewarding, selecting against, or making expensive over time.
A team needs to explain why fitness for survival or uptake is dominating elegance or explicit rationality.
Communication or design should fit the pressures actually acting on the audience or organization.

failure_modes.mode: selected based on maximizing survival or Darwinian fitness
failure_modes.description: A system can optimize for fitness under pressure while drifting away from truth, happiness, or long-term design quality.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the system is optimizing for fitness under pressure or for the outcome you actually want; survival and truth are not always aligned.
failure_modes.source_quote: selected based on maximizing survival or Darwinian fitness

failure_modes.mode: false pattern recognition
failure_modes.description: False pattern recognition reuses an old survival heuristic on a genuinely novel problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test old survival heuristics against the current environment; false pattern recognition reuses evolutionary shortcuts on genuinely novel problems.
failure_modes.source_quote: false pattern recognition

failure_modes.mode: this is how people are wired
failure_modes.description: Fatalism creeps in when people say this is how the system selects behavior and stop redesigning incentives.
failure_modes.mitigation: Redesign incentives rather than accepting selection pressure as fate; fatalism stops teams from engineering better outcomes.
failure_modes.source_quote: this is how people are wired

premortem_questions.description: What is this system currently rewarding, and what behavior is it selecting against even if no one admits it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what the system is rewarding, what it is selecting against

premortem_questions.description: If this plan succeeds, what second-order consequences will selection pressure create three or more steps out?
premortem_questions.source_quote: three, six, and twelve months down the line

premortem_questions.description: What would happen if we applied a different disciplinary frame instead of the first survival story that came to mind?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would happen if I applied a frame from a completely different discipline?

heuristics.description: Package decisions and communication in a way the pressure-shaped audience can actually process.
heuristics.source_quote: simple, clear, visual, novel, and non-threatening

heuristics.description: Look at what survives competition, stress, and resource constraints rather than what merely sounds right in theory.
heuristics.source_quote: systems adapt around pressure, not around ideals

heuristics.description: Use worst-case checks to keep tail-risk blindness from hiding behind base-case comfort.
heuristics.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

danger_when: Evolutionary logic is becoming fatalism and blocking redesign of incentives, guardrails, or communication.
The team is using hardwired-survival rhetoric without checking whether the current pressure structure can actually be changed.
evolutionary-pressure.name: Evolutionary Pressure

select_when: Choices involve asymmetric payoffs and intuitive judgment is likely to overweight vivid outcomes rather than weighted outcomes.
The team can estimate probabilities and consequences well enough to compare options on a common decision basis.
A decision must trade off upside, downside, and likelihood instead of reacting to whichever outcome feels most salient.

failure_modes.mode: The tendency to process information to get a desired conclusion rather than seeking objective truth.
failure_modes.description: Motivated reasoning can contaminate the probabilities or values that feed the EV calculation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the probabilities and values for motivated reasoning before computing the EV; contaminated inputs produce a precise but wrong answer.
failure_modes.source_quote: The tendency to process information to get a desired conclusion rather than seeking objective truth.

failure_modes.mode: Overestimating the probability of success for complex undertakings where each of a series of likely events must occur.
failure_modes.description: Conjunctive or planning bias can overstate the success odds of a multi-link plan.
failure_modes.mitigation: Decompose multi-link plans into independent probability estimates and multiply; conjunctive or planning bias systematically overestimates compound success.
failure_modes.source_quote: Overestimating the probability of success for complex undertakings where each of a series of likely events must occur.

failure_modes.mode: Inside-view bias can make the EV look personal and compelling instead of disciplined by the outside ...
failure_modes.description: Inside-view bias can make the EV look personal and compelling instead of disciplined by the outside view.
failure_modes.mitigation: Anchor the EV inputs to outside-view reference-class data before adjusting for case-specific details; the inside view makes the calculation feel personal rather than disciplined.
failure_modes.source_quote: Evaluating a situation only through one’s own perspective and experiences, amplifying cognitive biases and ignoring what is true of the world in general.

failure_modes.mode: Insufficient weighting of extremes hides tail outcomes that matter more than the base case.
failure_modes.description: Insufficient weighting of extremes hides tail outcomes that matter more than the base case.
failure_modes.mitigation: Simulate outcomes one or two standard deviations from the mean; tail outcomes often matter more than the base case.
failure_modes.source_quote: Placing too much confidence in the mean outcome (base case) and insufficient weight on outcomes that are one or two standard deviations from the mean.

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: ask, What would you have to believe? to spell out all implicit assumptions required to accept the favorable outcome.

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask the question, Is the worst case bad enough? to ensure low-probability, high-impact negative outcomes are sufficiently analyzed.

premortem_questions.description: What other perspectives are there as to why things turned out the way they did?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What other perspectives are there as to why things turned out the way they did?

premortem_questions.description: What sources of information could I have missed or minimized?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What sources of information could I have missed or minimized?

heuristics.description: Run sensitivity analysis to find which assumptions actually drive the payoff.
heuristics.source_quote: performing a sensitivity analysis is crucial. This involves testing inputs to uncover key drivers

heuristics.description: Use decision trees to model probabilistic choices instead of relying on flat pro-con logic.
heuristics.source_quote: Decision Trees ... are particularly useful for modeling choices that are probabilistic in nature

heuristics.description: Use scenario analysis and the knowns or unknowns matrix to surface the payoff drivers you still have not bounded.
heuristics.source_quote: scenario analysis to systematically uncover risks and assess their impact. The Knowns & Unknowns model helps categorize risks

heuristics.description: Create a business case that makes the premises and reasoning chain inspectable before commitment.
heuristics.source_quote: produce a business case, a document outlining the explicit reasoning behind a decision, laying out the premises and explaining how they lead to the conclusion

danger_when: EV math is being used to launder weak assumptions, speculative probabilities, or ethically unacceptable downside into a false aura of rationality.
The case is too assumption-fragile or cognitively overloaded for the numbers to support a trustworthy weighted comparison.
expected-value.name: Expected Value

select_when: Uncertainty can be reduced faster and cheaper through targeted tests than through longer debate, forecasting, or opinion escalation.
Each experiment can be tied to a clear hypothesis, success threshold, and downstream decision instead of being run for its own sake.
Reversible probes can inform larger irreversible commitments, reallocations, or product choices.
The team is stuck in argument about what is true, but a structured test could resolve the dispute.

failure_modes.mode: Conjunction Fallacy/Unwarranted Optimism
failure_modes.description: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
failure_modes.mitigation: Break complex experimental rollouts into independently testable steps; conjunctive optimism hides how many things must go right simultaneously.
failure_modes.source_quote: Conjunction Fallacy/Unwarranted Optimism

failure_modes.mode: Groupthink and Political Pressure
failure_modes.description: Groupthink and political pressure can keep a favored hypothesis alive even when the experiment should be disproving it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect the experiment from political pressure by pre-registering the hypothesis and the criteria for disproving it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Groupthink and Political Pressure

failure_modes.mode: Experimentation theater can produce dashboards and learning language without changing the underlying...
failure_modes.description: Experimentation theater can produce dashboards and learning language without changing the underlying decision.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require that every experiment produce a decision change, not just a dashboard update; experimentation theater creates learning language without altering the plan.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when experimentation becomes theater, generating activity, dashboards, or post-hoc learning language without changing any real decision.

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: “Is the worst case bad enough?”

premortem_questions.description: What do you think the other side doesn't get about your perspective?
premortem_questions.source_quote: “What do you think the other side doesn’t ‘get’ about your perspective?”

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What You Have to Believe" Analysis

heuristics.description: Use a one-day answer so the team always has a current hypothesis and solution path guiding what facts to gather next.
heuristics.source_quote: Hypothesis-Driven Problem Solving (The "One-Day Answer")

heuristics.description: Keep the experiment tied to a clear hypothesis and gather only enough facts to prove or disprove it.
heuristics.source_quote: collecting enough facts only to prove or disprove a hypothesis

heuristics.description: Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.
heuristics.source_quote: He stresses that these steps must be done one at a time and in order

danger_when: Experimentation has become theater, generating activity, dashboards, or post-hoc learning language without changing any real decision.
Local test wins are being overgeneralized while the team ignores system interactions, long-term effects, or behavior changes outside the test window.
experimentation.name: Experimentation

select_when: The same message, prompt, or procedure is landing very differently across experience levels and the support level may be mismatched to expertise.
Performance is dropping because experts are being slowed by redundant explanation or because novices are being under-supported.
A training, workflow, or prompt should deliberately remove supports that have become drag while retaining structure for the less experienced user.
Context volume is rising, but clarity depends more on fit to expertise than on adding still more examples or scaffolding.

failure_modes.mode: The additional material imposes redundant cognitive load.
failure_modes.description: Novice scaffolding can become drag for experts once redundant detail starts adding cognitive load instead of support.
failure_modes.mitigation: Adjust scaffolding to the learner's actual competence level; novice support becomes drag for experts once it adds redundant cognitive load.
failure_modes.source_quote: The additional material imposes redundant cognitive load.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Less Guidance Is Confused with Better Guidance.
failure_modes.description: Compression can backfire when leaders assume experienced people need less guidance even though the task or context is still novel.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify that experienced team members still need full autonomy on this specific task: compression backfires when the context or domain is novel despite their general expertise.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Less Guidance Is Confused with Better Guidance.

failure_modes.mode: Works when performance is dropping because support is mismatched to expertise.
failure_modes.description: One-size-fits-all procedures can degrade performance because the real mismatch is the support level, not the raw capability of the operator.
failure_modes.mitigation: Customize support level, not just difficulty level; one-size-fits-all procedures degrade performance because the mismatch is in guidance, not capability.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when performance is dropping because support is mismatched to expertise.

premortem_questions.description: Who is being slowed by support that has become redundant, and who is still exposed because support was removed too early?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when the same message is landing very differently across experience levels.

premortem_questions.description: What part of this prompt, training, or procedure adds load without adding guidance for the people already fluent in it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Avoid Redundancy.

premortem_questions.description: Where are we treating less guidance as better guidance even though the stakes or novelty still require renewed structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger When Less Guidance Is Confused with Better Guidance.

heuristics.description: Retune guidance by expertise level so novices get scaffolding and experts get compression, autonomy, or speed.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when training, prompts, or operating procedures must adapt as mastery rises.

heuristics.description: Strip redundant examples and context only when the task is already clear for the more experienced operator.
heuristics.source_quote: If the problem is clear to the model, avoid using excessive examples.

heuristics.description: Reintroduce structure when the task becomes new, high-stakes, or context-shifted enough that expertise alone will not cover it.
heuristics.source_quote: The model misfires when teams strip away support merely because someone is experienced, even though the task, context, or stakes are new enough to require renewed structure.

danger_when: Less guidance is being treated as automatically better even though the task, stakes, or novelty still require renewed structure.
An expert-facing simplification is being generalized to novices who still need concrete steps, examples, or explicit distinctions.
expertise-reversal-effect.name: Expertise Reversal Effect

select_when: The cost of mediocre performance is large enough that the system must distinguish robust excellence from merely acceptable output.
Evaluation has to separate signal from luck across difficult, nonlinear, or high-variance performance environments.
The goal is to improve standards, calibration, and readiness rather than reward appearances of effort.

failure_modes.mode: Evaluators can protect favored theories or people instead of using the standard to falsify their ass...
failure_modes.description: Evaluators can protect favored theories or people instead of using the standard to falsify their assumptions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use the standard to falsify assumptions, not to protect favored theories or people; evaluation integrity depends on willingness to let the data disappoint.
failure_modes.source_quote: extreme-performance-evaluation conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when evaluators protect favored hypotheses instead of falsifying them.

failure_modes.mode: Score maximization can push teams too close to operating limits when the evaluation logic ignores ne...
failure_modes.description: Score maximization can push teams too close to operating limits when the evaluation logic ignores necessary buffers.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build buffers into the evaluation logic so score-maximization does not push the team past safe operating limits.
failure_modes.source_quote: extreme-performance-evaluation conflicts with margin-of-safety (margin of safety) when score-maximization pushes teams to run too close to operational limits.

failure_modes.mode: Extreme standards can dissolve into fear or noise when context, support, and measurement quality do ...
failure_modes.description: Extreme standards can dissolve into fear or noise when context, support, and measurement quality do not match the standard being imposed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Match context, support, and measurement quality to the standard being imposed; extreme standards dissolve into fear when the infrastructure does not support them.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when extreme standards are imposed without matching context, support, or measurement quality, turning evaluation into noise, fear, or vanity metrics.

premortem_questions.description: How much of the observed performance is skill signal, and how much is variance or luck?
premortem_questions.source_quote: separate signal from luck across difficult, nonlinear, or high-variance performance environments

premortem_questions.description: What safety margin are we quietly sacrificing in order to maximize the score?
premortem_questions.source_quote: run too close to operational limits

premortem_questions.description: Would this standard still be useful if the team did not know which candidate we already favored?
premortem_questions.source_quote: protect favored hypotheses instead of falsifying them

heuristics.description: Use the evaluation to distinguish robust excellence from acceptable noise, not to reward surface effort.
heuristics.source_quote: distinguish robust excellence from merely acceptable output

heuristics.description: Check whether the standard has enough measurement quality and support behind it to be interpretable.
heuristics.source_quote: matching context, support, or measurement quality

heuristics.description: Keep a margin-of-safety lens in the review so extreme performance does not become reckless performance.
heuristics.source_quote: margin of safety

danger_when: Extreme standards are being imposed without matching context, support, or measurement quality.
The review is really a vanity-metric exercise dressed up as rigor.
extreme-performance-evaluation.name: Extreme Performance Evaluation

select_when: A decision needs directional clarity, bounded ranges, or operating thresholds more than an illusion of exact certainty.
Extra decimal places, caveats, or scenario branches are consuming attention without changing the decision that would actually be made.
The audience needs to act, align, or prioritize quickly and will be harmed more by cluttered precision than by a well-calibrated approximation.
The current explanation is accurate enough to sound rigorous but too overloaded to be useful for prediction, choice, or coordination.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can use simplicity to hide uncertainty and present a rounded answer as if the underlying varia...
failure_modes.description: Teams can use simplicity to hide uncertainty and present a rounded answer as if the underlying variance no longer matters.
failure_modes.mitigation: Label the range of uncertainty when presenting simplified numbers; rounded answers that hide underlying variance create false confidence.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams use simplicity to hide uncertainty, skip the underlying analysis, or present a rounded answer as if the underlying variance no longer matters.

failure_modes.mode: short-term memory is a limited resource
failure_modes.description: Excessive detail can overload cognition and distract from the decision-relevant structure of the problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Remove detail that does not change the decision; excessive precision overloads cognition and distracts from the decision-relevant structure.
failure_modes.source_quote: short-term memory is a limited resource

failure_modes.mode: Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or...
failure_modes.description: Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.
failure_modes.mitigation: Ensure every simplification still guides prediction, action, or alignment; vague slogans that remove too much structure are as dangerous as false precision.
failure_modes.source_quote: Simplifying too far can result in mission statements or explanations that are abstract and ambiguous to the point of being meaningless.

premortem_questions.description: If this was cut, would anything be lost?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this was cut, would anything be lost?

premortem_questions.description: Does this create confusion?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Does this create confusion?

premortem_questions.description: What's the one-day answer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What’s the one-day answer?

heuristics.description: Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.
heuristics.source_quote: gather only enough facts to prove or disprove a hypothesis or support an analysis

heuristics.description: Use bounded approximations when they preserve the decision while removing clutter that would otherwise slow alignment.
heuristics.source_quote: Decision-makers should understand the concept of "by-and-large" and use approximations

heuristics.description: Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
heuristics.source_quote: If information isn't explicitly helping to achieve the purpose of the explanation, it stays out.

danger_when: Simplicity is being used to hide uncertainty, skip the underlying analysis, or present a rounded answer as if the variance no longer matters.
The model is simplifying so far that the result becomes vague, slogan-like, or detached from the assumptions that make it usable.
false-precision-avoidance.name: False Precision Avoidance

select_when: The team has a strong thesis and needs a disciplined way to prove it wrong before committing.
Credibility depends on stating what evidence would reverse the recommendation instead of projecting certainty.
The real risk is protecting a belief from challenge rather than discovering whether it survives challenge.
A hypothesis is already guiding analysis, so the next useful move is to define the disconfirming test rather than gather more congenial evidence.

failure_modes.mode: The Ego Barrier
failure_modes.description: The need to be right can overpower the search for truth and block disconfirming evidence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define what evidence would disprove the hypothesis before seeking confirmation; the need to be right is the enemy of the search for truth.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Ego Barrier

failure_modes.mode: Torturing the Facts
failure_modes.description: A team can torture the facts until they support the favored hypothesis instead of treating the hypothesis as provisional.
failure_modes.mitigation: Treat the hypothesis as provisional: if the facts have to be tortured to fit, the hypothesis should change, not the facts.
failure_modes.source_quote: Torturing the Facts

failure_modes.mode: Systemic Inertia (Groupthink)
failure_modes.description: Groupthink or systemic inertia can stop the team from challenging the leading belief even when the evidence is turning against it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Create explicit challenge mechanisms that override groupthink and systemic inertia when the evidence is turning against the leading belief.
failure_modes.source_quote: Systemic Inertia (Groupthink)

premortem_questions.description: What single fact, if true, would cause us to abandon this hypothesis immediately?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What single fact, if true, would cause us to abandon this hypothesis immediately?

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: By how much would your assumptions need to change for your conclusion to be wrong?
premortem_questions.source_quote: By how much would your assumptions need to change... for your conclusion to be wrong?

heuristics.description: Use strong, testable hypotheses because they are easier to challenge and pressure test than vague claims.
heuristics.source_quote: Formulating Strong, Testable Hypotheses

heuristics.description: Institutionalize a dialectic standard so every thesis meets an antithesis before synthesis.
heuristics.source_quote: The Dialectic Standard (Team Process)

heuristics.description: Stress-test the assumptions underneath the recommendation rather than only the recommendation itself.
heuristics.source_quote: Sensitivity Analysis and Reverse Questions

danger_when: The work is still so vague or underscoped that any initial hypothesis would be decorative rather than testable.
Organizational politics make the hypothesis untouchable, so the language of falsification would only create theater instead of real reversal conditions.
falsifiability.name: Falsifiability
reframing_when: The question seeks confirmation of an existing belief without defining what evidence would disprove it — the question is designed to succeed rather than to learn.
The question asks 'is X true?' without asking 'what would prove X false?' — the falsification frame is missing.
The question's implicit assumptions have not been spelled out concretely enough to be tested — the 'what would you have to believe?' analysis has not been done.
