premortem_questions.source_quote: focus on what you can control and not on what you can't

premortem_questions.description: What am I at least one percent responsible for in this outcome?
premortem_questions.source_quote: 1% responsible

premortem_questions.description: Did we separate diagnosis from action, or are we moving too fast to feel in control?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Deliberate, Then Act

heuristics.description: Concentrate effort on controllable levers and stop spending attention on the uncontrollable.
heuristics.source_quote: focus on what you can control

heuristics.description: Use a structured goal-problem-diagnosis-design-do loop to turn agency into action.
heuristics.source_quote: The 5-Step Process

heuristics.description: Distinguish objective facts from emotions, wishes, and beliefs before acting.
heuristics.source_quote: objective facts and personal emotions, beliefs, wishes, and desires

danger_when: Agency language is being used to deny structural constraints, missing resources, or system incentives.
Self-accountability is sliding into solitary heroics or self-blame instead of asking for help or redesign.
internal-locus-of-control.name: Internal Locus Of Control

select_when: No party can simply impose its will without triggering response, retaliation, or coalition effects.
The decision is not only what deal is efficient, but how positions, concessions, credibility, and sequencing will be interpreted by multiple audiences.
Technical correctness alone will not move the system unless interests, legitimacy, face, and communication are managed together.

failure_modes.mode: A negotiation can look efficient on paper while failing because positions, concessions, and credibil...
failure_modes.description: A negotiation can look efficient on paper while failing because positions, concessions, and credibility are interpreted differently by different audiences.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map how positions, concessions, and credibility are likely to be interpreted by the other side, not just how they are intended.
failure_modes.source_quote: The model has high leverage when the decision is not just what deal is efficient, but how positions, concessions, credibility, and sequencing will be interpreted by multiple audiences.

failure_modes.mode: Coalition narratives can filter out disconfirming intelligence before it affects the negotiating pos...
failure_modes.description: Coalition narratives can filter out disconfirming intelligence before it affects the negotiating posture.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check coalition narratives against disconfirming intelligence before they harden into the negotiating posture.
failure_modes.source_quote: international-negotiation-and-diplomacy-models conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when coalition narratives filter out disconfirming intelligence.

failure_modes.mode: Technical correctness can fail to move the system if interests, legitimacy, or face have not been ha...
failure_modes.description: Technical correctness can fail to move the system if interests, legitimacy, or face have not been handled.
failure_modes.mitigation: Address interests, legitimacy, and face alongside technical correctness; correct proposals fail when relational dimensions are ignored.
failure_modes.source_quote: It is especially useful when technical correctness alone will not move the system unless interests, face, legitimacy, and communication are managed together.

premortem_questions.description: How will each relevant audience interpret this concession or sequence step differently?
premortem_questions.source_quote: positions, concessions, credibility, and sequencing will be interpreted by multiple audiences

premortem_questions.description: What politically convenient story are we accepting because it fits the coalition narrative?
premortem_questions.source_quote: coalition narratives filter out disconfirming intelligence

premortem_questions.description: What fact-grounded option are we dropping because signaling pressure makes it harder to say aloud?
premortem_questions.source_quote: political signaling pressures displace fact-grounded option analysis

heuristics.description: Model the negotiation as a multi-audience interpretation problem, not just as a single efficient bargain.
heuristics.source_quote: multiple audiences

heuristics.description: Sequence concessions with explicit attention to credibility, legitimacy, and retaliation risk.
heuristics.source_quote: positions, concessions, credibility, and sequencing

heuristics.description: Force disconfirming intelligence into the posture review before public narratives harden.
heuristics.source_quote: filter out disconfirming intelligence

danger_when: Political signaling pressure is displacing fact-grounded option analysis.
The problem is really a simple bilateral transaction and diplomatic theater would overcomplicate it.
international-negotiation-and-diplomacy-.name: International Negotiation And Diplomacy Models

select_when: The cost of a mistake is catastrophic, making the avoidance of stupidity a far more profitable strategy than the pursuit of brilliance.
The team is planning forward without having mapped the failure landscape — paths to ruin are identifiable even when the path to success is unclear.
A plan, strategy, or assumption needs stress-testing before committing resources, and stepping from 'how do we win' to 'how do we ensure we don't lose' would limit downside risk.
First-conclusion bias is locking the team into a favored hypothesis and no one has tried to disprove it from the opposite direction.

failure_modes.mode: A practitioner who only identifies what to avoid—without subsequently taking positive action—will suffer from paralysis.
failure_modes.description: Inversion without action leads to paralysis when teams map the failure landscape exhaustively but never commit to the positive moves required to build something.
failure_modes.mitigation: Pair obstacle removal with positive force augmentation (Lewin): every inverse finding must name both what to avoid and what forward action it unblocks.
failure_modes.source_quote: A practitioner who only identifies what to avoid—without subsequently taking positive action—will suffer from paralysis.

failure_modes.mode: Straw-man inverse analysis gives false confidence when easily dismissed failure scenarios substitute...
failure_modes.description: Straw-man inverse analysis gives false confidence when easily dismissed failure scenarios substitute for genuinely uncomfortable ones that threaten the preferred plan.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use the Dr. Evil Game: imagine you are an evil genius whose sole mission is to sabotage this project — if the team cannot generate uncomfortable answers, the exercise is too shallow.
failure_modes.source_quote: if inversion is applied without genuine intellectual humility, a person might just generate "straw man" negative scenarios that they easily avoid

failure_modes.mode: The checklist delusion creates false safety when teams identify specific failure modes, implement sa...
failure_modes.description: The checklist delusion creates false safety when teams identify specific failure modes, implement safeguards, and falsely believe the system is now foolproof — blinding them to unknown unknowns.
failure_modes.mitigation: Run controlled experiments to see how the system actually breaks in reality; inverse analysis identifies known failure modes, but unknown unknowns require real-world contact.
failure_modes.source_quote: A user might identify the five ways a project could fail, implement safeguards for those five things, and falsely believe the system is now foolproof, blinding them to Unknown Unknowns

failure_modes.mode: Fundamental attribution error in failure analysis makes teams attribute hypothetical failure to exte...
failure_modes.description: Fundamental attribution error in failure analysis makes teams attribute hypothetical failure to external uncontrollable circumstances rather than internal controllable decisions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Apply double-loop learning: question the underlying assumptions and policies that made the failure mode possible, not just the surface action that would trigger it.
failure_modes.source_quote: teams might incorrectly attribute hypothetical failure to external, uncontrollable circumstances rather than looking at internal, controllable inputs

premortem_questions.description: If our failure was entirely our own fault — not market conditions, competitors, or bad luck — what internal decision or blind spot destroyed us?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "If our failure was entirely our own fault—and not due to market conditions, competitors, or bad luck—what internal decision or blind spot destroyed us?"

premortem_questions.description: Which of our inverse scenarios, if true, would require us to abandon our current plan entirely — and have we taken that scenario seriously?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Which of our inverse scenarios, if true, would require us to abandon our current plan entirely — and have we taken that scenario seriously?"

premortem_questions.description: What failure mode are we most confident cannot happen — and what would we do if it happened tomorrow?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What failure mode are we most confident cannot happen — and what would we do if it happened tomorrow?"

heuristics.description: Define the anti-goal first: ask what you don't want to achieve, what causes it, and how to avoid it — the negative formulation often reveals simpler paths than forward planning.
heuristics.source_quote: Rather than setting direct goals, establish "anti-goals." Ask yourself: "What don't I want to achieve? What causes the non-goal? How can I avoid that?"

heuristics.description: Focus on removing obstacles rather than pushing harder on supporting forces — removing friction is frequently more effective and less costly than adding propulsion.
heuristics.source_quote: instead of just pushing harder on the supporting forces, you focus on removing the obstacles (the impeding forces)

heuristics.description: Actively seek to disprove your own hypothesis before defending it — invert confirmation bias by asking where the disconfirming evidence lives.
heuristics.source_quote: rather than looking for evidence to confirm it, use inversion to actively seek ways to disprove your own theory

danger_when: Inversion is being used as a substitute for execution — identifying what to avoid without subsequently taking positive action leads to paralysis, not progress.
The team is generating easily dismissed straw-man failure scenarios rather than genuinely uncomfortable inverse analyses that threaten the preferred plan.
inversion.name: Inversion
reframing_when: The question assumes a positive goal without examining what would guarantee failure — flipping from 'how do we achieve X' to 'what would ensure we fail at X' changes the answer set.
The question takes a forward-only framing where the path to success is unclear but paths to ruin are identifiable and more tractable.
The question inherits a commitment or continuation baseline without asking what conditions would make abandoning the current path the obvious move.
The question asks how to optimize or improve without first asking what to stop doing — addition-by-subtraction is being skipped.
inversion.reframing_move: Flip the question: instead of 'how do we achieve X', ask 'what would guarantee we fail at X, and are any of those conditions already present?' — the negative formulation surfaces specific, tractable interventions that the positive formulation obscures.

select_when: The first version is unlikely to be correct, so the cheapest path to quality is repeated prototype-test-learn cycles.
Uncertainty can be reduced faster through bounded learning loops than through one long round of upfront planning.
A team needs a way to keep revising a hypothesis, design, or solution path as new evidence accumulates.
The work is drifting toward static planning even though progress depends on comparing each version against the last one.

failure_modes.mode: Iteration can become endless refinement when there are no stop rules or commitment thresholds.
failure_modes.description: Iteration can become endless refinement when there are no stop rules or commitment thresholds.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define stop rules and commitment thresholds before starting: iteration without an exit condition becomes endless refinement.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when iteration continues without stop rules, success thresholds, or a decision about what evidence is strong enough to commit.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can polish local details while avoiding the need to reset the underlying problem framing or hy...
failure_modes.description: Teams can polish local details while avoiding the need to reset the underlying problem framing or hypothesis.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the team is polishing details within the current frame or avoiding the harder work of resetting the framing or hypothesis.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams keep polishing local details while avoiding a reset on the underlying problem framing, hypothesis, or strategy.

failure_modes.mode: The human inclination is to jump into analysis without being perfectly clear about the desired output.
failure_modes.description: Premature analysis can turn iteration into inefficient churn because the team starts exploring before defining the real output.
failure_modes.mitigation: Clarify the real output before starting the cycle; premature iteration without a defined target produces churn, not progress.
failure_modes.source_quote: The human inclination is to jump into analysis without being perfectly clear about the desired output.

premortem_questions.description: What's the one-day answer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What's the one-day answer?

premortem_questions.description: If we had to stop the project right now, how would we communicate a coherent solution?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How would we communicate a coherent solution if we had to stop the project right now?

premortem_questions.description: Are we currently diagnosing the root cause, or are we jumping ahead to design the solution?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are we currently diagnosing the root cause, or are we jumping ahead to design the solution?

heuristics.description: Use hypothesis-driven cycles that explicitly revise the leading explanation as evidence accumulates.
heuristics.source_quote: This is a core consulting method where teams establish an initial hypothesis and then continuously gather facts and perform analyses to prove or disprove it.

heuristics.description: Force periodic synthesis so even partial learning becomes a usable next-step plan rather than hidden work in progress.
heuristics.source_quote: This ongoing synthesis forces continuous calibration, ensuring the current level of confidence accurately reflects the work completed, not just the final goal.

heuristics.description: Keep each loop bounded by a clear learning objective and a way to compare the next version against the last one.
heuristics.source_quote: Works best when each loop has a clear learning objective, bounded scope, and a way to compare the next version against the last one.

danger_when: The team is iterating without stop rules, success thresholds, or a decision about what evidence is strong enough to commit.
Local polishing is continuing even though the underlying problem framing or hypothesis likely needs a reset.
iteration.name: Iteration

select_when: Customers are adopting, switching, or abandoning solutions for reasons that feature comparisons alone do not explain.
The team needs to clarify the real progress the user is trying to make before choosing product scope, messaging, or go-to-market.
A roadmap is organized around attributes or internal categories even though the real lift depends on understanding the outcome being hired for.
The next move should separate the functional job from surface wants, channel feedback, or the team's preferred solution story.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when teams call every preference a job
failure_modes.description: Teams can call every preference a job and skip the work of separating true functional progress from surface wants or channel noise.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate true functional progress from surface preferences: calling every want a job inflates the framework beyond its utility.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams call every preference a job

failure_modes.mode: Superficial Job Definition
failure_modes.description: Analysis can stop at the obvious functional task and miss the deeper emotional or social job, leading to the wrong solution.
failure_modes.mitigation: Probe for the emotional and social dimensions behind the obvious functional task; stopping at the surface job leads to the wrong solution.
failure_modes.source_quote: Superficial Job Definition

failure_modes.mode: Solving the Wrong Problem
failure_modes.description: The team can solve the wrong problem by backing into the job from a preferred solution instead of from objective customer reality.
failure_modes.mitigation: Start from objective customer reality, not from a preferred solution; backing into the job from a product concept is motivated reasoning.
failure_modes.source_quote: Solving the Wrong Problem

premortem_questions.description: What real progress is the user trying to make that feature comparisons alone are failing to explain?
premortem_questions.source_quote: real progress the user is trying to make

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions are we making about the job that would need to be true for this framing to hold up?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If we ask why repeatedly, do we still land on the same job or do we uncover a deeper motivation?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Five Whys

heuristics.description: Define the job in terms of the outcome being hired for, not the product attribute being sold.
heuristics.source_quote: People don't want to buy a quarter-inch drill. They want a quarter-inch hole

heuristics.description: State the goal in ten words or less so the team cannot hide behind a feature-heavy story.
heuristics.source_quote: ten words or less

heuristics.description: Use plain-language explanation before acting so the job stays clear and decision-relevant.
heuristics.source_quote: Feynman Technique

danger_when: Teams are calling every preference a job and skipping the discipline of distinguishing true progress from surface wants or internal strategy wishes.
The model is being over-applied as a hammer when the real issue is not user progress but another structural problem outside the customer-job frame.
jobs-to-be-done.name: Jobs To Be Done
reframing_when: The question asks about a solution or product feature when the real question is about the outcome the user is trying to achieve — the drill is being discussed instead of the hole.
The question confuses the means (a specific tool, process, or approach) with the end (the progress the person is trying to make) — solution-space is being explored before problem-space is defined.
The question assumes the current framing of the problem is correct without checking whether the customer, stakeholder, or user would describe their need the same way.
The question focuses on functional requirements without surfacing the emotional or social job that is actually driving the decision.
jobs-to-be-done.reframing_move: Shift from solution-space to problem-space: instead of 'how do we improve X?', ask 'what progress is the person actually trying to make, and is X even the right thing to improve?' — the quarter-inch-hole reframe forces the question back to the job being hired for.

select_when: Blind spots are materially affecting coordination, trust, or self-assessment quality.
Better execution depends on turning feedback and disclosure into specific behavior change instead of vague openness.
A team needs structured self-awareness about what others can see that it cannot yet see for itself.
The work would improve if outside-view input were used to reduce inside-view overconfidence and repeated misdefinition.

failure_modes.mode: Blind Spot Intolerance
failure_modes.description: Blind spots persist when people resist the outside view because the feedback threatens ego or self-image.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect the feedback channel from ego defense: blind spots persist when the outside view is resisted because it threatens self-image.
failure_modes.source_quote: Blind Spot Intolerance

failure_modes.mode: The Illusion of Knowing
failure_modes.description: Teams can mistake fluency with reflection language for actual self-knowledge and calibration.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test self-knowledge claims with behavioral evidence; fluency with reflection language is not the same as actual calibration.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Illusion of Knowing

failure_modes.mode: The tool requires specific, honest input.
failure_modes.description: The process collapses when feedback and disclosure are too generic, socially safe, or judgmental to reduce the real blind spot.
failure_modes.mitigation: Make feedback concrete, specific, and behaviorally grounded; generic or socially safe exchanges do not reduce the real blind spot.
failure_modes.source_quote: The tool requires specific, honest input.

premortem_questions.description: What do others know about our situation that we are refusing to acknowledge?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What do others know about our situation that we are refusing to acknowledge?

premortem_questions.description: What evidence outside our own team would unambiguously resolve this issue?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What evidence, external to our team, would unambiguously resolve this issue?

premortem_questions.description: If we failed, what assumptions about our team dynamics or external environment were proven false?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If we failed, what specific assumptions about our external environment/team dynamics were proven false?

heuristics.description: Seek descriptive feedback that reveals how others see your actions instead of asking only for abstract approval.
heuristics.source_quote: The act of receiving feedback is emphasized as a vital process in cooperation.

heuristics.description: Use self-disclosure to increase transparency where it will actually improve cooperation rather than just image management.
heuristics.source_quote: Increasing Transparency to Enable Cooperation

heuristics.description: Combine feedback uptake with calibration methods so reflection translates into better judgment rather than more self-storytelling.
heuristics.source_quote: Use Calibration techniques

danger_when: Disclosure is becoming performance theater or socially safe sharing rather than the specific feedback and self-revelation needed to reduce the real blind spot.
The model is being used as a standalone substitute for evidence, calibration, or problem-definition discipline.
johari-window.name: Johari Window

select_when: Familiar frames are producing stale options because hidden assumptions remain unchallenged.
The blockage is conceptual rather than effort-based, so more analysis inside the current frame is unlikely to help.
Divergence should come before convergence so the team can widen the set of possible options first.
A problem needs reframing or deliberate interruption of the dominant mental template before commitment.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when familiar frames are producing stale options.
failure_modes.description: Teams can keep producing cleaner versions of the same answer when familiar frames remain unchallenged.
failure_modes.mitigation: Explicitly challenge the current frame before refining within it; cleaner versions of the same answer are not lateral thinking.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when familiar frames are producing stale options.

failure_modes.mode: Works when the blockage is conceptual, not effort-based.
failure_modes.description: More analysis inside the current frame can waste time when the real blockage is conceptual rather than effort-based.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish effort from insight: more analysis inside the current frame is wasted when the real blockage is conceptual, not analytical.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when the blockage is conceptual, not effort-based.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge.
failure_modes.description: Avoiding fixation can become overcorrection if the team rejects all prior knowledge instead of just interrupting stale lock-in.
failure_modes.mitigation: Interrupt stale lock-in without rejecting all prior knowledge; overcorrection that discards useful patterns is as wasteful as fixation.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the team treats avoid fixation as a reason to reject all prior knowledge.

premortem_questions.description: What assumption is keeping us inside the current frame even though it is no longer producing good options?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when familiar frames are producing stale options.

premortem_questions.description: If we reframed the implicit problem as a new question, what option space would reopen?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Generative Questioning (HMWs)

premortem_questions.description: Are we widening possibilities before narrowing, or are we converging too early out of habit?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when divergence comes before convergence.

heuristics.description: Interrupt the dominant template early enough that alternative frames actually get airtime.
heuristics.source_quote: Works when the blockage is conceptual, not effort-based.

heuristics.description: Use reframed questions to prevent the first solution from anchoring the whole session.
heuristics.source_quote: If a solution arises prematurely, the playbook dictates reframing the implicit problem as a new HMW question.

heuristics.description: Delay convergence until the team has genuinely explored unconventional options.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when divergence comes before convergence.

danger_when: Avoiding fixation is being used as an excuse to reject all prior knowledge instead of separating useful pattern recognition from stale lock-in.
The team is generating novelty without any path back to execution or selection.
lateral-thinking.name: Lateral Thinking
reframing_when: The question keeps producing cleaner versions of the same answer because hidden assumptions in the question's frame remain unchallenged — the blockage is conceptual, not effort-based.
The question is locked into a single disciplinary lens when the problem lives at the intersection of multiple fields — the 'if all you have is a hammer' pattern is active.
The question asks 'how do we fix X?' when the more productive question is 'How Might We think about X differently?' — divergence is needed before convergence.
The question assumes the current reality is the only possible frame when a thought experiment ('what would it look like if...?') would open a fundamentally different option space.
lateral-thinking.reframing_move: Break the frame by asking the question from outside its own discipline: instead of refining the question within its current frame, apply SCAMPER (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Minimize, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse) or ask 'what would need to be true for a completely different approach to work?' — the Airbnb reframe that changed the venture capital conversation.

select_when: A problem spans several causal layers and a single disciplinary frame keeps missing incentives, trade-offs, or second-order effects.
The team has many facts but they do not yet hang together into a usable theory of what is happening.
A recommendation needs multiple lenses checked against each other before committing to a single explanation.
Blind spots are likely because the current approach is trapped inside one specialist vocabulary.

failure_modes.mode: collect model names as intellectual decoration
failure_modes.description: Collecting model names as intellectual decoration without concrete tests or comparisons leaves the latticework useless in practice.
failure_modes.mitigation: Apply each model to a concrete case before collecting it; intellectual decoration without tested comparisons is useless in practice.
failure_modes.source_quote: collect model names as intellectual decoration

failure_modes.mode: rich models in one area but deficiencies in others
failure_modes.description: Relying on one specialist frame creates blind spots when the real problem crosses incentives, systems, and psychology at once.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require at least two frames from different disciplines before committing to a diagnosis; single-frame reliance creates predictable blind spots.
failure_modes.source_quote: rich models in one area but deficiencies in others

failure_modes.mode: overloading the mind with too many models
failure_modes.description: Overloading the mind with too many models or too much complexity can impair clear thinking and slow action.
failure_modes.mitigation: Limit active model count to those relevant to the current decision; overloading the mind with models impairs clear thinking and slows action.
failure_modes.source_quote: overloading the mind with too many models

premortem_questions.description: What frame are we currently using, and what assumptions are embedded in it that might break on this problem?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What frame am I currently using, and what assumptions are implicit in it?

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe to accept the opposite thesis, and what facts would ruin our current hypothesis?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe to accept the opposite thesis?

premortem_questions.description: Which second- or third-order consequences are we missing because we are still viewing the problem through one lens?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What are the consequences of this decision three or more steps out?

heuristics.description: Test several cuts at the problem using models from different disciplines before committing to one explanatory frame.
heuristics.source_quote: test several cuts at a problem using frames from different disciplines

heuristics.description: Use the scientific method to generate a one-day answer and then prune dead-end analysis aggressively.
heuristics.source_quote: The Scientific Method and Hypothesis-Driven Approach

heuristics.description: Use inversion and anti-goals to avoid stupidity before chasing brilliance.
heuristics.source_quote: avoid stupidity

danger_when: The team is collecting model names as decoration but not translating them into concrete tests, comparisons, or decision pressure.
The problem is narrow enough that disciplined use of one strong model already explains the mechanism and action path.
latticework-of-mental-models.name: Latticework Of Mental Models
reframing_when: The question is being analyzed through a single disciplinary lens when the situation sits at the intersection of multiple domains — the frame is locked to one model when a lattice of models would reveal cross-domain structure.
The question keeps producing the same class of answers because the same mental model is being applied repeatedly — the model is a hammer and every problem looks like a nail.
The question treats its own framing methodology as transparent rather than as one of many possible lenses — the meta-question 'which models am I using to frame this?' has not been asked.

The question would benefit from triangulation — applying 2-3 models from different disciplines and seeing where they agree and where they conflict, using disagreement as a signal about the problem's structure.
latticework-of-mental-models.reframing_move: Before settling on one analytical frame, ask: 'What would this problem look like through 2-3 models from entirely different disciplines, and where do those models disagree about what matters?' — the latticework reframe uses cross-model disagreement as a structural signal.

select_when: A decision is being distorted by anecdotes, vivid stories, or tiny samples when population logic should dominate.
Repeated observations can reveal the signal beneath noise better than a single memorable case can.
Base rates, frequency, or convergence matter more than the intuitiveness of one representative example.
The team is mistaking a noisy short run for proof about the underlying process.

failure_modes.mode: The Law of Small Numbers Bias
failure_modes.description: Law-of-small-numbers bias makes tiny samples look more representative than they really are.
failure_modes.mitigation: Flag small-sample conclusions explicitly: tiny samples are far more volatile than intuition suggests.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Law of Small Numbers Bias

failure_modes.mode: Exaggerated Faith in Scraps
failure_modes.description: Exaggerated faith in scraps can turn a few observations into hasty confident judgment.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require a stated sample size and confidence interval before treating any observation as a reliable pattern.
failure_modes.source_quote: Exaggerated Faith in Scraps

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Large-N Logic Is Applied to the Wrong Population
failure_modes.description: Large-N logic can mislead when the sample is biased or drawn from the wrong population.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the sample is drawn from the relevant population; large-N logic misleads when the sample is biased or mismatched.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Large-N Logic Is Applied to the Wrong Population

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: Are we letting a vivid story stand in for the population-level evidence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The Availability Heuristic encourages reliance on a small, biased sample of experiences

premortem_questions.description: Have we disaggregated the conjunctive plan enough to see where the overall probability actually collapses?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Systematic Disaggregation

heuristics.description: Use probabilistic thinking so fair bets and risk estimates reflect frequencies instead of vivid anecdotes.
heuristics.source_quote: Probabilistic Thinking

heuristics.description: Apply reference class forecasting before arguing that this case is uniquely special.
heuristics.source_quote: Reference Class Forecasting

heuristics.description: Use simulations to inspect the full distribution instead of anchoring only on the mean outcome.
heuristics.source_quote: Use Simulations

danger_when: The sample is biased, structurally mismatched, or drawn from the wrong population, so large-N confidence would be fake reassurance.
Mean outcomes are being used as an excuse to ignore tail risk, distribution shape, or conjunctive failure structure.
law-of-large-numbers.name: Law Of Large Numbers

select_when: A product or market hypothesis is still fragile enough that learning speed matters more than polished completeness.
The team can scope experiments narrowly, measure them honestly, and use the result to continue, pivot, or kill a path.
The work is at risk of wasting effort because assumptions about demand or fit have not been validated against real behavior.
A beachhead, foothold, or one-day-answer framing would improve focus compared with broad undifferentiated ambition.

failure_modes.mode: The Othello Fault (Jumping to Conclusion)
failure_modes.description: Jumping to conclusions can turn speed into the Othello fault where the team moves fast on the wrong framing.
failure_modes.mitigation: Slow down enough to define the framing before moving fast; speed on the wrong hypothesis is the Othello fault.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Othello Fault (Jumping to Conclusion)

failure_modes.mode: Danger when teams call any customer contact or usage blip validated learning
failure_modes.description: Vanity signals can be misread as validated learning even when they are disconnected from genuine adoption or fit.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish validated learning from vanity metrics; signals that feel positive but are disconnected from real adoption are waste.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams call any customer contact or usage blip validated learning

failure_modes.mode: Maslow's Hammer / Availability Heuristic
failure_modes.description: Availability-style hammer use can lock the team into an MVP pattern that no longer fits the real problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check whether the MVP pattern still fits the current problem; availability-style reuse of the lean playbook can lock the team into a format that no longer serves.
failure_modes.source_quote: Maslow's Hammer / Availability Heuristic

premortem_questions.description: What is the one-day answer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: the one-day answer

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What You Have to Believe

premortem_questions.description: Which assumptions about demand, fit, or behavior would force a pivot rather than a continuation?
premortem_questions.source_quote: used to trigger real continuation, pivot, or kill decisions

heuristics.description: Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the current product hypothesis instead of boiling the ocean.
heuristics.source_quote: only enough facts to prove or disprove the hypothesis

heuristics.description: Define the beachhead clearly so the team bites off a foothold before chasing the larger vision.
heuristics.source_quote: define a foothold or beachhead

heuristics.description: Use empathy maps or journey evidence to make the measure phase about real users rather than internal guesses.
heuristics.source_quote: user empathy map or user journey map

danger_when: Weak usage blips or vanity metrics are being mislabeled as validated learning.
The domain has compliance, safety, or trust thresholds that cannot be responsibly approximated by a thin MVP shortcut.
lean-startup-methodology.name: Lean Startup Methodology

select_when: Performance is expected to improve through repetition, feedback, and chunked skill-building rather than one-off inspiration.
Leaders need to distinguish temporary beginner inefficiency from a capability that compounds with deliberate practice.
The real question is not whether the team can do the work once, but how quickly it can become reliably good at it.
Skill acquisition depends on integrating many specific sub-skills rather than vague general ability claims.

failure_modes.mode: Early progress can create false mastery when leaders scale responsibility before the capability has ...
failure_modes.description: Early progress can create false mastery when leaders scale responsibility before the capability has actually consolidated.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify capability consolidation before scaling responsibility; early progress can create false mastery that collapses under load.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when early progress is mistaken for mastery and the organization scales responsibility faster than capability has actually consolidated.

failure_modes.mode: Illusion of Competence/Fluency
failure_modes.description: Passive exposure can look like learning even when the team has not built the retrieval strength needed for reliable performance.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test for retrieval strength, not just exposure: passive familiarity does not prove the team can perform reliably under pressure.
failure_modes.source_quote: Illusion of Competence/Fluency

failure_modes.mode: Cognitive Entrenchment
failure_modes.description: Pattern success can harden into entrenchment when an old framework gets reused mechanically in a new context.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge pattern reuse in new contexts: old frameworks applied mechanically can harden into entrenchment instead of accelerating learning.
failure_modes.source_quote: Cognitive Entrenchment

premortem_questions.description: Are we judging this capability by smooth early progress or by evidence that it will perform reliably after repetition and feedback?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when performance improves through repetition, feedback, and chunked skill-building rather than one-off inspiration.

premortem_questions.description: If I had to explain the core skill in plain English from memory, where would my explanation fall apart?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If I were to test myself on the main ideas, how exactly would I describe them?

premortem_questions.description: What assumption is embedded in the framework we are reusing, and are those assumptions actually met here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumption is embedded in the framework I am currently using, and are those assumptions met?

heuristics.description: Treat capability as something that compounds through repetition, feedback, and chunked skill-building instead of as a one-shot test.
heuristics.source_quote: Most useful when performance improves through repetition, feedback, and chunked skill-building rather than one-off inspiration.

heuristics.description: Use active recall and self-testing so fluency is measured by retrieval strength rather than by passive familiarity.
heuristics.source_quote: Use Active Recall and Self-Testing

heuristics.description: Stress-test edge cases and worst-case scenarios so ramp confidence does not rest only on the comfortable base case.
heuristics.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

danger_when: Early progress is being mistaken for mastery and responsibility is scaling faster than capability has actually consolidated.
A familiar framework is being reapplied mechanically because it worked before, even though the current problem may require a new pattern.
learning-curve.name: Learning Curve

select_when: A system has many moving parts, but only a few intervention points are likely to change behavior materially.
Resources are limited, so the team must decide where one action can alter flow, incentives, or constraints disproportionately.
Repeated effort at the surface has failed, and the real opportunity likely sits deeper in the system structure.
The work needs a disciplined way to prioritize what to do and what not to do instead of boiling the ocean.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can call every preferred initiative a leverage point without proving that it sits near a real ...
failure_modes.description: Teams can call every preferred initiative a leverage point without proving that it sits near a real rule, bottleneck, or feedback loop.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require proof that the proposed leverage point sits near a real rule, bottleneck, or feedback loop; labeling a preferred initiative as leverage is not evidence.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when leaders label every preferred initiative a leverage point without proving that it sits near a real feedback loop, bottleneck, or governing rule.

failure_modes.mode: Boiling the Ocean
failure_modes.description: Ocean-boiling analysis can bury the few structural moves that actually matter.
failure_modes.mitigation: Focus analysis on the few structural moves that actually matter; ocean-boiling analysis that maps everything buries the signal.
failure_modes.source_quote: Boiling the Ocean

failure_modes.mode: A good leverage diagnosis can still fail if the execution gap between what to do and how to do it is...
failure_modes.description: A good leverage diagnosis can still fail if the execution gap between what to do and how to do it is ignored.
failure_modes.mitigation: Close the execution gap: a good leverage diagnosis still fails if the team does not design how to implement the structural move.
failure_modes.source_quote: It is often straightforward to identify the necessary strategy (the "what," or the leverage point), but extremely difficult to figure out the execution (the "how").

premortem_questions.description: How does what you’re doing solve the problem?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what you’re doing solve the problem?

premortem_questions.description: How does it advance your thinking?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does it advance your thinking?

premortem_questions.description: Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?

heuristics.description: Disaggregate the problem and prioritize the few structural elements that matter most before analysis expands.
heuristics.source_quote: Problem Disaggregation and Prioritization

heuristics.description: Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the central hypothesis instead of boiling the ocean.
heuristics.source_quote: To avoid wasting time on low-leverage activities ("Don't boil the ocean"), one must gather only enough facts required to prove or disprove a central hypothesis.

heuristics.description: Use value-driver trees to expose the few variables that truly move the system and make what-if analysis tractable.
heuristics.source_quote: Employing a Return on Capital Tree can expose the fundamental levers of the business.

danger_when: Preferred initiatives are being labeled as leverage points without evidence that they sit near a real bottleneck, feedback loop, or governing rule.
The team is wading into analysis before clearly defining the system structure and the candidate intervention point.
leverage-points.name: Leverage Points
reframing_when: The question asks about a low-leverage intervention when a higher-leverage point of intervention exists in the same system — the question is about the 80% of effort that produces 20% of results.
The question treats all interventions as equally promising without prioritizing based on which variables the team can actually influence and which have the biggest system-wide impact.
The question asks 'how do we improve X?' when X is a downstream metric driven by an upstream variable Y that the question doesn't mention — the question is chasing the effect, not the cause.
The question is about optimizing within the current paradigm when the highest leverage point is changing the paradigm itself — the system resists this change, which is why the question avoids it.
leverage-points.reframing_move: Shift the question upstream: instead of asking about the metric or symptom, ask 'what is the highest-leverage variable in this system, and are we asking the right question about it?' — the Pareto reframe directs the question toward the 20% of variables that drive 80% of outcomes.

select_when: Trust, receptivity, or willingness to engage is the bottleneck and the audience first needs to feel understood rather than merely informed.
Warmth is being paired with demonstrated competence, reliability, and concrete relevance to the other person's actual problem.
The relationship needs to lower defensiveness enough for a harder message, recommendation, or ask to be heard.
A communication plan must distinguish genuine connection from charisma that would lower evidentiary standards.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when social warmth causes teams to lower their evidentiary standards for favored messengers
failure_modes.description: Teams can let warmth and familiarity lower evidentiary standards for favored messengers or charismatic leaders.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the messenger from the evidence: warmth and familiarity should not lower the evidentiary standard for claims made by liked people.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when social warmth causes teams to lower their evidentiary standards for favored messengers

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the desire to stay likable suppresses dissent, difficult feedback, or necessary escalation
failure_modes.description: A desire to stay likable can suppress dissent, hard feedback, or escalation about weak reasoning.
failure_modes.mitigation: Create explicit space for dissent so the desire to stay likable does not suppress hard feedback or escalation about weak reasoning.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the desire to stay likable suppresses dissent, difficult feedback, or necessary escalation

failure_modes.mode: An over-reliance on rapport and charisma can lead to the danger described by Maslow's Hammer
failure_modes.description: Charm can become a substitute for technical rigor when relationship energy is used to cover weak substance.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit whether substance or charm is carrying the argument; relationship energy that covers for weak technical rigor is a liability.
failure_modes.source_quote: An over-reliance on rapport and charisma can lead to the danger described by Maslow's Hammer

premortem_questions.description: What would someone who hates this idea say about our evidence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would someone who hates this idea say about our evidence?

premortem_questions.description: If a competitor presented this exact recommendation, would we still like it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If a competitor presented this exact recommendation, would we still like it?

premortem_questions.description: Does the emotional connection we have established distract from a critical flaw in the facts?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Does the emotional connection we’ve established distract from a critical flaw in the facts?

heuristics.description: Tailor the message to the audience's actual language and problem so feeling understood supports, rather than replaces, rigor.
heuristics.source_quote: Enter the conversation already taking place in the customer's mind

heuristics.description: Pair warmth with faultless reliability so trust rests on both relationship and competence.
heuristics.source_quote: you must treat all commitments ... faultlessly and on time

heuristics.description: Use emotional connection to lower defensiveness enough for the truth to land, not to exempt the idea from scrutiny.
heuristics.source_quote: Best used when the relationship must lower defensiveness enough for a harder message, recommendation, or ask to be heard.

danger_when: The decision requires deep analytical assessment and rapport is being treated as a substitute for evidence, structure, or technical competence.
The desire to stay likable is suppressing dissent, difficult feedback, or necessary escalation about risk, cost, or weak reasoning.
liking-principle.name: Liking Principle

select_when: Choosing among non-perishable ideas, tools, or practices where survival over time is itself meaningful evidence of resilience.
The environment is noisy and forecasting is weak, making longevity a better guide than recent hype.
The decision is about durability, staying power, or what is likely to remain useful beyond the current cycle.

failure_modes.mode: People can mistake survival so far for proof of superiority and stop testing whether the environment...
failure_modes.description: People can mistake survival so far for proof of superiority and stop testing whether the environment has changed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test whether survival so far is explained by genuine robustness or by luck and an unchanged environment; Lindy is not proof of superiority.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when age is treated as proof of superiority and the analysis ignores technological discontinuity, changing constraints, or new evidence that breaks the old baseline.

failure_modes.mode: Works best when the environment is noisy and forecasting is weak, making longevity a better guide than recent hype.
failure_modes.description: Recent hype can crowd out a durability signal when the environment is too noisy for short-term forecasting to be trusted.
failure_modes.mitigation: Use the durability signal when short-term noise makes forecasting unreliable; but verify that the long-lived baseline has not already been disrupted.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works best when the environment is noisy and forecasting is weak, making longevity a better guide than recent hype.

failure_modes.mode: Rare disruptive events can abruptly end a long-lived baseline that once looked stable.
failure_modes.description: Rare disruptive events can abruptly end a long-lived baseline that once looked stable.
failure_modes.mitigation: Model rare disruptive events that could abruptly end a long-lived baseline; Lindy-style reasoning is blind to regime changes.
failure_modes.source_quote: lindy-effect conflicts with black-swan-events (black swan events) when black Swan Events highlight unpredictable, rare occurrences that can abruptly end the longevity of ideas

premortem_questions.description: What exactly is non-perishable here, and what might actually be changing faster than the longevity signal suggests?
premortem_questions.source_quote: choosing among non-perishable ideas, tools, or practices

premortem_questions.description: What new constraint or discontinuity would break the old baseline even if the idea has lasted a long time?
premortem_questions.source_quote: ignores technological discontinuity, changing constraints, or new evidence

premortem_questions.description: Are we using Lindy because forecasting is weak, or because we are uncomfortable making a fresher judgment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: forecasting is weak, making longevity a better guide than recent hype

heuristics.description: Use longevity as evidence when the object is non-perishable and the environment is noisy.
heuristics.source_quote: survival over time is itself meaningful evidence of resilience

heuristics.description: Treat age as a prior, not as proof; re-check the baseline for discontinuity before deciding.
heuristics.source_quote: age is treated as proof of superiority

heuristics.description: Prefer Lindy logic when recent performance is mostly hype-sensitive and hard to forecast cleanly.
heuristics.source_quote: recent hype

danger_when: Age is being treated as proof of superiority while technological discontinuity or new evidence is ignored.
The object is perishable or structurally changing fast enough that longevity no longer carries the same evidentiary weight.
lindy-effect.name: Lindy Effect

select_when: Consistency, standardization, or deep commitment is creating compounding advantage, so the real question is which path should become sticky.
A migration or platform decision is being treated as reversible even though dependency drag, retraining, or coexistence costs are already growing.
Switching looks cheap in slides, but local habits, contracts, or workflows are quietly narrowing the option set over time.
The team needs to distinguish beneficial commitment from inertia-driven persistence.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when leaders treat "temporary coexistence" as harmless implementation detail.
failure_modes.description: Temporary coexistence can quietly become the mechanism that keeps the organization trapped in the old path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set an explicit sunset date for temporary coexistence; without one, interim arrangements quietly become the permanent path.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when leaders treat "temporary coexistence" as harmless implementation detail.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when switching later is priced as if it were the same option as switching now.
failure_modes.description: Teams can price switching later as if it were the same option as switching now even while reversal cost is compounding.
failure_modes.mitigation: Price switching cost from today's position, not from a future hypothetical; reversal cost compounds while the team deliberates.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when switching later is priced as if it were the same option as switching now.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Commitment Outlives Fit
failure_modes.description: Lock-in can outlive fit when a path is defended mainly because departure feels costly or identity-threatening.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the path's actual fit from the emotional or identity cost of departure; lock-in that outlives fit is defended by sunk-cost logic.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Commitment Outlives Fit

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe for the current sticky path to still be the best choice a year from now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If switching later were materially harder than switching now, where would that difficulty already be coming from?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The option set narrows long before the plan admits it.

premortem_questions.description: What part of the current plan assumes reversibility that coexistence, retraining, or local workflow drift is already eroding?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Dual-run periods, exception handling, retraining, and partner workarounds can become the mechanism that locks the organization into the old path while everyone still speaks as if the switch remains easy.

heuristics.description: Decide deliberately which norms or systems should become sticky and which must stay revisable.
heuristics.source_quote: Lock-in becomes productive when leaders consciously choose which norms, systems, or strategic positions should remain sticky and which must stay revisable.

heuristics.description: Treat dual-run coexistence as a real cost center instead of as harmless implementation detail.
heuristics.source_quote: The real economic and operational burden comes from keeping both the old and new path alive at the same time.

heuristics.description: Test whether the option to switch later survives time instead of assuming the future option set will stay open.
heuristics.source_quote: Best used when teams say "we can switch later" without testing whether the option survives time.

danger_when: The situation is genuinely novel, so reusing the incumbent frame would likely become an unhelpful or misleading default.
The model is being used to defend inherited architecture or belief simply because departure feels costly, disloyal, or identity-threatening.
lock-in.name: Lock In
reframing_when: The question treats the current platform, vendor, standard, or approach as a fixed constraint when it is actually a lock-in effect that can be broken — the constraint is economic or political, not physical.
The question escalates commitment to the current approach because switching costs feel prohibitive, without comparing those costs to the long-term cost of staying locked in.
The question frames the decision as 'how to optimize within our current system' when the real question is whether the system itself should be changed — the lock-in has collapsed the option space.
The question assumes that widespread adoption or institutional commitment validates the current frame, when network effects and switching costs explain the persistence better than quality does.
lock-in.reframing_move: Before optimizing within the current system, ask: 'Are we optimizing within this frame because it is the best frame, or because switching costs make alternatives feel impossible?' — the lock-in reframe separates quality from stickiness.

select_when: An argument sounds persuasive before it is valid and the inference structure needs to be checked explicitly.
Decision quality depends on separating evidence quality from presentation quality before resources are committed.
The team needs disciplined challenge of premises, causal links, or burden of proof.
A recommendation may be hiding overgeneralization, bad causality, or framework misuse under confident rhetoric.

failure_modes.mode: little flaws in the logic, similar to tiny stress fractures
failure_modes.description: A small flaw in the reasoning can behave like a stress fracture that only breaks the plan under pressure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Stress-test the argument structure before committing resources: diagram the logical chain and challenge each link with a 'what if this premise is wrong?' check.
failure_modes.source_quote: little flaws in the logic, similar to tiny stress fractures

failure_modes.mode: causal fallacies
failure_modes.description: Causal claims can outrun evidence and turn weak correlation into unjustified explanation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require independent evidence for every causal claim; if the link relies solely on temporal sequence or surface correlation, flag it as unearned.
failure_modes.source_quote: causal fallacies

failure_modes.mode: fallacy hunting replaces problem solving
failure_modes.description: Performative fallacy hunting can replace actual problem clarification and leave the decision no better than before.
failure_modes.mitigation: Redirect fallacy identification toward the decision: after naming the flaw, immediately ask what changes in the recommendation if this reasoning step is removed.
failure_modes.source_quote: fallacy hunting replaces problem solving

premortem_questions.description: What evidence actually supports this claim, and how does that evidence support the inference step by step?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Can you show how your evidence supports your claim?

premortem_questions.description: What would prove our current hypothesis wrong rather than merely making us uncomfortable?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What evidence, if I saw it, would prove my current hypothesis wrong?

premortem_questions.description: Are we treating this belief like a favorite team instead of a claim that should survive scrutiny?
premortem_questions.source_quote: favorite football team

heuristics.description: Separate correlation from causation before treating a pattern as an explanation.
heuristics.source_quote: separate correlation from causation

heuristics.description: Use Socratic questioning to surface weak premises, missing proof, and hidden leaps.
heuristics.source_quote: Socratic Questioning for Critical Thinking

heuristics.description: Think in gray instead of collapsing complex evidence into all-or-nothing language.
heuristics.source_quote: Thinking Gray

danger_when: Fallacy hunting is being used performatively to score debate points instead of clarifying the decision or action path.
The situation primarily requires relationship repair or implementation design rather than another round of argumentative nitpicking.
logical-fallacies.name: Logical Fallacies

select_when: Downside is hard to reverse and small forecast errors in time, capital, or quality could cascade into major loss.
Estimates are uncertain, but the team can still make buffers explicit instead of optimizing only the happy path.
Resilience can be built directly into budgets, staffing, inventory, thresholds, or decision rules before stress hits the system.
The real decision is whether the plan can survive volatility and error rather than merely whether the base case looks attractive.

failure_modes.mode: What You See Is All There Is (WYSIATI)
failure_modes.description: WYSIATI can make the buffer look sufficient because the team never surfaces the failure evidence that would widen it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Run a pre-mortem specifically for hidden failure evidence: ask what the team would need to believe for the buffer to be too small, then check whether anyone holds that evidence.
failure_modes.source_quote: What You See Is All There Is (WYSIATI)

failure_modes.mode: Illusion of Explanatory Depth (Overconfidence)
failure_modes.description: Overconfidence can shrink the needed margin because the operator assumes they know enough without further investigation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Calibrate the margin against historical miss rates, not against current confidence: use reference-class data from past projects to size the buffer.
failure_modes.source_quote: Illusion of Explanatory Depth (Overconfidence)

failure_modes.mode: Conjunctive-events bias can make timelines or budgets look safe even though success depends on too m...
failure_modes.description: Conjunctive-events bias can make timelines or budgets look safe even though success depends on too many steps going right.
failure_modes.mitigation: Decompose the plan into independent steps and check how many must succeed simultaneously; widen the margin when conjunctive dependencies stack up.
failure_modes.source_quote: People tend to overestimate the overall probability of these conjunctive events, leading to unwarranted optimism about a plan succeeding or being completed on time.

premortem_questions.description: A margin of safety is only real if it is wider than your recent track record of estimation errors. Before committing: what was the actual error range on the last three comparable estimates this team made?
premortem_questions.source_quote: A margin of safety is only real if it is wider than your recent track record of estimation errors -- not wider than your best-case model. Before committing: what was the actual error range on the last three comparable estimates this team made?

premortem_questions.description: What key facts or inputs have we ignored because they didn't fit our narrative?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What key facts or inputs have we ignored because they didn't fit our narrative?

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe for the opposite hypothesis to be true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would we have to believe for the opposite hypothesis to be true?

heuristics.description: Define failure first and devote effort to preventing it instead of describing only success.
heuristics.source_quote: Inverse Goals (Anti-Goals)

heuristics.description: Map known knowns, known unknowns, and unknown unknowns before deciding how much buffer the system needs.
heuristics.source_quote: Explicit Risk Categorization

heuristics.description: Model the full distribution of outcomes and ask whether the worst case is bad enough before proceeding.
heuristics.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

danger_when: The assumed buffer is being treated as static even though burn rate, exposure, or environmental volatility is moving underneath it.
Margin of safety is being used as an excuse for never shipping, never committing, or demanding certainty the situation cannot provide.
margin-of-safety.name: Margin Of Safety

select_when: The problem can be simplified into discrete states where the next step depends mainly on the current state.
Funnels, queues, or operational progressions need a bounded transition model instead of a full historical narrative.
The team wants to reason about stable handoffs, stage changes, or workflow branching with explicit transition assumptions.
Forecasting depends on whether transition patterns are stable enough to guide decisions.

failure_modes.mode: memory, path dependence, or hidden state matters more than the current visible state
failure_modes.description: A stable transition model breaks when hidden state or path dependence matters more than the current visible step.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the memoryless assumption explicitly: ask whether knowing the path that led here would change the next-step forecast, and if so, add state variables.
failure_modes.source_quote: memory, path dependence, or hidden state matters more than the current visible state

failure_modes.mode: yesterday's transition probabilities no longer describe today's regime
failure_modes.description: Sequential plans can overfit yesterday's regime when transition probabilities have shifted.
failure_modes.mitigation: Refit transition probabilities on recent windows and compare to the original model; if the distributions have drifted, flag regime change before acting on stale rates.
failure_modes.source_quote: yesterday's transition probabilities no longer describe today's regime

failure_modes.mode: risks magnifying confirmation bias
failure_modes.description: Hypothesis-driven workflows can magnify confirmation bias by overcommitting to a preferred path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build disconfirming branches into the state graph so the workflow forces exploration of alternatives rather than tunneling toward the preferred path.
failure_modes.source_quote: risks magnifying confirmation bias

premortem_questions.description: What hidden state or memory effect would make our current-state simplification misleading?
premortem_questions.source_quote: hidden state matters more than the current visible state

premortem_questions.description: Are the transition patterns still stable enough to trust this sequence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: transition patterns are stable enough

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough if this sequence fails at one critical step?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

heuristics.description: Break the process into clear states before reasoning about the next move.
heuristics.source_quote: defined steps lead to predictable outcomes

heuristics.description: Use logic trees or decision trees to model step-wise branches explicitly.
heuristics.source_quote: Logical Decomposition and Decision Trees

heuristics.description: Keep a one-day answer so each step still connects to the broader goal.
heuristics.source_quote: What's the one-day answer?

danger_when: Hidden state, memory effects, or path dependence matter more than the visible current state.
The environment is shifting so fast that yesterday's transition assumptions are unlikely to remain stable.
markov-chains.name: Markov Chains

select_when: Repeated surprise or prediction failure suggests the current frame is missing the real causal structure.
The team must compare competing worldviews explicitly before choosing the one that best predicts and guides action.
A problem spans multiple domains and no single vocabulary explains what is happening well enough.
Communication is failing because the current map is being mistaken for the territory.

failure_modes.mode: the map is not reality
failure_modes.description: A mental model becomes dangerous when it is treated as the territory rather than as a partial map.
failure_modes.mitigation: Label every model as a map, not the territory: require the team to name at least one important thing the model leaves out before using it to decide.
failure_modes.source_quote: the map is not reality

failure_modes.mode: substituting a simple model we know for the complicated model actually required
failure_modes.description: Availability-driven substitution makes the team reuse a simple familiar model instead of the more complicated one actually required.
failure_modes.mitigation: Rotate models deliberately: when availability keeps surfacing the same frame, force a second pass with a structurally different model to expose what the familiar one hides.
failure_modes.source_quote: substituting a simple model we know for the complicated model actually required

failure_modes.mode: cognitive entrenchment
failure_modes.description: Cognitive entrenchment leads experts to skip details and protect the worldview they already carry.
failure_modes.mitigation: Schedule periodic model audits where experts must explain, not just assert, the mechanisms — surfacing where their shortcuts have become stale.
failure_modes.source_quote: cognitive entrenchment

premortem_questions.description: What repeated surprise or prediction failure is telling us that our current map is missing the real structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: repeated surprise, disagreement, or prediction failure

premortem_questions.description: What mechanism, threshold, or protocol would follow if this worldview were actually true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what specific mechanism, threshold, or protocol would follow from it

premortem_questions.description: Which outside-view fact would force us to update this model of reality rather than defend it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what is true generally of the world independent of one's perspective

heuristics.description: Compare competing mental models explicitly and choose the one that predicts, explains, and guides action best.
heuristics.source_quote: compare competing worldviews explicitly

heuristics.description: Use anti-goals and inversion to reduce error before optimizing for brilliance.
heuristics.source_quote: Inverse Thinking / Anti-Goal Setting

heuristics.description: Translate complex ideas into simple explanations or analogies to reveal where the model still has gaps.
heuristics.source_quote: The Feynman Technique

danger_when: High-level abstraction is being used as a substitute for concrete mechanism, threshold, or protocol.
The team is treating one familiar worldview as sufficient evidence instead of testing it against reality.
mental-models-of-reality.name: Mental Models Of Reality

select_when: A decision is expensive, slow, or risky to test directly, but the team can still rehearse plausible scenarios, reactions, and contingencies.
Multiple futures need to be compared explicitly, including hostile stakeholders, user confusion, competitor moves, or operational edge cases.
The team needs to tie rehearsal to concrete thresholds, pre-mortems, or contingency plans rather than treating simulation as imaginative theater.
The next move should test assumptions by rehearsing ugly cases, not just the preferred storyline.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when vivid rehearsal is mistaken for evidence
failure_modes.description: Vivid rehearsal can be mistaken for evidence, making confidence rise faster than the quality of the assumptions or data.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate vividness from evidence: after any mental rehearsal, list the empirical inputs it actually consumed and flag any confidence that exceeds the data.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when vivid rehearsal is mistaken for evidence

failure_modes.mode: Danger when teams keep simulating their preferred storyline
failure_modes.description: Teams can keep simulating the preferred storyline while skipping ugly cases, tail risks, and disconfirming scenarios.
failure_modes.mitigation: Mandate at least one ugly-case simulation for every success-path rehearsal; require the team to simulate a failure scenario with the same level of detail.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams keep simulating their preferred storyline

failure_modes.mode: Confirmation Bias
failure_modes.description: Simulations can reinforce model-defending behavior when they are run mainly to confirm an existing leadership hypothesis.
failure_modes.mitigation: Assign a red-team member whose sole job is to run the simulation against the opposite hypothesis, not to confirm leadership's preferred story.
failure_modes.source_quote: Confirmation Bias

premortem_questions.description: What ugly case, hostile response, or edge condition would actually change this plan if it showed up in the simulation?
premortem_questions.source_quote: ugly cases, tail risks, and disconfirming scenarios

premortem_questions.description: What threshold, contingency, or trigger are we tying this simulation to so it is not just imaginative theater?
premortem_questions.source_quote: tied to concrete thresholds, pre-mortems, or contingency plans

premortem_questions.description: Which assumption in the simulation is least grounded and most capable of flipping the recommendation?
premortem_questions.source_quote: underlying assumptions or data

heuristics.description: Use simulation to compare multiple plausible futures, not to glorify the favorite plan.
heuristics.source_quote: multiple futures can be compared explicitly

heuristics.description: Pair the rehearsal with premortems, contingencies, and edge-case stress checks so it changes behavior rather than just confidence.
heuristics.source_quote: pre-mortems, or contingency plans

heuristics.description: Treat simulation as the next best thing to experience, not as a substitute for reality.
heuristics.source_quote: not as good as actually doing something, but it is the next best thing

danger_when: Vivid rehearsal is being mistaken for evidence and confidence is rising faster than the quality of the underlying assumptions or data.
The team keeps simulating its preferred story but is skipping the ugly cases, tail risks, or disconfirming scenarios that would actually change the plan.
mental-simulation.name: Mental Simulation

select_when: The thinker needs to inspect how they are thinking, not just what they are concluding, because the process itself may be the constraint.
Planning, learning, or decision quality would improve if the team deliberately monitored assumptions, progress, and blind spots.
The work is moving, but without enough self-audit to notice drift, confusion, or shallow understanding.
A pause for internalized teacher-like review could improve the next move more than more raw effort.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Reflection Becomes Self-Soothing Rather Than Corrective
failure_modes.description: Reflection can become self-soothing introspection that leaves the actual decision process unchanged.
failure_modes.mitigation: Tie each reflection cycle to a concrete decision change: if the reflection does not alter at least one action or assumption, treat it as incomplete.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Reflection Becomes Self-Soothing Rather Than Corrective

failure_modes.mode: metacognition involves planning, monitoring, and evaluating one's thinking
failure_modes.description: The model fails when motion and effort continue without any monitoring of whether the current strategy is working.
failure_modes.mitigation: Install lightweight checkpoints that ask 'is the current strategy producing the expected signal?' at defined intervals instead of relying on spontaneous self-monitoring.
failure_modes.source_quote: metacognition involves planning, monitoring, and evaluating one's thinking

failure_modes.mode: reflection should improve performance, not paralyze it
failure_modes.description: Too much self-observation can interrupt execution when the task now needs action or repetition instead of more process commentary.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a reflection budget: when the task needs execution or repetition, cap the meta-commentary and redirect energy into deliberate practice.
failure_modes.source_quote: reflection should improve performance, not paralyze it

premortem_questions.description: How am I thinking about this, and what might that process be missing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: thinking about thinking

premortem_questions.description: What would an internalized teacher say I am not checking closely enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: an internalized teacher monitors and guides the learner

premortem_questions.description: What sign would show that this reflection improved the next move rather than just my feeling of control?
premortem_questions.source_quote: reflection should improve performance

heuristics.description: Deliberately monitor the thinking process, not just the output, while the work is still adjustable.
heuristics.source_quote: planning, monitoring, and evaluating one's thinking

heuristics.description: Use reflection to catch drift, confusion, and shallow understanding before they harden into rework.
heuristics.source_quote: metacognition improves learning and performance by revealing misunderstandings

heuristics.description: Translate reflection into a corrected next move so the loop changes behavior instead of just self-description.
heuristics.source_quote: reflection should improve performance
