danger_when: Reflection is becoming self-soothing, journaling theater, or introspective overprocessing instead of improving the next decision.
The situation is already clear and the team needs deliberate action, repetition, or execution more than another layer of process self-observation.
meta-cognitive-reflection.name: Meta Cognitive Reflection

select_when: Better questions would change the path because the main constraint is weak inspection of assumptions, strategy choice, or evidence quality.
The team can produce answers quickly but still needs to explain how it knows and where the chain might be weak.
Self-monitoring could prevent drift by interrupting autopilot before the work hardens around a bad frame.
A problem needs explicit next-step questions that review variables, constraints, and reasoning quality before more analysis is added.

failure_modes.mode: Self-questioning can become infinite deferral that keeps postponing commitment, experimentation, or ...
failure_modes.description: Self-questioning can become infinite deferral that keeps postponing commitment, experimentation, or ownership.
failure_modes.mitigation: Impose a question deadline: after a fixed number of inquiry rounds, the team must commit to a reversible experiment that generates real data.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Self-Questioning Becomes Infinite Deferral: The model is misused when reflective questions keep postponing commitment, experimentation, or ownership instead of sharpening the next concrete move.

failure_modes.mode: The inquiry can overload working memory when it keeps accumulating low-quality or irrelevant informa...
failure_modes.description: The inquiry can overload working memory when it keeps accumulating low-quality or irrelevant information.
failure_modes.mitigation: Prune the question queue ruthlessly: rank questions by decision relevance and drop those that would not change the next action even if answered.
failure_modes.source_quote: If the resulting self-inquiry leads to too much irrelevant or low-quality information, it can clutter the mental picture and confuse the model.

failure_modes.mode: Strategy can fail when people question what the right answer is but never interrogate how the work w...
failure_modes.description: Strategy can fail when people question what the right answer is but never interrogate how the work will actually be executed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Extend the inquiry to execution planning: for every 'what is the right answer?' question, require a paired 'how will we actually implement and verify this?' question.
failure_modes.source_quote: Excessive metacognition on what the right strategy is, without applying rigorous questioning to the executable steps (how), can render brilliant insights useless.

premortem_questions.description: How do you define a problem in a precise way to meet the decision maker's needs?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How do you define a problem in a precise way to meet the decision maker’s needs?

premortem_questions.description: How do you disaggregate the issues and develop hypotheses to be explored?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How do you disaggregate the issues and develop hypotheses to be explored?

premortem_questions.description: What's the one-day answer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What’s the one-day answer?

heuristics.description: Review the relevant variables, define the next-step question, and then consolidate what the answer changes before widening the inquiry again.
heuristics.source_quote: This cycle involves: Reviewing and storing relevant variables ... Defining the specific next-step question ... Consolidating gains by clearly reviewing the answer identified.

heuristics.description: Ask process questions, not just outcome questions, so the underlying thought chain becomes transferable.
heuristics.source_quote: Instead of asking an expert, "Whom should I hire for this project?" ... ask: "What skills matter and which ones can be learned on the job? Why? Where do I find the best people? How do I test these skills?"

heuristics.description: Set the purpose boundary in advance so the questioning sharpens action instead of boiling the ocean.
heuristics.source_quote: In advance, strictly define the purpose of the explanation: everything that does not explicitly help explain that purpose stays out.

danger_when: Reflective questioning is turning into infinite deferral that postpones commitment, experimentation, or ownership.
The inquiry is widening into low-quality information gathering instead of sharpening the next concrete move.
metacognitive-questioning.name: Metacognitive Questioning
reframing_when: The question proceeds directly to evaluating options without first examining what kind of thinking is being used to frame the problem — the reasoning strategy itself is unquestioned.

The question treats its own framing as transparent ('obviously this is about X') when stepping back to ask 'what am I assuming about the nature of this problem?' would reveal a different problem structure.
The question is stuck in a single problem-solving mode (analytical, creative, procedural) and would benefit from asking 'is this the right type of thinking for this situation?'
The question treats surface symptoms as the problem definition without asking 'what would I need to know to be sure this is the real problem and not a symptom of something deeper?'
metacognitive-questioning.reframing_move: Before answering the question as posed, ask: 'What kind of thinking is this question inviting me to do, and is that the right kind of thinking for this situation?' — the metacognitive step-back forces examination of the reasoning strategy itself rather than just its outputs.

select_when: The decision depends on the spread of possible outcomes rather than on a single base-case forecast.
Uncertain inputs can be expressed as ranges or distributions well enough to support repeated simulation.
Leaders need to understand downside, variance, or tail exposure before committing resources.
The team must compare scenarios where many variables interact and simple point estimates would hide risk.

failure_modes.mode: Move Beyond the Mean
failure_modes.description: Base-case bias hides tail risk when decision-makers overweight the mean outcome.
failure_modes.mitigation: Report tail percentiles (5th, 1st) alongside the mean and require decision-makers to state which tail scenario they can survive.
failure_modes.source_quote: Move Beyond the Mean

failure_modes.mode: convert weak assumptions into convincing-looking rigor
failure_modes.description: Simulation can create false rigor when the input distributions are casually guessed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the input distributions: trace each assumption back to empirical data or expert calibration and flag any distribution that was set by convenience.
failure_modes.source_quote: convert weak assumptions into convincing-looking rigor

failure_modes.mode: unknown unknowns or structural breaks dominate the outcome
failure_modes.description: Unknown unknowns and structural breaks can make simulated ranges look safer than reality.
failure_modes.mitigation: Stress-test the model against structural breaks by running scenarios where key relationships change, not just where parameters vary within the assumed distribution.
failure_modes.source_quote: unknown unknowns or structural breaks dominate the outcome

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: Which input distributions are anchored to reference classes rather than wishful guesses?
premortem_questions.source_quote: reference classes

premortem_questions.description: What unknown unknown or regime break would this simulation fail to imagine?
premortem_questions.source_quote: unknown unknowns

heuristics.description: Model a range of outcomes instead of relying on one point forecast.
heuristics.source_quote: range of possible outcomes

heuristics.description: Run stochastic sensitivity analysis across wide ranges, not narrow comfort bands.
heuristics.source_quote: Sweep one or more probabilities through wide ranges of value

heuristics.description: Use the outside view and reference classes to seed assumptions before simulating.
heuristics.source_quote: Anchor to the Outside View

danger_when: The input distributions are mostly guesswork, so simulation would only convert weak assumptions into false rigor.
Unknown unknowns or regime breaks dominate the case, making sampled scenarios look more complete than they really are.
monte-carlo-methods.name: Monte Carlo Methods

select_when: One party can take action while another party absorbs a meaningful share of the downside.
Incentives, insurance, guarantees, or governance structures may be rewarding hidden risk-taking.
The key question is whether accountability, exposure, and decision rights are aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior.
A system needs to separate bad incentives from bad luck or weak capability before deciding how to respond.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can blame every bad outcome on distorted incentives even when uncertainty, luck, or weak capab...
failure_modes.description: Teams can blame every bad outcome on distorted incentives even when uncertainty, luck, or weak capability are the dominant drivers.
failure_modes.mitigation: Diagnose the real driver before invoking incentive distortion: check whether the outcome is better explained by uncertainty, capability limits, or system design before blaming hidden behavior.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when every poor outcome gets attributed to moral hazard even when the dominant driver is uncertainty, bad luck, or weak capability rather than distorted incentives.

failure_modes.mode: In reality, outcomes are dependent not just on the agent's action/effort, but also on chance factors.
failure_modes.description: Outcome-based contracts can misfire when observable results are shaped heavily by chance rather than by the hidden behavior being targeted.
failure_modes.mitigation: When outcomes are noisy, shift from pure outcome-based contracts to process-observable metrics that reward the behavior you can actually verify.
failure_modes.source_quote: In reality, outcomes are dependent not just on the agent's action/effort, but also on chance factors.

failure_modes.mode: Immediate outcome incentives can create short-termism that externalizes longer-term risk.
failure_modes.description: Immediate outcome incentives can create short-termism that externalizes longer-term risk.
failure_modes.mitigation: Add longer time horizons or clawback provisions to contracts so agents cannot externalize tail risk by cashing out before consequences arrive.
failure_modes.source_quote: If payments are based solely on immediate observable outcomes ... this can inadvertently incentivize short-term gains at the expense of long-term stability or ethical behavior.

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask: “Is the worst case bad enough?”.

premortem_questions.description: Who takes the action, and who absorbs the downside if it goes wrong?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when one party can take action while another party absorbs a meaningful share of the downside.

premortem_questions.description: Are accountability, exposure, and decision rights aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best used when the question is whether accountability, exposure, and decision rights are aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior.

heuristics.description: Structure incentives so people experience enough consequence from their decisions to behave like owners rather than insulated agents.
heuristics.source_quote: Managers should structure incentives and penalties to encourage people to take full ownership of what they do.

heuristics.description: Discuss failures at both the machine level and the case-at-hand level so the fix targets the system that produced the behavior.
heuristics.source_quote: When a problem occurs, always discuss it at two levels: 1) the machine level ... and 2) the case-at-hand level.

heuristics.description: Welcome probing and peer review because observers often detect hidden risk-taking logic better than the actors inside it.
heuristics.source_quote: Observers (critics and gossipers) are often more effective at detecting errors than actors, suggesting the use of peer review or a brain trust to catch blind spots.

danger_when: Every poor outcome is being attributed to distorted incentives even though uncertainty, chance, or weak capability are the dominant drivers.
The analysis assumes a clean one-to-one link between observable outcome and effort even though luck and hidden conditions materially affect the result.
moral-hazard.name: Moral Hazard

select_when: Several criteria matter at once and intuitive trade-offs are likely to be inconsistent or politically distorted.
A high-stakes choice needs explicit weighting logic that can be challenged, revised, and audited.
The team must compare options transparently across competing goals rather than pretending one metric is enough.
The current argument keeps collapsing into preference instead of explicit criteria and trade-offs.

failure_modes.mode: criteria are subconsciously chosen or weighted to steer toward a desired conclusion
failure_modes.description: Precise-looking scores can hide criteria manipulation when the team really chose the answer first.
failure_modes.mitigation: Lock criteria weights before scoring alternatives; if the team adjusts weights after seeing scores, flag reverse-engineering of a predetermined answer.
failure_modes.source_quote: criteria are subconsciously chosen or weighted to steer toward a desired conclusion

failure_modes.mode: anchoring
failure_modes.description: Anchoring on one factor can distort the whole weighted decision even inside a formal matrix.
failure_modes.mitigation: Run a sensitivity test on the dominant criterion: check whether a plausible reweight of the top factor would flip the ranking, and if so, debate that factor first.
failure_modes.source_quote: anchoring

failure_modes.mode: Analysis Paralysis
failure_modes.description: Analysis paralysis appears when the team keeps refining the matrix instead of making the call.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision deadline before opening the matrix: agree in advance on when analysis stops and the call gets made, regardless of matrix refinement state.
failure_modes.source_quote: Analysis Paralysis

premortem_questions.description: Which criterion weights would have to change materially to reverse the current ranking?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Run Sensitivity Analysis

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe for the favored option to deserve these weights?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What do we have to believe for this option to succeed?

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough that a low-probability downside should dominate our scoring logic?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

heuristics.description: Build an explicit selection matrix with weighted criteria before debating favorite options.
heuristics.source_quote: Construct the selection matrix

heuristics.description: Start with make-or-break hypotheses and criteria rather than equal treatment of every nice-to-have factor.
heuristics.source_quote: Start with Make or Break

heuristics.description: Separate process quality from outcome luck so a good decision is not judged only by a single result.
heuristics.source_quote: good decisions consistently

danger_when: The scoring model is creating false objectivity by assigning precise weights no one actually understands or believes.
The team is spending more effort polishing the matrix than making the decision or checking the real sensitivities.
multi-criteria-decision-analysis.name: Multi Criteria Decision Analysis
reframing_when: The question optimizes on a single proxy metric ('what is cheapest?', 'what is fastest?') when the decision actually involves multiple criteria that trade off against each other in non-obvious ways.
The question collapses a multi-dimensional evaluation into a binary ('should we do this or not?') when the real question is how to weight competing priorities.
The question treats all criteria as equally important by default, or weights them implicitly based on which is easiest to measure rather than which matters most.
The question frames the decision as choosing between options when the real leverage is in clarifying what criteria to optimize — the frame determines which option looks best.
multi-criteria-decision-analysis.reframing_move: Before evaluating options, ask: 'What criteria are we actually optimizing, how should we weight them, and would different weights change which option looks best?' — the MCDA reframe makes the implicit weighting explicit and testable.

select_when: Decision quality depends on surfacing assumptions, interpretations, and stakeholder signals that a homogeneous team would miss.
The work crosses functions, cultures, or markets and simple internal consensus would otherwise hide costly blind spots.
The team is willing to translate across differences and turn diversity into better reasoning rather than symbolic representation.

failure_modes.mode: a homogeneous team would miss
failure_modes.description: A culturally homogeneous consensus can hide blind spots that a more diverse interpretive set would surface.
failure_modes.mitigation: Staff the decision team for interpretive range: ensure at least two genuinely different cultural or disciplinary perspectives have veto-grade input, not just attendance.
failure_modes.source_quote: a homogeneous team would miss

failure_modes.mode: Hierarchy and social comfort can suppress dissenting cross-cultural signals before they affect the d...
failure_modes.description: Hierarchy and social comfort can suppress dissenting cross-cultural signals before they affect the decision.
failure_modes.mitigation: Create explicit dissent protocols (e.g., anonymous pre-meeting input, rotating devil's advocate) so hierarchy cannot filter cross-cultural signals before they reach the decision.
failure_modes.source_quote: multicultural-team-dynamics conflicts with authority-bias (authority bias) when hierarchy overrides cross-cultural signal from lower-power members.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can recruit diversity for optics while centralizing the actual decision process so tightly tha...
failure_modes.description: Teams can recruit diversity for optics while centralizing the actual decision process so tightly that difference never shapes the outcome.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit whether diverse voices actually changed the outcome: after each major decision, trace which inputs shifted the answer and flag any pattern of decorative inclusion.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when leaders recruit diversity for optics but keep the real decision process so centralized that difference never shapes the outcome.

premortem_questions.description: What interpretation or stakeholder signal would this team miss if everyone shared the same default assumptions?
premortem_questions.source_quote: decision quality depends on surfacing assumptions, interpretations, and stakeholder signals that a homogeneous team would miss

premortem_questions.description: Where is hierarchy or social comfort suppressing information from lower-power members?
premortem_questions.source_quote: hierarchy overrides cross-cultural signal from lower-power members

premortem_questions.description: Are we translating across differences to improve reasoning, or just displaying diversity without changing judgment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: turn diversity into better reasoning rather than symbolic representation

heuristics.description: Design the discussion so different backgrounds surface genuinely different interpretations before consensus forms.
heuristics.source_quote: turn diversity into better reasoning

heuristics.description: Treat cross-cultural disagreement as information about the system, not as noise to smooth away quickly.
heuristics.source_quote: work crosses functions, cultures, or markets

heuristics.description: Check whether lower-power members have an intact path to influence the final decision.
heuristics.source_quote: authority bias when hierarchy overrides cross-cultural signal from lower-power members

danger_when: Leadership wants diversity optics without allowing difference to change the real decision process.
The coordination problem is straightforward enough that adding diversity theater would only slow execution without improving judgment.
multicultural-team-dynamics.name: Multicultural Team Dynamics

select_when: An abstract recommendation needs a memorable causal story so people can understand why the change matters and what to do next.
Adoption depends on belief shift, identity shift, or coherent sequencing rather than on dumping more disconnected facts at the audience.
Scattered evidence needs to be turned into one clear line of meaning without losing the operational point.
The next move should make the causal logic legible while resisting the temptation to overclaim certainty.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when story coherence outruns evidence quality
failure_modes.description: A clean story arc can outrun evidence quality and create more certainty than the proof warrants.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require the narrator to list what the story leaves out: every clean arc should be paired with the uncertainties, trade-offs, and alternative interpretations it compressed away.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when story coherence outruns evidence quality

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the narrative compresses genuine uncertainty into false inevitability
failure_modes.description: Narrative compression can erase genuine uncertainty, trade-offs, or contingencies by turning them into false inevitability.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test narrative claims against base-rate data: if the story implies inevitability, check whether the reference class of similar situations shows the same trajectory.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the narrative compresses genuine uncertainty into false inevitability

failure_modes.mode: Motivated Reasoning/Inside View
failure_modes.description: Motivated reasoners can use rhetorical skill to persuade themselves toward a desired conclusion instead of seeking objective truth.
failure_modes.mitigation: Appoint a designated counter-narrator who constructs an equally plausible story from the same evidence but with the opposite conclusion.
failure_modes.source_quote: Motivated Reasoning/Inside View

premortem_questions.description: Which missing evidence, trade-off, or contingency disappears if we tell this as one tidy story?
premortem_questions.source_quote: making contingencies, trade-offs, or missing evidence disappear

premortem_questions.description: Are we using the story to clarify causal meaning or to paper over uncertainty we do not want to face?
premortem_questions.source_quote: story coherence outruns evidence quality

premortem_questions.description: Can the core message still hold if we strip away every detail that does not serve the central purpose?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Focus on the Core and Concrete

heuristics.description: Use narrative to make causality memorable, but keep the operational point and evidence trace visible.
heuristics.source_quote: one clear line of meaning without losing the operational point

heuristics.description: Keep the core message concrete and small enough that people can act on it rather than just admire the story.
heuristics.source_quote: Rule of Three

heuristics.description: Seek the outside view before trusting a compelling story, especially when the arc feels too clean.
heuristics.source_quote: Seek the Outside View

danger_when: Story coherence is outrunning evidence quality and a clean arc is making the audience feel more certainty than the proof warrants.
The narrative is compressing genuine uncertainty into false inevitability and making contingencies, trade-offs, or missing evidence disappear.
narratives.name: Narratives

select_when: Your best move depends on how other players are likely to respond rather than on your action in isolation.
The strategic landscape can be bounded to a few credible players, payoff drivers, and response options.
The main question is whether the system will settle into a stable pattern, focal point, or lock-in that no one can escape alone.
Individually rational actors may still converge on a collectively mediocre steady state.

failure_modes.mode: If your laptop cannot find it, neither can the market
failure_modes.description: A theoretically stable equilibrium may be useless if real actors cannot compute or sustain it.
failure_modes.mitigation: Before applying equilibrium analysis, verify that real actors can observe payoffs, compute strategies, and sustain the equilibrium under actual information and resource constraints.
failure_modes.source_quote: If your laptop cannot find it, neither can the market

failure_modes.mode: treating a stable outcome as a good outcome
failure_modes.description: Analysts can mistake a stable pattern for a good or efficient outcome.
failure_modes.mitigation: Label the equilibrium's welfare properties explicitly: state whether the stable outcome is efficient, fair, or merely self-reinforcing, and flag any gap between stability and desirability.
failure_modes.source_quote: treating a stable outcome as a good outcome

failure_modes.mode: Confirmation Bias and Groupthink
failure_modes.description: Teams can model the game to confirm a preferred equilibrium rather than mapping the full space honestly.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map the full game space before locking onto one equilibrium: require at least one alternative equilibrium analysis to prevent confirmation of the preferred outcome.
failure_modes.source_quote: Confirmation Bias and Groupthink

premortem_questions.description: Can the actual players find and sustain this equilibrium, or only the analyst?
premortem_questions.source_quote: participants cannot be taken for granted to discover or reach the NE

premortem_questions.description: What reasons might make the real outcome differ from the candidate equilibrium?
premortem_questions.source_quote: think of reasons why, and the manner in which, the outcome may differ

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe about payoffs and responses for this stable pattern to hold?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.description: Begin strategic interaction analysis with candidate best responses, then pressure-test them.
heuristics.source_quote: begin the analysis of strategic interactions with the Nash equilibrium

heuristics.description: Use war games to model moves and countermoves before acting.
heuristics.source_quote: war games

heuristics.description: Lay strategic choices out in logic trees so assumptions and payoffs are explicit.
heuristics.source_quote: logic tree

danger_when: The equilibrium is computationally or behaviorally unreachable because the actors cannot actually discover or sustain it.
A stable outcome is being mistaken for a good, efficient, or desirable outcome.
nash-equilibrium.name: Nash Equilibrium

select_when: A solution space is better explored by generating variants, testing them, and letting evidence eliminate weak options.
The environment is noisy or hard to model directly, so iterative experimentation beats static up-front prediction.
The team needs to reason from variation, selection, and retention rather than from one master-plan explanation.
Adaptive strategy depends on ecosystem feedback more than on one perfect design assumption.

failure_modes.mode: failure to define what fitness actually means
failure_modes.description: Teams talk about selection and emergence without defining the fitness criteria the human system should actually optimize.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define the fitness function explicitly before invoking selection language: state what is being optimized, by what mechanism, and over what time horizon.
failure_modes.source_quote: failure to define what fitness actually means

failure_modes.mode: superficial similarities instead of the correct structural relationship
failure_modes.description: A bad analogy imports biological logic into a business context that does not share the same structural mechanism.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test structural homology before importing the analogy: list at least three mechanisms the biological and business contexts share and one they do not, then judge whether the analogy still holds.
failure_modes.source_quote: superficial similarities instead of the correct structural relationship

failure_modes.mode: survival bias
failure_modes.description: Survival bias makes people study only the winners and miss what the eliminated variants would have taught them.
failure_modes.mitigation: Deliberately study the eliminated variants: run a post-mortem on failed experiments or abandoned options to extract learning that survivor-only analysis would miss.
failure_modes.source_quote: survival bias

premortem_questions.description: What evidence would show this situation does not actually fit the selection process we are borrowing from biology?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Does this situation fit the reference class

premortem_questions.description: Which part of the current design needs more variation before we can trust the environment to select among options?
premortem_questions.source_quote: generate multiple credible variants

premortem_questions.description: If this solution wins, will it be because we improved the machine or just masked a symptom temporarily?
premortem_questions.source_quote: fix a fundamental flaw in our design

heuristics.description: Do it like Darwin by generating more credible variants before narrowing too early.
heuristics.source_quote: Do It Like Darwin

heuristics.description: Use iterative refinement and feedback loops so retention follows evidence instead of taste.
heuristics.source_quote: gradually adjusting it based on the responses

heuristics.description: Treat quantity in ideation as a path to better quality when the environment is still selecting.
heuristics.source_quote: quantity of ideas drives quality

danger_when: The language of emergence is being used to avoid setting goals, decision rights, or fitness criteria in a human system.
The analogy is being applied on surface similarity without checking whether the structural selection mechanism really matches.
natural-selection-analogy.name: Natural Selection Analogy
reframing_when: The question frames the situation as designed or intentional when it is actually the emergent result of selection pressures — the 'purpose' is post-hoc narrative, not actual design.
The question assumes that what survived is what is best, when selection only ensures survival relative to a specific environment — survivors are fit, not optimal.
The question treats the current competitive landscape as stable when environmental shifts will change which approaches are selected for — fitness is contextual, not absolute.
The question optimizes a single trait in isolation when real selection acts on the whole organism — local optimization may reduce overall fitness.
natural-selection-analogy.reframing_move: Before treating the current situation as designed or optimal, ask: 'What selection pressures produced this outcome, and would different pressures have selected for something completely different?' — the natural selection reframe replaces intentional narrative with environmental analysis.

select_when: The value of adoption may rise materially as more users, contributors, or counterparties join the same system.
The team needs to know whether growth mechanics are self-reinforcing after a threshold or are merely linear scale wrapped in better storytelling.
A strategy depends on reaching critical mass before the system becomes viable, defensible, or self-sustaining.
The next move should test density, reach, or seeding assumptions rather than assuming that ordinary growth automatically compounds into network value.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when leaders label ordinary scale benefits as network effects
failure_modes.description: Teams can label ordinary scale benefits as network effects without proving that each added participant improves value for the next one.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require proof of the feedback loop: show that adding one more participant measurably improves value for existing participants, not just that the product scales with volume.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when leaders label ordinary scale benefits as network effects

failure_modes.mode: Mistaking Local Success for Critical Mass
failure_modes.description: Local success can be mistaken for critical mass before the threshold for self-sustaining adoption is actually real.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define the critical-mass threshold quantitatively and track adoption against it; do not declare network effects until the self-sustaining point is empirically reached.
failure_modes.source_quote: Mistaking Local Success for Critical Mass

failure_modes.mode: The Boiling the Ocean Trap
failure_modes.description: Teams can overcomplicate the system before viability by trying to maximize every connection or data point too early.
failure_modes.mitigation: Defer complexity until the core loop works: resist adding features, integrations, or data layers until the minimal network-effect mechanism is validated with real users.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Boiling the Ocean Trap

premortem_questions.description: What density is actually needed to reach the critical mass point in this market or segment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What density ... is needed to reach the critical mass point

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe to accept that critical mass is not reachable here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would we have to believe to accept that critical mass is not reachable?

premortem_questions.description: Can the business model be tweaked so the threshold becomes reachable before organic growth is expected to carry it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Can you tweak the business model so that this critical mass point is reachable

heuristics.description: Separate threshold effects from ordinary growth by asking whether each added participant actually increases value for the next one.
heuristics.source_quote: value of adoption rises materially as more users ... join

heuristics.description: Use seeding intentionally when the network would otherwise fail before it reaches viable density.
heuristics.source_quote: seed each area with its own tools

heuristics.description: Focus on the few users, nodes, or features that matter most for pushing the system past critical mass.
heuristics.source_quote: Pareto Principle (80/20 Rule)

danger_when: Leaders are labeling ordinary scale benefits as network effects without showing that each additional participant actually increases value for the next one.
The strategy is boiling the ocean by overcomplicating the system before critical mass is even reachable.
network-effects.name: Network Effects

select_when: Small interventions could create outsized effects because feedback loops, delays, or threshold crossings dominate the system.
Local optimization may improve one metric while degrading the wider system over time.
The current plan assumes straight-line cause and effect even though the environment is full of interacting parts.
The team needs to map reinforcing and balancing loops before deciding where to intervene.

failure_modes.mode: outcomes in complex systems rarely follow a straight-line progression
failure_modes.description: Straight-line plans can miss threshold crossings, delayed blowback, and outsized effects from small moves.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map threshold crossings and feedback loops explicitly before planning: identify where small changes could trigger disproportionate effects and build monitoring around those points.
failure_modes.source_quote: outcomes in complex systems rarely follow a straight-line progression

failure_modes.mode: local optimization can hurt the whole system
failure_modes.description: Local optimization can damage long-run system behavior elsewhere.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check second-order effects of every local optimization: ask what worsens elsewhere in the system when this parameter improves here.
failure_modes.source_quote: local optimization can hurt the whole system

failure_modes.mode: Complexity Talk Excuses Lack of Operational Choice
failure_modes.description: Complexity talk can become an excuse for avoiding concrete levers, checkpoints, or experiments.
failure_modes.mitigation: Convert complexity language into concrete levers and checkpoints: for every 'it's complex' statement, require naming the specific variable to watch and the experiment to run.
failure_modes.source_quote: Complexity Talk Excuses Lack of Operational Choice

premortem_questions.description: Which feedback loops or delays could make a small move create an outsized effect later?
premortem_questions.source_quote: feedback loops and delays that produce emergent effects

premortem_questions.description: What second-order or third-order dominoes are we ignoring?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Visualize All the Dominoes

premortem_questions.description: What concrete lever or experiment would still improve this system under uncertainty?
premortem_questions.source_quote: concrete levers, checkpoints, or experiments

heuristics.description: Map causal chains and feedback loops before committing to a fix.
heuristics.source_quote: Thinking in Systems Maps

heuristics.description: Start with the big picture before diving into local details.
heuristics.source_quote: The Big Picture First Approach

heuristics.description: Stress-test edge cases instead of trusting the base case alone.
heuristics.source_quote: Stress-Test Edge Cases

danger_when: Complexity language is being used to avoid naming concrete levers, checkpoints, or experiments.
The case is stable and sequential enough that a simpler one-step causal model already explains the decision.
non-linear-dynamics.name: Non Linear Dynamics

select_when: Completion and synchronization matter more than winning an argument, and the team needs a clearer split between content and relationship data.
Hard conversations are being distorted by threat, defensiveness, or status pressure rather than by missing intelligence alone.
The next move requires directness without infringement so that truth, needs, and trade-offs can be made discussable.
A discussion needs language that expands shared understanding instead of reinforcing polluted, defensive, or adversarial framing.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can soften conflict language enough to avoid argument while still failing to make the real bou...
failure_modes.description: Teams can soften conflict language enough to avoid argument while still failing to make the real boundary or trade-off discussable.
failure_modes.mitigation: After collaborative dialogue, verify that the real trade-off or boundary was stated, not just softened into vagueness: ask each party to restate what was decided and what was given up.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when teams smooth over the sharp edge of disagreement so much that real boundaries, trade-offs, or accountability requests never become explicit.

failure_modes.mode: Rigidity and Inflexibility
failure_modes.description: A favored interpretation can harden into rigidity and block the open, non-judgmental listening the model depends on.
failure_modes.mitigation: Practice active listening audits: periodically check whether each participant can accurately paraphrase the other's position before responding to it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Rigidity and Inflexibility

failure_modes.mode: Suboptimal Decisions in Groups
failure_modes.description: Groups can mistake collaborative tone for good decision hygiene and still drift into groupthink or weak challenge.
failure_modes.mitigation: Distinguish process tone from decision rigor: after any consensus reached through collaborative communication, run a quick devil's advocate check to confirm the group did not drift into unchallenged agreement.
failure_modes.source_quote: Suboptimal Decisions in Groups

premortem_questions.description: Are we trying to convince each other that we are right, or are we trying to hear each perspective to figure out what is true and what to do?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are we going to try to convince each other that we are right or are we going to open-mindedly hear each other’s perspectives to try to figure out what’s true and what to do about it?

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe for the opposite viewpoint to be true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would we have to believe for the opposite viewpoint to be true?

premortem_questions.description: If our audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If our audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?

heuristics.description: Check both the content level and the relationship level before deciding what the conversation actually needs next.
heuristics.source_quote: Always operate at two levels: the analytical content and the relationship tension.

heuristics.description: Lead with objective observation and impact before inviting joint problem solving.
heuristics.source_quote: Provide Information ... Detail the Impact ... Extend an Invitation

heuristics.description: Use the most respectful interpretation available so the exchange can stay direct without becoming needlessly adversarial.
heuristics.source_quote: The Most Respectful Interpretation (MRI)

danger_when: The conversation is being softened so much that real boundaries, trade-offs, or accountability requests never become explicit.
The model is being used as vague harmony language rather than as disciplined clarity about observation, impact, and next-step collaboration.
non-violent-communication.name: Non Violent Communication

select_when: The obligation is already known, but the controls, checkpoints, ownership, or operational boundaries needed to fulfill it are still vague.
Failure would come from handoff ambiguity between people, systems, or process stages rather than from missing intent.
Decision-makers need compliance, reliability, and traceability at the same time, not as separate afterthoughts.
A team must translate a stated objective into concrete process boundaries, control points, and enforcement paths.

failure_modes.mode: Structuring the investigation or mapping the controls/data gathering to yield a pre-determined conclusion or hypothesis.
failure_modes.description: Confirmation bias can structure the map to confirm the preferred conclusion instead of exposing the real obligation gap.
failure_modes.mitigation: Have an independent reviewer audit the map against the raw obligation source to catch any framing that conveniently narrows or reinterprets what is actually required.
failure_modes.source_quote: Structuring the investigation or mapping the controls/data gathering to yield a pre-determined conclusion or hypothesis.

failure_modes.mode: Jumping into analysis or solution design (Controls) without adequate diagnostic work.
failure_modes.description: Rushing the map can push the team into control design before the real obligation is diagnosed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Sequence the work: complete obligation diagnosis before designing controls, and flag any team that starts building controls before the obligation scope is locked.
failure_modes.source_quote: Jumping into analysis or solution design (Controls) without adequate diagnostic work.

failure_modes.mode: Scope inflation can bury the real obligation under mountains of low-value mapping work.
failure_modes.description: Scope inflation can bury the real obligation under mountains of low-value mapping work.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a scope boundary before mapping starts: define which obligations are in scope and timebox the mapping effort to prevent completionism from burying the critical gaps.
failure_modes.source_quote: The user gathers a mountain of data or performs needless analyses, wasting time and effort without advancing the thinking.

failure_modes.mode: Neglecting the human element can make a clean control map fail at implementation time.
failure_modes.description: Neglecting the human element can make a clean control map fail at implementation time.
failure_modes.mitigation: Include implementation owners and human-factors review in the control design: for every control, name who operates it and test whether they can realistically execute it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Over-focusing on the analytical, rational, or technical content ... while neglecting essential personal and organizational data

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask the crucial pre-mortem question: What would you have to believe? to spell out all assumptions implicit in a perspective.

premortem_questions.description: How does what you're doing solve the problem? Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How does what you’re doing solve the problem? Is it the most important thing you could be doing right now?

heuristics.description: Define the role and its constraints explicitly before the system starts acting.
heuristics.source_quote: the system prompt defines the agent’s role ... and its backstory ... Explicitly documenting and encoding behavioral tendencies

heuristics.description: Use the goal-oriented seven-step process so obligation definition, mapping, synthesis, and execution do not blur together.
heuristics.source_quote: The process starts with a precise definition of the problem ... followed by disaggregation and hypothesis generation ... Synthesizing findings and then structuring the execution

heuristics.description: Apply MECE disaggregation so obligations break into distinct and complete control areas.
heuristics.source_quote: the principle of Mutually Exclusive and Collectively Exhaustive (MECE) is fundamental

heuristics.description: Use the TOSCA problem statement to define trouble, owner, success criteria, constraints, and actors before mapping controls.
heuristics.source_quote: TOSCA Problem Statement ... Trouble, Owner, Success criteria, Constraints, and Actors.

danger_when: The situation is novel enough that forcing an existing control map would create false pattern recognition or suppress needed learning.
The mapping exercise is becoming documentation theater that no longer reflects live decision points, real obligations, or actual operating behavior.
The team still has to discover what the obligation really is before formalizing controls and accountabilities.
obligations-controls-mapping.name: Obligations Controls Mapping

select_when: A decision is carrying extra assumptions, variables, or explanatory machinery that may be making the answer feel smarter without making it truer.
Several explanations fit the facts, and the team needs a disciplined reason to start with the one that asks the least of reality.
A plan or diagnosis is becoming neat, plausible, and wrong because complexity is being rewarded more than evidential fit.
The next move should prune unsupported story structure before more analysis compounds it.

failure_modes.mode: the simplest explanation is not always the correct one if reality is genuinely complex
failure_modes.description: The model fails when simplicity is chosen for elegance or comfort instead of because it best fits the evidence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test simplicity against evidence fit, not against aesthetic appeal: the simpler explanation wins only when it accounts for the same observations as the complex one.
failure_modes.source_quote: the simplest explanation is not always the correct one if reality is genuinely complex

failure_modes.mode: Danger When the Explanation Is Neat, Plausible, and Wrong
failure_modes.description: A neat, plausible story can crowd out a truer explanation when the team rewards coherence more than evidential support.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge coherence by asking what disconfirming data would look like: a neat story that cannot specify what would falsify it is probably rewarding coherence over truth.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When the Explanation Is Neat, Plausible, and Wrong

failure_modes.mode: prefer the explanation that makes the fewest assumptions
failure_modes.description: Occam's Razor breaks when hidden assumptions are smuggled in under the label of simplification.
failure_modes.mitigation: Surface hidden assumptions: before accepting a 'simple' explanation, list every implicit premise it requires and check whether those premises have been verified.
failure_modes.source_quote: prefer the explanation that makes the fewest assumptions

premortem_questions.description: What assumptions are we adding that the evidence has not earned?
premortem_questions.source_quote: the explanation that requires the fewest assumptions is usually the best

premortem_questions.description: What would make the simpler explanation too simple to be true here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: the simplest explanation is not always the correct one if reality is genuinely complex

premortem_questions.description: What disconfirming evidence would force us to keep the harder causal structure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: doesn't eliminate the need for evidence or testing

heuristics.description: Start with the explanation that fits the facts while adding the fewest unsupported assumptions.
heuristics.source_quote: when presented with multiple explanations for a phenomenon, one should select the explanation that makes the fewest assumptions

heuristics.description: Use simplicity as a pruning rule, not as a substitute for testing.
heuristics.source_quote: Occam's Razor doesn't eliminate the need for evidence or testing

heuristics.description: Strip away ornamental complexity before adding more analysis, models, or variables.
heuristics.source_quote: avoid overcomplicating explanations or systems

danger_when: The simplest explanation is being chosen for comfort, speed, or elegance even though the evidence points to interacting causes or real complexity.
The situation is genuinely multicausal and the team is using simplicity rhetoric to avoid tracing the harder causal structure.
occams-razor.name: Occams Razor

select_when: A team wants to add one more initiative even though the same people, budget, or launch window are needed by a higher-leverage move.
A default renewal, inherited project, or existing channel is being treated as free because the next-best use of the same resources has not been named.
Resources are scarce and each yes displaces a real alternative in capital, attention, headcount, or calendar time.
Trade-offs span more than money and the decision should price lost optionality, learning, morale, or trust rather than only explicit cash cost.

failure_modes.mode: Foreclosure hidden by commitment
failure_modes.description: Commitment can hide what becomes impossible once resources, time, or attention are locked into one path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Before committing, explicitly list what becomes impossible: name the specific projects, options, or capabilities that this commitment forecloses.
failure_modes.source_quote: Foreclosure hidden by commitment

failure_modes.mode: Decision Paralysis
failure_modes.description: Decision paralysis can keep the team comparing endless alternatives instead of acting on a reversible test.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision timer for reversible choices: if the decision is easily reversible, cap comparison time and run a small experiment instead of endlessly modeling alternatives.
failure_modes.source_quote: Decision Paralysis

failure_modes.mode: Cheap add-on displaces the real priority
failure_modes.description: A request can look cheap in isolation while still stealing the same launch window, engineering time, or management attention from the highest-value move.
failure_modes.mitigation: Price requests in the same currency as the best alternative: express the cost of this commitment in terms of what the same resources would deliver on the highest-value alternative.
failure_modes.source_quote: Cheap add-on displaces the real priority

premortem_questions.description: Before approval: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months.
premortem_questions.source_quote: Before approving: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months.

premortem_questions.description: Which comparison in this plan is pretending to be irreversible even though we could run it as a bounded test first, and what learning are we giving up by waiting for certainty before acting?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which comparison in this plan is pretending to be irreversible even though we could run it as a bounded test first, and what learning are we giving up by waiting for certainty before acting?

premortem_questions.description: Which initiative in this plan looks inexpensive only because its cost is being charged to the same people, budget, or launch window needed by a more valuable move?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Which initiative in this plan looks inexpensive only because its cost is being charged to the same people, budget, or launch window needed by a more valuable move, and what exactly are we delaying by saying yes?

heuristics.description: Upgrade simple pro-con thinking into a comparison that includes the implicit costs of the best foregone option.
heuristics.source_quote: Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) Upgrade

heuristics.description: Use opportunity cost as a prioritization filter so scarce time and attention flow toward the highest-leverage work.
heuristics.source_quote: The Prioritization Filter

heuristics.description: Force every recommendation to answer compared to what before calling it attractive.
heuristics.source_quote: Compared to what?

danger_when: The option set is still poorly framed, so comparison would create false precision around the wrong alternatives or objective function.
The analysis is loading the comparison with unrealistic alternatives that are not genuinely available, fundable, or executable.
opportunity-cost.name: Opportunity Cost
reframing_when: The question asks 'how should we do X' without asking 'should we be doing X at all given what else we could do with the same resources' — the displaced alternative is invisible.
The question treats a continuation or default path as free when the real cost is the best alternative use of the same resources, attention, or time.
The question frames the decision in terms of explicit costs only and suppresses the implicit costs — the non-financial opportunity costs (optionality, learning, trust) are hidden.
opportunity-cost.reframing_move: Surface the displaced alternative: ask 'compared to what?' — the question shifts from 'should we do X' to 'what is the best alternative use of the same resources, and is X better than that?'

select_when: A plan sounds coherent internally but still needs an outside-view check, a pre-mortem, or a reference class before commitment.
The forecast is being carried by conviction, mission clarity, or proposer charisma more than by downside imagination and execution assumptions.
The team is preparing in detail, but that detail may be narrowing attention instead of improving realism about external events.
The next useful move is to expose underweighted downside paths before the current confidence hardens into commitment.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when enthusiasm is being used as evidence
failure_modes.description: Enthusiasm, charisma, or emotional mission clarity can be mistaken for evidence that the plan, timeline, or forecast is sound.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the messenger's conviction from the plan's evidence: require enthusiasm to be paired with outside-view base rates before it influences commitment.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when enthusiasm is being used as evidence

failure_modes.mode: Detailed, step-by-step preparation ... can actually amplify the planning fallacy
failure_modes.description: Detailed preparation can amplify the planning fallacy when it narrows attention and hides external events or downside paths.
failure_modes.mitigation: After detailed planning, run a reference-class check: compare the plan's timeline and cost to the historical distribution of similar projects, not to the team's inside-view narrative.
failure_modes.source_quote: Detailed, step-by-step preparation ... can actually amplify the planning fallacy

failure_modes.mode: objective forecasting
failure_modes.description: The model fails when confidence about conjunctive success replaces explicit outside-view and downside checks.
failure_modes.mitigation: Decompose conjunctive assumptions explicitly: list every condition that must hold simultaneously for the plan to succeed, estimate each independently, and multiply — the product is usually far lower than gut confidence.
failure_modes.source_quote: objective forecasting

premortem_questions.description: What outside-view check would most likely cut this forecast down to reality?
premortem_questions.source_quote: outside-view check

premortem_questions.description: What downside path are we underweighting because the plan feels vivid and internally coherent?
premortem_questions.source_quote: needs explicit downside imagination

premortem_questions.description: If the proposer's conviction were removed, what evidence would still support this timeline or success probability?
premortem_questions.source_quote: strength of conviction, charisma of the proposer

heuristics.description: Run an outside-view check, reference class, or pre-mortem before real commitment.
heuristics.source_quote: outside-view check, a pre-mortem, or a reference class

heuristics.description: Treat detailed preparation as something to audit, not as proof that the plan is realistic.
heuristics.source_quote: Detailed, step-by-step preparation ... can actually amplify the planning fallacy

heuristics.description: Separate emotional clarity from execution realism by demanding downside imagination before commitment.
heuristics.source_quote: explicit downside imagination

danger_when: The group is using anti-optimism language to crush initiative even though the current question mainly needs execution discipline, not more downside narration.
The plan already has explicit outside-view checks, reversal conditions, and downside scenarios, so another bias warning would mostly repeat known information.
optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy.name: Optimism Bias And Planning Fallacy

select_when: The team can name what it is actually maximizing or minimizing instead of treating a vague aspiration as the objective.
Real constraints like time, capital, risk, or operating bottlenecks materially change which option is best.
A recommendation needs to make trade-offs explicit by stating what is being protected, what is being sacrificed, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
The work is drowning in detail and needs a leverage-centered frame that filters nonessential effort while keeping the true target visible.

failure_modes.mode: Optimization drifts into proxy worship when a measurable indicator or elegant model output replaces ...
failure_modes.description: Optimization drifts into proxy worship when a measurable indicator or elegant model output replaces the real outcome.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the objective function: verify that the metric being optimized is still a faithful proxy for the real outcome, and sunset it if the gap has grown.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the proxy becomes the target: Danger when teams optimize a measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output that no longer tracks the real outcome they claim to care about.

failure_modes.mode: Optimization necessitates filtering out the nonessential to focus on the core leverage points.
failure_modes.description: Teams boil the ocean when they optimize for completeness instead of filtering toward the core leverage points.
failure_modes.mitigation: Apply the Pareto filter early: identify the 2-3 levers that drive most of the outcome and defer completeness until the core leverage is captured.
failure_modes.source_quote: Optimization necessitates filtering out the nonessential to focus on the core leverage points.

failure_modes.mode: A favorite framework can become a shackle when it is reused on a problem whose assumptions no longer...
failure_modes.description: A favorite framework can become a shackle when it is reused on a problem whose assumptions no longer fit.
failure_modes.mitigation: Mandate a periodic framework rotation: after two cycles with the same optimization model, force a pass with a structurally different approach to detect assumption drift.
failure_modes.source_quote: Applying a favorite or familiar mental model ... to every problem, even novel ones, simply because it's readily available.

premortem_questions.description: What are we actually maximizing or minimizing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when the team can say what it is actually maximizing or minimizing and can distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.

premortem_questions.description: What are we sacrificing, what are we protecting, and which metric wins when goals conflict?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when trade-offs must be made explicit: Best when a recommendation should state what is being sacrificed, what is being protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Ask: "Is the worst case bad enough?"

heuristics.description: Name the objective function before debating options so the comparison is anchored to the real target.
heuristics.source_quote: Optimization is intrinsically linked to defining clear goals.

heuristics.description: Make constraints operational instead of decorative so the recommendation fits actual limits on time, capital, risk, or bottlenecks.
heuristics.source_quote: Works when constraints are real rather than decorative.

heuristics.description: Reduce extraneous complexity so effort concentrates on leverage points rather than on low-value completeness.
heuristics.source_quote: Optimization necessitates filtering out the nonessential to focus on the core leverage points.

danger_when: The measurable proxy is drifting away from the real outcome, so local optimization would improve the dashboard while damaging the actual objective.
The situation is genuinely novel or uncertainty-dominated enough that strict maximization language will hide unknowns and discourage resilience thinking.
optimization-theory.name: Optimization Theory

select_when: Uncertainty is high, early commitment is expensive, and cheap experiments can buy learning before an irreversible call.
Decisions are reversible enough that preserving multiple paths creates information value rather than just indecision.
Downside can be capped while upside remains open across several plausible futures.
The problem has collapsed into binary framing, but the real move is to create more viable paths before choosing.

failure_modes.mode: Analysis Paralysis
failure_modes.description: Analysis paralysis can keep the team waiting for perfect information instead of learning through reversible movement.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set a decision deadline that forces commitment before analysis consumes more value than the option preserves; make the deadline visible and non-negotiable.
failure_modes.source_quote: Analysis Paralysis

failure_modes.mode: The Paradox of Choice (Decision Fatigue)
failure_modes.description: The paradox of choice can consume cognitive energy and leave the system selecting no option at all.
failure_modes.mitigation: Reduce cognitive load by pre-filtering: eliminate clearly dominated options early so the remaining set is small enough for genuine deliberation.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Paradox of Choice (Decision Fatigue)

failure_modes.mode: Anchoring Bias
failure_modes.description: Anchoring on the first option presented can quietly shrink the perceived option set and bias later evaluation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Actively generate alternatives before evaluating: brainstorm options independently from the first one presented to prevent anchoring from shrinking the choice set.
failure_modes.source_quote: Anchoring Bias

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Is the worst case bad enough?"

premortem_questions.description: Would we regret making or not making this decision when we look back later?
premortem_questions.source_quote: one visualizes themselves at age 80 and asks if they would regret making (or not making) a specific decision

premortem_questions.description: Have we generated enough options before narrowing the choice set?
premortem_questions.source_quote: generate many options before selecting among them

heuristics.description: Use the 3+ principle to force more than a binary choice on important decisions.
heuristics.source_quote: The 3+ Principle

heuristics.description: Invent first and decide later so evaluation does not choke off option generation too early.
heuristics.source_quote: Invent First, Decide Later

heuristics.description: Bias toward action on reversible decisions because they buy information at low downside cost.
heuristics.source_quote: Reversible Decisions Bias

danger_when: Optionality has become a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit.
Maintaining optionality is consuming cash, attention, or organizational simplicity that would be better spent on a chosen path.
optionality.name: Optionality
reframing_when: The question forces a premature commitment to a single path when the situation has enough uncertainty that preserving multiple paths would be more valuable than choosing one now.
The question presents only two options when the 3+ principle suggests the problem isn't fully understood until at least three genuine alternatives exist.
The question frames the decision as irreversible when it may actually be reversible — the framing forecloses cheap experiments that could buy learning.
optionality.reframing_move: Reopen the option space: ask 'what are three genuinely different ways we could approach this?' and 'what cheap experiment would tell us which path is right before we commit?' — the question shifts from 'which option' to 'how do we learn which option'.

select_when: A small subset of causes, customers, tasks, or constraints appears to drive a disproportionate share of the result.
Time or attention is scarce enough that the main problem is deciding what not to work on.
A complex issue tree needs pruning so analysis concentrates on the branches most likely to move the answer.
The team can revisit the prioritization as the distribution shifts instead of defending the current vital few as permanent truth.

failure_modes.mode: Teams can neglect emerging products, markets, or drivers by optimizing only around the current high-...
failure_modes.description: Teams can neglect emerging products, markets, or drivers by optimizing only around the current high-impact few.
failure_modes.mitigation: Schedule a periodic scan for emerging drivers: review whether new products, markets, or inputs have crossed the threshold into the vital few since the last assessment.
failure_modes.source_quote: A common anti-pattern is focusing solely on the 20% of products that generate 80% of the revenue, which can lead to neglecting emerging products or markets that could be crucial for future growth.

failure_modes.mode: Pareto can become a lazy excuse when people invoke 80/20 language without actually measuring leverag...
failure_modes.description: Pareto can become a lazy excuse when people invoke 80/20 language without actually measuring leverage.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require measurement behind every 80/20 claim: do not accept Pareto language without data showing which inputs actually drive disproportionate output.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When the Vital Few Become a Lazy Excuse: The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage.

failure_modes.mode: Premature pruning can discard lower-signal branches before they have a fair chance to prove future i...
failure_modes.description: Premature pruning can discard lower-signal branches before they have a fair chance to prove future importance.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect experimental branches with a small dedicated budget so promising long-tail options get a fair trial before being pruned by current-state concentration analysis.
failure_modes.source_quote: Prematurely trimming ideas based on early, low-impact metrics can lead to abandoning breakthrough concepts before they have a chance to prove their value.

premortem_questions.description: How do you prioritize what to do and what not to do?
premortem_questions.source_quote: How do you prioritize what to do and what not to do?

premortem_questions.description: What is the low-impact 80% that can be minimized, streamlined, or eliminated?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What is the low-impact 80% that can be minimized, streamlined, or eliminated to gain greater clarity and freedom?

premortem_questions.description: Are we trimming based on likelihood of impact, or because some areas are simply easier or less emotionally taxing to ignore?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are we trimming based on likelihood of impact, or because some areas are simply easier or less emotionally taxing to ignore?

heuristics.description: Search for the vital few by pruning low-impact branches so scarce attention goes to the biggest drivers.
heuristics.source_quote: When a problem is disaggregated into many branches of potential causes or solutions, the 80/20 Rule dictates cutting off the low-impact branches.

heuristics.description: Treat the current leverage pattern as revisitable rather than permanent so the focus can change when the distribution shifts.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when prioritization can be revisited as the distribution shifts.

heuristics.description: Measure impact before invoking Pareto language so the model sharpens focus instead of rationalizing convenience.
heuristics.source_quote: The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage.

danger_when: The 80/20 framing is being used as rhetoric without actual leverage measurement, making it a lazy excuse to ignore inconvenient work.
Emerging drivers or long-horizon bets are still too early to measure cleanly, so aggressive pruning would likely discard future-important factors.
pareto-principle.name: Pareto Principle

select_when: Early choices are constraining current options more than people realize, so history matters more than present-day preference alone.
A system keeps reproducing old behavior despite new intent because inherited grooves are stronger than the latest goals.
Changing course requires more than a better argument because habits, standards, interfaces, or prior investments are locking the system into its route.
A migration or redesign looks clean on paper, but installed constraints are already narrowing the real path out.

failure_modes.mode: Works when a system keeps reproducing old behavior despite new intent.
failure_modes.description: A system can keep reproducing old behavior even after intent changes because historical grooves are stronger than the latest plan.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map the switching costs explicitly: for each path-dependent behavior, estimate what it would actually cost to change, and compare that cost to the value of the alternative.
failure_modes.source_quote: Works when a system keeps reproducing old behavior despite new intent.

failure_modes.mode: Path-lock can become a self-justifying excuse for not redesigning anything that is actually within r...
failure_modes.description: Path-lock can become a self-justifying excuse for not redesigning anything that is actually within reach to change.
failure_modes.mitigation: Challenge fatalism with a 'what if we started today?' exercise: design the ideal approach from scratch, then compare it to the inherited path to see what the gap reveals.
failure_modes.source_quote: path-dependence conflicts with status-quo-bias (status quo bias) when a correctly diagnosed path-lock is used as an excuse to avoid redesign rather than as a genuine structural constraint.

failure_modes.mode: Historical commitment can distort evidence reading so decision-makers selectively notice what justif...
failure_modes.description: Historical commitment can distort evidence reading so decision-makers selectively notice what justifies staying on the current path.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit evidence reading for confirmation of the status quo: ask whether the team would interpret the same data differently if they were not already committed to this path.
failure_modes.source_quote: path-dependence conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when decision-makers selectively credit evidence that justifies staying on the current path rather than fairly evaluating the case for switching.

premortem_questions.description: Which early choice is constraining current options more than people realize?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when early choices are constraining current options more than people realize.

premortem_questions.description: What current redesign assumption is treating the inherited estate like a clean scheduling problem when installed constraints already narrow the real path out?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when a migration plan treats the inherited estate like a clean scheduling problem.

premortem_questions.description: If we tried to change course now, which habits, standards, interfaces, or prior investments would fight us first?
premortem_questions.source_quote: It is a strong fit when habits, standards, interfaces, or prior investments are locking the system into the current route.

heuristics.description: Map inherited constraints explicitly so the team can tell the difference between present preference and historical route-lock.
heuristics.source_quote: Path dependence is strongest when the real driver is inherited architecture, history, or commitment rather than present-day preference alone.

heuristics.description: Stress-test redesign plans against installed customers, contracts, interfaces, and operating dependencies before calling the path reversible.
heuristics.source_quote: The plan underprices installed customers, contracts, interfaces, and operational dependencies that already narrow the real path out.

heuristics.description: Use path-lock diagnosis to target redesignable constraints rather than to excuse staying with the inherited route by default.
heuristics.source_quote: Path dependence conflicts with status-quo-bias when a correctly diagnosed path-lock is used as an excuse to avoid redesign rather than as a genuine structural constraint.

danger_when: Path-lock is being diagnosed so broadly that it becomes an excuse for not redesigning anything that could actually change.
The model is being used to romanticize history when the live issue is really current economics, current incentives, or an untested assumption.
path-dependence.name: Path Dependence
reframing_when: The question treats the current situation as the natural or optimal state when it is actually the accumulated result of historical decisions that could have gone differently — the path is invisible.
The question frames the available options as inherent to the problem when they are actually constrained by sunk costs, prior commitments, or institutional inertia from the path already traveled.

The question assumes continuity ('given where we are, what should we do next?') without asking whether the path that brought us here has created structural constraints that should themselves be questioned.
The question treats switching costs as absolute barriers rather than as path-created constraints that may be worth paying to reach a better frame.
path-dependence.reframing_move: Before accepting the current constraints as given, ask: 'Which of these constraints exist because of the path we have already traveled, and what options would open up if we treated the path as a choice rather than a fact?' — the path-dependence reframe makes historical contingency visible.

select_when: The current conclusion is vulnerable to inside-view confidence and needs independent challenge before it hardens into a decision.
A team is approaching consensus too smoothly, and dissent, critique, or outside scrutiny may surface missing assumptions.
An important judgment would improve if alternative perspectives attacked the reasoning before reality does.
The thinker needs structured feedback that tests the logic, incentives, and blind spots in the current view.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Feedback Becomes Social Comfort
failure_modes.description: Peer review fails when the room rewards agreement and reassurance more than independent challenge.
failure_modes.mitigation: Structure review for genuine adversarial challenge: require reviewers to state the strongest argument against the proposal before offering support.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Feedback Becomes Social Comfort

failure_modes.mode: The Wisdom of Crowds fails when people are not making independent judgments
failure_modes.description: Status and consensus pressure can make outside review ceremonial instead of genuinely adversarial.
failure_modes.mitigation: Anonymize or randomize reviewer input to reduce status effects: collect critiques in writing before group discussion so seniority cannot filter the challenge.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Wisdom of Crowds fails when people are not making independent judgments

failure_modes.mode: the key is not more opinions but better challenge
failure_modes.description: Perspective collection becomes wasteful when critique stays vague instead of testing the assumptions that would change the answer.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require specific, assumption-level critique: reject vague 'looks good' or 'needs work' feedback and insist reviewers name the exact assumption they would change and why.
failure_modes.source_quote: the key is not more opinions but better challenge

premortem_questions.description: What might be wrong with our current view?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What might I be wrong about?

premortem_questions.description: What assumption fails first if our conclusion is weaker than we think?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What assumptions is this conclusion resting on?

premortem_questions.description: Who would disagree for substantive reasons, and what would they say before we hear our own explanation again?
