failure_modes.mitigation: Set a backup threshold: define how many alternative analyses or perspectives are needed, and stop accumulating once the diminishing-returns point is reached.
failure_modes.source_quote: An over-reliance on seeking redundant analyses or perspectives can lead to getting mired in analysis paralysis

failure_modes.mode: When planning, people overestimate the overall probability of success for complex conjunctive events
failure_modes.description: A system can still be fragile if planners assume a conjunctive chain will work without enough redundancy in risk assessment.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check conjunctive chains for single points of failure: if the plan requires too many sequential successes, add redundancy at the weakest link rather than spreading it evenly.
failure_modes.source_quote: When planning, people overestimate the overall probability of success for complex conjunctive events

premortem_questions.description: What exact failure mode justifies this extra layer, copy, or backup path?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger When Duplication Is Added Without a Clear Failure Mode

premortem_questions.description: Would anything important be lost if this duplicate element were removed?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this was cut, would anything be lost?

premortem_questions.description: Are we adding redundancy that changes survival or only adding drag that feels safer?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when repetition or duplication increases robustness more than it increases drag

heuristics.description: Add redundancy only when a named failure mode makes the backup path materially valuable.
heuristics.source_quote: single-point failure is unacceptable

heuristics.description: Use repeated review or rehearsal to strengthen retention, not to create framework soup.
heuristics.source_quote: reviewing information multiple times strengthens neural connections

heuristics.description: Trim duplicate content aggressively when it increases cognitive load more than resilience.
heuristics.source_quote: presenting information that is simultaneously available in another format is harmful

danger_when: Duplication is being added by habit without a clear failure mode that justifies its cost.
Repeated analysis or repeated content is creating cognitive overload instead of real protection.
redundancy.name: Redundancy

select_when: The first story is trapping the decision by making one option feel inevitable or impossible.
The same facts could support different actions depending on viewpoint.
Leaders need a deliberate frame shift that changes what counts as success or what variables matter.
The team is stuck in a false binary that a better frame could reopen.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when the first story is trapping the decision.
failure_modes.description: A plan can look inevitable only because the current story hides other valid lenses and success criteria.
failure_modes.mitigation: After reframing, check whether the new lens opened genuinely different options or merely relabeled the same plan: if the action set didn't change, the reframe was decorative.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when the first story is trapping the decision.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when reframing becomes euphemism.
failure_modes.description: Reframing can become euphemism when teams rename a hard reality without improving the truth surface or option set.
failure_modes.mitigation: Demand truth-surface improvement: accept a reframe only if it makes previously hidden trade-offs, risks, or stakeholder concerns more visible, not less.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when reframing becomes euphemism.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when reframing keeps resetting the conversation instead of forcing a decision test.
failure_modes.description: New language can turn into avoidance when leaders keep resetting the conversation instead of specifying the next decision test.
failure_modes.mitigation: Pair any new language with a concrete decision test: specify what evidence would confirm or disconfirm the reframed hypothesis so the conversation cannot keep resetting indefinitely.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when reframing keeps resetting the conversation instead of forcing a decision test.

premortem_questions.description: What different action becomes reasonable if we look at the same facts through a user, system, risk, or trade-off lens?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when the same facts support different actions depending on viewpoint.

premortem_questions.description: Which binary in our language is fake, and what conditional or segmented option is hidden by it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when the team is stuck in a false binary.

premortem_questions.description: What evidence or trade-off would actually make this new frame better instead of merely nicer sounding?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger when reframing keeps resetting the conversation instead of forcing a decision test.

heuristics.description: Change the question before changing the answer when the original framing is making one path look falsely inevitable.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when the first recommendation sounds inevitable because of how the problem was posed.

heuristics.description: Try alternative lenses that change what counts as success, risk, or reversibility.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when leaders need a deliberate frame shift.

heuristics.description: Use reframing only when it sharpens evidence, trade-offs, or decision quality rather than softening reality.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when reframing becomes euphemism.

danger_when: Reframing is becoming euphemism that renames a hard reality without improving the option set or truth surface.
New language is being used to postpone a decision instead of forcing an evidence or trade-off test.
reframing-perspective.name: Reframing Perspective
reframing_when: The question is trapped in a single story that makes one option feel inevitable or impossible — a deliberate viewpoint shift would change what counts as success.
The same facts could support different actions depending on whose perspective the question is asked from, and the question locks in one perspective without examining others.
The question embeds a false binary that a better frame could reopen into a richer option space.
The question's implicit assumptions need to be surfaced through 'What would you have to believe?' to test whether the frame is valid.
reframing-perspective.reframing_move: Shift the viewpoint: ask the same question from a different stakeholder's perspective, or from the inverse — 'What would you have to believe for this framing to be correct?' surfaces assumptions that feel invisible from inside the original frame.

select_when: A result looks dramatic, but causality is weak and the team may be explaining variance with a confident story too early.
Decision quality depends on anchoring to base rates, longer-run distributions, and repeated measurement instead of the loudest recent datapoint.
Rewards, punishments, or process changes occurred next to an extreme outcome, so the team may over-credit the intervention.
The real question is whether performance shifted structurally or merely deviated from the mean for a while.

failure_modes.mode: Mistaking Noise for Signal
failure_modes.description: Teams can mistake noise for signal by inventing causes for an extreme result before checking whether anything structural changed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Before assigning causation to an extreme result, check whether the base rate alone could explain the reversion: run a simple pre-post comparison against the historical distribution.
failure_modes.source_quote: Mistaking Noise for Signal

failure_modes.mode: Over-reaction to Extremes
failure_modes.description: Over-reaction to extremes can make the loudest datapoint dominate judgment instead of the longer-run distribution.
failure_modes.mitigation: Dampen response to outliers: weight decisions toward the running average rather than the latest extreme datapoint, and require structural evidence before treating a spike as a trend.
failure_modes.source_quote: Over-reaction to Extremes

failure_modes.mode: Justifying Inaction
failure_modes.description: Regression-to-the-mean can be misused as a story for inaction when the mean itself has actually shifted downward.
failure_modes.mitigation: Verify the baseline: before invoking regression-to-the-mean as a reason to wait, check whether the mean itself has shifted — inaction is only justified if the long-run distribution is stable.
failure_modes.source_quote: Justifying Inaction

premortem_questions.description: A change without a reason for the change is not really a change?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "A change without a reason for the change is not really a change?"

premortem_questions.description: What is the base rate or historical likelihood of this extreme performance being sustained?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What is the base rate or historical likelihood of this extreme performance being sustained?"

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What would you have to believe?"

heuristics.description: Use the outside view and longer-run distributions before assigning a special causal story to an extreme result.
heuristics.source_quote: The Outside View

heuristics.description: Separate correlation from causation before crediting a nearby intervention for the result.
heuristics.source_quote: Separating Correlation from Causation

heuristics.description: Inspect the full range of outcomes, including catastrophe cases, instead of reasoning only from the mean.
heuristics.source_quote: Incorporate simulations to highlight the full range of outcomes possible.

danger_when: There is evidence that the underlying system, market, or process quality has genuinely changed, so waiting for reversion would ignore a new mean.
Regression-to-the-mean is being used as a lazy wait-and-see excuse when the deteriorating trend actually requires intervention.
regression-to-the-mean.name: Regression To The Mean

select_when: A meaningful decision is stuck because anticipated regret about action or inaction is distorting the choice.
The options are already reasonably clear, but the bottleneck is psychological commitment rather than missing information.
A long-term values-loaded decision needs a deliberate outside-view check on whether omission regret is the real danger.
The next move should surface anticipated regret explicitly without letting it replace probability, reversibility, or scenario analysis.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When Regret Language Romanticizes Recklessness
failure_modes.description: Regret language can romanticize recklessness when underprepared bets are treated as noble anti-regret action without doing the risk work.
failure_modes.mitigation: Pair anti-regret boldness with structured risk work: before acting to avoid future regret, complete a downside assessment and verify the bet is sized to survive failure.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When Regret Language Romanticizes Recklessness

failure_modes.mode: Ignoring Probabilities/Risks
failure_modes.description: Projected emotion can substitute for probability, data, and structured analysis when the model is applied to operational choices.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate emotional projection from analytical judgment: after imagining the regret scenario, return to probability estimates and expected-value calculations before deciding.
failure_modes.source_quote: Ignoring Probabilities/Risks

failure_modes.mode: Anticipatory Regret/Emotional Distortion
failure_modes.description: Vivid imagined futures can distort judgment and produce irrational hedges, indecision, or overestimation of success.
failure_modes.mitigation: Reality-test vivid futures: when a projected outcome feels overwhelming, check its base-rate probability and the actual reversibility of the decision before it distorts the choice.
failure_modes.source_quote: Anticipatory Regret/Emotional Distortion

premortem_questions.description: Are my feelings of regret driven by objective risk, or by the vividness of a potential negative scenario?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Are my feelings of regret driven by objective risks, or by the vividness of a potential negative scenario?

premortem_questions.description: What objective data do I have about this outcome independent of how I feel right now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What objective data (e.g., base rates, reference class forecasts) do I have about the likelihood of this outcome occurring, independent of how I feel right now?

premortem_questions.description: What third, fourth, or fifth options could reduce both short-term pain and long-term regret?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What are the third, fourth, or fifth options I haven't considered

heuristics.description: Use mental time travel to test whether omission regret is the real danger, not just the immediate discomfort of acting now.
heuristics.source_quote: Visualization and Mental Time Travel

heuristics.description: Pair regret minimization with premortem-style obstacle review so anticipated emotion improves preparation rather than replacing it.
heuristics.source_quote: emotionally driven premortem

heuristics.description: Anchor the inside view to the outside view before acting so regret does not outrun reality.
heuristics.source_quote: Anchor to the Outside View

danger_when: The decision is mainly operational, vendor-comparison, or analytically routine, where projected emotion would be a poor substitute for structured analysis.
Regret language is being used to romanticize recklessness, underprepared bets, or the avoidance of needed risk work.
regret-theory.name: Regret Theory
reframing_when: The question frames the decision in terms of expected outcomes without accounting for the asymmetric emotional weight of regret — losses from action versus losses from inaction are treated as equivalent when they are experienced very differently.
The question suppresses counterfactual thinking by focusing only on the chosen path, when explicitly imagining 'what will I regret not having done?' would reveal options the current frame hides.
The question escalates commitment to the current path because switching would force acknowledgment of a prior mistake — regret avoidance is driving the frame, not outcome quality.

The question treats risk symmetrically when the regret from commission (doing something wrong) and omission (failing to act) actually have very different psychological weights depending on the context.
regret-theory.reframing_move: Before choosing based on expected outcomes alone, ask: 'Which choice would I regret most if it turned out to be wrong — and is my frame designed to minimize regret or to maximize the outcome?' — the regret reframe exposes whether anticipated regret is silently shaping the frame.

select_when: External rules, enforcement priorities, or policy narratives are moving early enough that waiting for certainty would create strategic lag.
Weak signals need to be converted into scenarios, ownership questions, and preparatory options before the pressure becomes immediate.
Leaders must connect long-horizon regulatory shifts to present-day product, operating, or capital decisions.
The organization needs structured anticipation, not reactive compliance after the fact.

failure_modes.mode: horizon scanning becomes a slide ritual
failure_modes.description: Trend language without thresholds or owners creates slide theater rather than anticipatory readiness.
failure_modes.mitigation: Attach thresholds, owners, and decision triggers to every regulatory signal: if a trend has no named owner and no action boundary, it is slide theater.
failure_modes.source_quote: horizon scanning becomes a slide ritual

failure_modes.mode: confuses signal detection with signal interpretation
failure_modes.description: Headline overreaction mistakes signal detection for signal interpretation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate signal detection from signal interpretation: after identifying a regulatory change, run a structured impact assessment before reacting to the headline.
failure_modes.source_quote: confuses signal detection with signal interpretation

failure_modes.mode: confirmation bias
failure_modes.description: Confirmation-seeking can make the team read policy noise as support for a preferred roadmap.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit the scan for confirmation bias: require the team to include at least one regulatory development that contradicts the preferred roadmap and assess its plausibility honestly.
failure_modes.source_quote: confirmation bias

premortem_questions.description: What weak signal would actually force a change in product, operating, or capital decisions if it strengthened?
premortem_questions.source_quote: weak signals can be turned into explicit scenarios

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe about timing, enforcement, or ownership for this policy scenario to matter now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: If the worst regulatory case became the expected case, are we prepared enough today?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

heuristics.description: Use a knowns-and-unknowns matrix to turn vague regulatory uncertainty into named monitoring work.
heuristics.source_quote: Known Knowns, Known Unknowns, Unknown Unknowns

heuristics.description: Write an active hypothesis about the future state and test it instead of collecting undirected trend slides.
heuristics.source_quote: Hypothesis-Driven Problem Solving

heuristics.description: Connect long-horizon patterns to present-day triggers, owners, and response options before urgency arrives.
heuristics.source_quote: response triggers, or accountable owners

danger_when: Horizon scanning has become a trend-slide ritual without thresholds, response triggers, or accountable owners.
The team is overreacting to headlines that have not yet become structurally meaningful.
regulatory-horizon-scanning.name: Regulatory Horizon Scanning

select_when: A judgment is being driven by surface similarity or a vivid category match rather than by rates, frequencies, or causal detail.
People are substituting the question 'What does this resemble?' for the harder question 'What is statistically or causally likely here?'
The risk is that a compelling pattern match will crowd out denominator discipline.
A decision maker is intuitively classifying a case before checking whether the reference class actually supports that classification.
A vivid story, category label, or familiar prototype is overpowering statistical reasoning in a forecast or judgment.
A recommendation depends on forcing a handoff from intuitive categorization to explicit outside-view checking before action.
Problem solvers are at risk of declaring that they have seen this pattern before and skipping verification of whether the old frame still fits.

failure_modes.mode: A vivid pattern match can crowd out base-rate and frequency discipline, making the case feel more di...
failure_modes.description: A vivid pattern match can crowd out base-rate and frequency discipline, making the case feel more diagnostic than it is.
failure_modes.mitigation: Anchor to frequencies first: before accepting a pattern match, check the base-rate probability of the category in the relevant population.
failure_modes.source_quote: The representativeness heuristic often leads to base-rate neglect by focusing on specific details rather than statistical regularities.

failure_modes.mode: It judges the probability of an event by the degree to which it resembles a typical case.
failure_modes.description: Decision makers can confuse similarity with probability and treat a plausible story as if it were a strong forecast.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate plausibility from probability: require the team to distinguish 'this story sounds right' from 'this outcome is statistically likely' and flag any conflation.
failure_modes.source_quote: It judges the probability of an event by the degree to which it resembles a typical case.

failure_modes.mode: This incorrect pattern recognition risks disastrously wrong solutions.
failure_modes.description: Experts can force a new problem into an old pattern and end up with disastrously wrong solutions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test pattern fit explicitly: list the structural features the new case shares with the template and at least one it does not, then judge whether the mismatch is material.
failure_modes.source_quote: This incorrect pattern recognition risks disastrously wrong solutions.

failure_modes.mode: Smart teams can become especially vulnerable when they construct elegant narratives that hide their ...
failure_modes.description: Smart teams can become especially vulnerable when they construct elegant narratives that hide their own bias blind spot.
failure_modes.mitigation: Assign a designated base-rate advocate: when the team's narrative is compelling, one member's job is to surface the statistical denominator and test the story against it.
failure_modes.source_quote: The more cognitively sophisticated a person is, the better they are at constructing narratives and rationalizing data to support their preconceived beliefs

failure_modes.mode: Mitigating the risks involves deliberately engaging System 2 thinking to police quick judgments.
failure_modes.description: The heuristic is misread when all intuitive resemblance is treated as invalid instead of being checked against frequencies and causal evidence.
failure_modes.mitigation: Preserve the valid signal in intuitive resemblance: rather than discarding pattern recognition entirely, check it against frequency data and causal mechanisms to see what survives.
failure_modes.source_quote: Mitigating the risks involves deliberately engaging System 2 thinking to police quick judgments.

premortem_questions.description: What are the actual rates, frequencies, or reference-class outcomes for cases like this, not just the vivid pattern match?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Use the Outside View and Reference Classes.

premortem_questions.description: Which details in this story are making it feel representative, and how many of them are actually diagnostic?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The risk is judging by resemblance rather than by statistical reality.

premortem_questions.description: If this representative-looking pattern fails, what ignored denominator or low-probability scenario would explain the miss?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this representative pattern we identified fails, what previously ignored facts or assumptions would have caused the failure?

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe for the favored representative story to be truer than the base-rate evidence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough if this resemblance-based judgment is wrong?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: Why might my belief not be true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Why might my belief not be true?

heuristics.description: Treat intuitive resemblance as a hypothesis generator, not as the final probability estimate.
heuristics.source_quote: Mitigating the risks of RH involves deliberately engaging System 2 thinking.

heuristics.description: Check the denominator before trusting the story-like fit of a case to a category.
heuristics.source_quote: Reference Class Forecasting uses historical outcomes from similar problems to predict the current outcome.

heuristics.description: If the case feels obviously representative, pause and ask what causal or statistical evidence is still missing.
heuristics.source_quote: Explicitly challenge assumptions and broaden the mental toolbox.

heuristics.description: Force an outside-view pass with reference classes or base rates before acting on an intuitive category match.
heuristics.source_quote: Forcing the Outside View (Base Rates)

heuristics.description: Separate independent judgments before discussion so the first representative story does not anchor the whole group.
heuristics.source_quote: obtain separate, independent judgments on the issue before any discussion takes place

heuristics.description: Challenge the favored frame with thesis, antithesis, and synthesis instead of letting the first pattern match become the decision.
heuristics.source_quote: Adopt the dialectic standard: require every idea or hypothesis ... to be met with its antithesis

danger_when: The model would be used as a generic anti-intuition slogan instead of checking where resemblance is actually informative.
Teams are treating every pattern match as wrong even when there is strong base-rate and causal support for it.
The judgment already rests on explicit outside-view evidence and the model would only restate a bias warning without changing the decision path.
The model is being used to criticize simplification itself rather than the specific move from surface similarity to unsupported causal confidence.
representativeness-heuristic.name: Representativeness Heuristic

select_when: People, teams, or systems must absorb shocks without losing the ability to keep functioning and adapt intelligently afterward.
Setbacks are likely, time horizons are long, and recovery quality matters as much as immediate performance.
The environment is volatile enough that success depends on learning, reconfiguration, and disciplined bounce-back rather than one perfect plan.
The work needs deliberate recovery and adaptation discipline instead of reactive overexposure to overwhelm.

failure_modes.mode: Resilience gets distorted when pain endurance replaces redesign of the system producing preventable ...
failure_modes.description: Resilience gets distorted when pain endurance replaces redesign of the system producing preventable stress.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit whether the system producing the stress is fixable: before celebrating endurance, ask whether the source of strain should be redesigned rather than merely tolerated.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when resilience is romanticized as enduring pain indefinitely instead of redesigning the system that keeps producing preventable stress.

failure_modes.mode: Risk Blindness via Optimism
failure_modes.description: Optimistic explanation style can become risk blindness that produces bold forecasts and timid decisions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Balance optimism with honest downside assessment: pair a can-do explanation style with explicit acknowledgment of risks so bold forecasts do not crowd out prudent contingencies.
failure_modes.source_quote: Risk Blindness via Optimism

failure_modes.mode: Overthinking as a Substitute for Action
failure_modes.description: Overthinking can masquerade as progress while the underlying issue remains unsolved and action keeps slipping.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set an action deadline for reflective cycles: if analysis has not produced a concrete next step within a fixed window, force a reversible experiment to break the overthinking loop.
failure_modes.source_quote: Overthinking as a Substitute for Action

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: What evidence could possibly prove our current assumption wrong?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What evidence could possibly prove our current assumption wrong?

premortem_questions.description: Am I arguing with this data, or seeking to understand its perspective to figure out what is true?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Am I arguing with this data, or seeking to understand its perspective to figure out what is true?

heuristics.description: Use iterative problem solving that sets goals, identifies problems, diagnoses causes, designs changes, and then acts in sequence.
heuristics.source_quote: Iterative Problem Solving (Systems Thinking)

heuristics.description: Deliberate, then act so the move is simplified and envisioned before pressure forces reactive execution.
heuristics.source_quote: Deliberate, Then Act (Agency Principle)

heuristics.description: Use a one-day answer standard so the team can always summarize the best current problem understanding and solution path coherently.
heuristics.source_quote: The "One-Day Answer" Heuristic

danger_when: Resilience is being romanticized as enduring pain indefinitely instead of redesigning the system that keeps producing preventable stress.
Leaders are using resilience language to normalize overload, underinvestment, or chronic firefighting rather than fixing root causes.
resilience.name: Resilience

select_when: A plan needs explicit mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring rather than generic caution language.
The system is exposed to multiple interacting downside paths, and the next move should govern uncertainty rather than merely acknowledge it.
A decision is leaning on intuition or surface plausibility even though the stakes require a more structured downside scan.
The work would improve if risks were translated into concrete indicators, scenarios, and response triggers before commitment.

failure_modes.mode: Misapplication of risk assessment often involves excessive deliberation
failure_modes.description: Risk assessment becomes process theater when more deliberation is added without sharper thresholds, mitigations, or decisions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Tie every risk review to a threshold and a decision: if no risk item triggers a plan change, mitigation, or escalation, the assessment was process theater.
failure_modes.source_quote: Misapplication of risk assessment often involves excessive deliberation

failure_modes.mode: focusing on the wrong variables
failure_modes.description: The model fails when teams focus on tidy secondary variables while the main fragility drivers remain unexamined.
failure_modes.mitigation: Prioritize the primary fragility drivers: identify the 2-3 risks that would actually kill the project and focus assessment depth there instead of spreading attention evenly.
failure_modes.source_quote: focusing on the wrong variables

failure_modes.mode: overestimate the probability of conjunctive events
failure_modes.description: Downside governance breaks when optimism or conjunctive success thinking crowds out realistic failure paths.
failure_modes.mitigation: Stress-test the plan against realistic failure paths: require at least one scenario where conjunctive assumptions break, and verify the team has a response ready.
failure_modes.source_quote: overestimate the probability of conjunctive events

premortem_questions.description: What downside path is underweighted because the plan feels coherent from the inside?
premortem_questions.source_quote: underweighted downside scenarios

premortem_questions.description: What threshold or indicator would tell us this risk is becoming real rather than theoretical?
premortem_questions.source_quote: explicit mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring

premortem_questions.description: Which interdependency or unknown-unknown could break the current risk picture?
premortem_questions.source_quote: unknown unknowns

heuristics.description: Translate risk into explicit mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring instead of generic caution language.
heuristics.source_quote: explicit mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring

heuristics.description: Use systems thinking to surface interdependencies before treating risks as isolated line items.
heuristics.source_quote: integrated ecosystem

heuristics.description: Check whether optimism about conjunctive success is hiding the real downside shape of the plan.
heuristics.source_quote: unwarranted optimism

danger_when: The team is using risk assessment as process theater, multiplying matrices and deliberation without improving decisions or mitigations.
The analysis is focusing on tidy but secondary variables while the main fragility drivers remain unexamined.
risk-assessment.name: Risk Assessment

select_when: Confidence is outrunning evidence and the core drivers remain ambiguous, unstable, or only partly observable.
Commitment size should depend on whether the situation has been converted into measurable risk or remains genuine uncertainty.
Downside is asymmetric and reversibility matters more than producing a single confident forecast.
The decision should be evaluated through ranges, failure envelopes, and thresholds rather than a single point estimate.

failure_modes.mode: Conjunction Fallacy / Unwarranted Optimism
failure_modes.description: Conjunction-fallacy style optimism can make a complex plan look safer than the uncertainty really permits.
failure_modes.mitigation: Decompose the plan into steps and check conjunctive probability: if too many independent things must go right simultaneously, the compound risk is much higher than any single-step estimate suggests.
failure_modes.source_quote: Conjunction Fallacy / Unwarranted Optimism

failure_modes.mode: Uncertainty treated like measurable risk
failure_modes.description: Treating uncertainty like measurable risk can convert ignorance into false precision and irreversible commitments.
failure_modes.mitigation: Label what you don't know honestly: when uncertainty is genuine, use scenario ranges and decision rules rather than converting ignorance into false-precision point estimates.
failure_modes.source_quote: Uncertainty treated like measurable risk

failure_modes.mode: Decision Paralysis from Complexity
failure_modes.description: Decision paralysis from complexity can trap the team in richer scenario language without commitment rules.
failure_modes.mitigation: Set commitment rules for each scenario: pair scenario analysis with explicit 'if X then we do Y' triggers so richer uncertainty language does not become richer paralysis.
failure_modes.source_quote: Decision Paralysis from Complexity

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.description: What part of this plan is genuinely measurable risk, and what part is uncertainty that has not earned point estimates yet?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What part of this plan is genuinely measurable risk, and what part is uncertainty that has not earned point estimates yet?

premortem_questions.description: If this decision goes wrong because the distribution itself moved, which threshold, dependency, or commitment fails first?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If this decision goes wrong because the distribution itself moved, which threshold, dependency, or commitment in this plan fails first?

heuristics.description: Classify the situation before committing so commitment size matches certainty level.
heuristics.source_quote: Classify before committing

heuristics.description: Use scenario analysis and simulations to inspect ranges and failure envelopes instead of one base-case forecast.
heuristics.source_quote: simulations are used

heuristics.description: Prefer reversible probes or no-regrets moves when uncertainty has not yet been converted into stable risk.
heuristics.source_quote: "big bets" ... or "no regrets" moves

heuristics.description: Precommit exit conditions before escalating so the team does not have to invent kill criteria while stress, sunk effort, or narrative attachment are already active.
heuristics.source_quote: big bets ... only after uncertainty has been converted into something decision-worthy

danger_when: Stable reference classes, credible probabilities, and operator-level thresholds already exist, so continued uncertainty framing would only avoid disciplined execution.
The model is being used to extend analysis without changing commitment size, reversibility, or decision thresholds.
risk-vs-uncertainty.name: Risk Vs Uncertainty

select_when: Recurring failures keep surfacing as different symptoms even though the same underlying mechanism is likely recreating the problem.
The next action should change the machine, not just fix the individual case, because process design, ownership, incentives, or environment will recreate the failure.
The apparent cause is too close to the event and is probably proximate, emotionally satisfying, or politically convenient rather than truly generative.
A problem of real consequence is too complex to solve without first being cleaved into logical, manageable parts.

failure_modes.mode: Premature Analysis/Jumping to Conclusions (Othello Trap)
failure_modes.description: Jumping into analysis too early can lock the team into tools before the underlying structure of the problem is clear.
failure_modes.mitigation: Validate the problem frame before choosing an analysis tool: spend time defining the problem structure so the tool fits the cause, not the other way around.
failure_modes.source_quote: Premature Analysis/Jumping to Conclusions (Othello Trap)

failure_modes.mode: Focusing on Symptoms, Not Drivers
failure_modes.description: Symptom chasing leaves the deeper machine-level driver untouched, so the problem recurs.
failure_modes.mitigation: Go deeper than the first plausible explanation: require at least three 'why' iterations before accepting a root cause, to avoid fixing symptoms while the driver persists.
failure_modes.source_quote: Focusing on Symptoms, Not Drivers

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the search for one root cause erases a causal stack
failure_modes.description: RCA breaks when the search for one neat root cause erases a causal stack of interacting failures.
failure_modes.mitigation: Allow for multiple interacting causes: when a single root cause feels too neat, map the causal stack and check whether removing just one factor would actually prevent recurrence.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the search for one root cause erases a causal stack

premortem_questions.description: Why does the problem exist?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Why does the problem exist?

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.description: Is the outcome good or bad, and if bad, is the design bad or is the responsible party incapable?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the outcome good or bad? Who is responsible for the outcome? If the outcome is bad, is the Responsible Party incapable and/or is the design bad?

heuristics.description: Use the Five Whys to push past the surface explanation toward the real generative driver.
heuristics.source_quote: The Five Whys

heuristics.description: Disaggregate the problem with logic trees so the full causal picture is visible before action.
heuristics.source_quote: Problem Disaggregation and Logic Trees

heuristics.description: Use fishbone diagrams to trace causes of causes instead of stopping at the first plausible mechanism.
heuristics.source_quote: Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagrams

danger_when: The team is jumping into analysis prematurely without specifying the underlying problem structure or the decision output needed.
The situation depends on several interacting causes, handoffs, or incentive failures and the analysis is forcing one neat root cause anyway.
root-cause-analysis.name: Root Cause Analysis
reframing_when: The question is about an action to take when the diagnostic step has been skipped — 'what should we do?' is being asked before 'what is actually going wrong?'
The question confuses a proximate cause (the immediate trigger) with the root cause (the systemic flaw) — the question is about the match, not the powder keg.
The question asks how to fix a specific component without first disaggregating the problem to understand which components actually matter — diamond-cutting precision is missing.
The question jumps to a solution based on pattern-matching from a prior similar case without verifying that the same root cause applies this time.
root-cause-analysis.reframing_move: Disaggregate before solving: instead of 'how do we fix X?', ask 'what is the root cause of X, and which specific component of X has the highest leverage for change?' — the logic tree reframe breaks the monolithic question into testable parts.

select_when: Learners or teams need staged support to move from novice confusion to independent execution.
People understand the goal but cannot yet perform the process reliably.
Complex or abstract material needs to be broken into manageable steps so working memory is not overloaded.

failure_modes.mode: Complex material can overwhelm working memory when support is removed before the learner has formed ...
failure_modes.description: Complex material can overwhelm working memory when support is removed before the learner has formed a stable process.
failure_modes.mitigation: Fade support gradually based on demonstrated competence: check that the learner can perform each sub-skill independently before removing the scaffold for that component.
failure_modes.source_quote: Scaffolding is essential for teaching complex or abstract topics because it respects the brain's limited working memory capacity

failure_modes.mode: Over-structured guidance can suppress reflective reframing instead of building independent judgment.
failure_modes.description: Over-structured guidance can suppress reflective reframing instead of building independent judgment.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build reflection into the scaffold: at each stage, ask the learner to explain why the current step works, not just how to execute it, to develop judgment alongside procedure.
failure_modes.source_quote: scaffolding-educational conflicts with step-back (step back) when over-structured guidance suppresses reflective reframing.

failure_modes.mode: A rigid sequence can prevent learners from decomposing the problem for themselves once they are read...
failure_modes.description: A rigid sequence can prevent learners from decomposing the problem for themselves once they are ready.
failure_modes.mitigation: Allow self-directed decomposition once readiness is established: loosen the prescribed sequence when the learner shows they can chunk the problem independently.
failure_modes.source_quote: scaffolding-educational conflicts with first-principles-thinking (first principles thinking) when rigid instructional sequence blocks independent decomposition.

premortem_questions.description: What support is still genuinely necessary, and what support should already be fading?
premortem_questions.source_quote: move from novice confusion to independent execution

premortem_questions.description: Are we teaching steps that help the learner think, or only steps that make the teacher feel organized?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Scaffolding addresses the crucial gap between knowing what needs to be done and figuring out how to execute it.

premortem_questions.description: What would independent performance look like if the scaffold were removed tomorrow?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The model adds value when people understand the goal but cannot yet perform the process reliably.

heuristics.description: Break the task into simpler units that working memory can absorb and connect over time.
heuristics.source_quote: complex idea broken down into smaller, simpler units

heuristics.description: Model the process explicitly first, then remove support as the learner becomes reliable.
heuristics.source_quote: cultivates the ability to think critically and eventually self-reflect without external prompting

heuristics.description: Use scaffolding to bridge strategy and execution, not to replace independent reasoning permanently.
heuristics.source_quote: forces the articulation of the process in simple, doable steps

danger_when: Instructional sequencing has become so rigid that it blocks independent decomposition or reflective reframing.
The learner is already operating independently and more support would only create dependence.
scaffolding-educational.name: Scaffolding Educational

select_when: Complexity exceeds current capacity and the learner or team can master the next increment but not yet the whole system.
Sequencing, pacing, and support quality will determine whether people build usable competence or just accumulate confusion.
Temporary structure should help people become independent later instead of remaining permanently dependent on a framework.
A complex concept or workflow needs to be made tangible through simpler chunks, examples, and staged handoff.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when complexity exceeds current capacity.
failure_modes.description: Learners can drown in complexity when they are asked to carry the whole system before mastering the next digestible chunk.
failure_modes.mitigation: Chunk complexity into digestible pieces and release each one only when the learner demonstrates mastery of the previous chunk.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when complexity exceeds current capacity.

failure_modes.mode: Danger When the Scaffold Never Comes Down.
failure_modes.description: A scaffold can harden into bureaucracy when the temporary support never gets removed and autonomy never transfers.
failure_modes.mitigation: Build in scaffold-removal checkpoints: define when temporary support should be withdrawn and ownership transferred, preventing the scaffold from hardening into permanent dependency.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger When the Scaffold Never Comes Down.

failure_modes.mode: The Law of the Instrument (Maslow's Hammer)
failure_modes.description: Framework-led teaching can drift into model-imposition when the instructor’s favorite structure outruns evidence about what the learner actually needs.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test framework fit against the learner's actual gap: verify that the structure being imposed matches what the learner needs, not what the instructor prefers.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Law of the Instrument (Maslow's Hammer)

premortem_questions.description: What is the next chunk this learner can reliably master, and what part of the full problem still exceeds current capacity?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Most useful when complexity exceeds current capacity.

premortem_questions.description: Which support should still be present now, and which one has already become drag instead of a bridge to independence?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Best when temporary structure can later be removed.

premortem_questions.description: Is this information chunked and simplified enough to be useful for the intended audience without becoming useless accuracy?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is this information chunked and simplified enough to be useful for decision-making by the intended audience?

heuristics.description: Break complex work into smaller units and hand over responsibility gradually rather than asking for mastery in one jump.
heuristics.source_quote: I Do, We Do, You Do

heuristics.description: Sequence explanations, modeling, probing, feedback, and mini-tasks so support evolves with the learner’s actual progress.
heuristics.source_quote: Provision of Knowledge; Demonstration and Modeling; Questioning and Probing; Feedback and Correction; Task Restructuring.

heuristics.description: Keep the scaffold temporary by removing supports as soon as independent performance becomes possible.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when temporary structure can later be removed.

danger_when: The scaffold has become permanent bureaucracy that blocks transfer of judgment, autonomy, or true mastery.
A familiar framework is being imposed unquestioningly even though its assumptions do not actually fit the problem.
scaffolding.name: Scaffolding

select_when: Fixed costs are high enough that spreading infrastructure or knowledge across more volume could create a real structural advantage.
Standardization, throughput, or process learning can improve with repeated use instead of requiring bespoke reinvention each time.
The organization needs to identify the few levers that scale output rather than adding more low-leverage activity.
A capability can be reused across many customers or units if the team designs the system for leverage.

failure_modes.mode: scale magnifies defects, coordination failures, or mediocre quality
failure_modes.description: Scale can magnify defects or coordination failures faster than the system can repair them.
failure_modes.mitigation: Monitor defect rates and coordination costs as volume grows: scale amplifies problems as fast as it amplifies output, so build quality feedback loops that grow with the operation.
failure_modes.source_quote: scale magnifies defects, coordination failures, or mediocre quality

failure_modes.mode: Busyness Paradox
failure_modes.description: Teams can confuse busyness with leverage and miss the few activities that actually scale results.
failure_modes.mitigation: Focus scaling effort on the few activities with genuine increasing returns: audit which operations actually become cheaper or better at scale versus those that just get busier.
failure_modes.source_quote: Busyness Paradox

failure_modes.mode: Ignoring Quality for Volume
failure_modes.description: Pursuing more volume can erode the quality or differentiation that made the offering valuable.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect the quality differentiator: set explicit quality floors and brand standards that cannot be traded away in pursuit of volume growth.
failure_modes.source_quote: Ignoring Quality for Volume

premortem_questions.description: Which defect would scale faster than our ability to detect and correct it?
premortem_questions.source_quote: scale magnifies defects

premortem_questions.description: What fixed infrastructure or knowledge cost are we really spreading, and is the volume enough to matter?
premortem_questions.source_quote: spread infrastructure, knowledge, or distribution costs

premortem_questions.description: Are we scaling an advantage, or merely scaling activity?
premortem_questions.source_quote: being busy with being productive

heuristics.description: Focus on the few revenue, cost, and utilization levers that move the whole system.
heuristics.source_quote: drivers of the problem

heuristics.description: Treat the organization like a machine with key measurement points and adjustable levers.
heuristics.source_quote: machine

heuristics.description: Do not boil the ocean; gather only enough facts to support the needed scaling choice.
heuristics.source_quote: DON'T BOIL THE OCEAN

danger_when: Pursuing volume would magnify defects, coordination failures, or mediocre quality faster than the system can correct them.
Scale is being treated as a universal good even though local fit or differentiation is what currently creates value.
scale-economies.name: Scale Economies

select_when: People need durable pattern recognition and usable retrieval under pressure, not just isolated facts or one-off memorization.
New material must attach to prior knowledge through analogy, chunking, scaffolding, or a clearer underlying map.
A recommendation or process needs mental compression that helps people decide faster and communicate more clearly later.
The real bottleneck is weak structure-building rather than missing effort alone.

failure_modes.mode: Facts must hang together on a latticework of theory.
failure_modes.description: Facts stay disconnected and hard to retrieve when learners never build the map that lets details hang together on a usable structure.
failure_modes.mitigation: Teach the organizing structure before the details: give learners the map first so new facts have a place to attach, rather than accumulating disconnected knowledge.
failure_modes.source_quote: Facts must hang together on a latticework of theory.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the schema becomes a substitute for re-seeing reality.
failure_modes.description: A familiar schema can become a trap when it gets applied automatically to a novel problem that does not fit its assumptions.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit schema fitness: when applying a familiar framework to a new problem, check whether the schema's assumptions still hold — if not, build a new one rather than force-fitting.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the schema becomes a substitute for re-seeing reality.

failure_modes.mode: Risk: Framework Soup and Over-Complexity
failure_modes.description: Framework soup can replace judgment when teams accumulate abstract models faster than they internalize their real purpose.
failure_modes.mitigation: Limit framework intake to what can be practiced: for each new model introduced, require a concrete application exercise before adding another, to prevent abstract accumulation without internalization.
failure_modes.source_quote: Risk: Framework Soup and Over-Complexity

premortem_questions.description: What map or analogy would let this new idea connect to something the learner already understands?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when new material must connect to prior knowledge.

premortem_questions.description: Where are we using a familiar template to classify the case quickly instead of checking whether it really fits this reality?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger when the schema becomes a substitute for re-seeing reality.

premortem_questions.description: What would we have to believe to accept a different schema that contradicts the one we are using now?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What would you have to believe?"

heuristics.description: Use chunking, scaffolding, and analogy so new material can attach to prior knowledge and become retrievable later.
heuristics.source_quote: Works when new material must connect to prior knowledge.

heuristics.description: Build a stable map that compresses the recommendation enough to guide later decisions instead of leaving people with scattered details.
heuristics.source_quote: Best when leaders need usable mental compression.

heuristics.description: Mobilize multiple frames when a familiar schema is dominating too early and may be blocking a better diagnosis.
heuristics.source_quote: Mobilize Multiple Frames

danger_when: A familiar template is being used as a substitute for re-seeing the concrete reality in front of the team.
Too many frameworks are being piled up without internalizing their core purpose, creating framework soup instead of a usable map.
schema-acquisition.name: Schema Acquisition

select_when: Causal claims, competing explanations, or high-cost choices need to be resolved by evidence rather than authority, intuition, or politics.
Hypotheses can be falsified clearly and the team can define disconfirming evidence before diving into the data.
Theory generation and evidence evaluation can be separated enough to keep the test from becoming answer defense.
The risk is not lack of data but lack of a disciplined test structure for deciding what the data should settle.

failure_modes.mode: Confirmation Bias
failure_modes.description: Confirmation bias can turn evidence gathering into answer defense instead of genuine pressure-testing.
failure_modes.mitigation: Design falsifiable tests: before gathering evidence, specify what result would disprove the hypothesis, and commit to accepting that outcome.
failure_modes.source_quote: Confirmation Bias

failure_modes.mode: Anchoring Bias
failure_modes.description: Anchoring on the first hypothesis can block equal evaluation of alternative explanations.
failure_modes.mitigation: Generate competing hypotheses upfront: require at least two alternative explanations and evaluate evidence against all of them, not just the first.
failure_modes.source_quote: Anchoring Bias

failure_modes.mode: Motivated Reasoning
failure_modes.description: Motivated reasoning can make the process look scientific while quietly steering evidence toward the desired conclusion.
failure_modes.mitigation: Separate the evidence-gathering process from the hypothesis advocate: assign different people to collect data and to defend the preferred theory.
failure_modes.source_quote: Motivated Reasoning

premortem_questions.description: What would you have to believe?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What would you have to believe?"

premortem_questions.description: What key outputs can I measure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What key outputs can I measure?"

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Is the worst case bad enough?"

heuristics.description: Structure the work with a hypothesis pyramid so each sub-claim can be tested conclusively.
heuristics.source_quote: Hypothesis Pyramid

heuristics.description: Do not boil the ocean; gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the hypothesis.
heuristics.source_quote: "Don't Boil the Ocean"

heuristics.description: Use the dialectic standard so every thesis meets an antithesis before synthesis.
heuristics.source_quote: Dialectic Standard

danger_when: A politically protected hypothesis cannot really be falsified, so the process would keep the language of science while losing the substance.
Measurement proxies are convenient but weakly connected to the actual phenomenon, creating disciplined-looking but misleading conclusions.
scientific-method-evidence-testing.name: Scientific Method Evidence Testing

select_when: The first-order win may create a second-order loss in incentives, trust, coordination, resilience, or future option value downstream.
A move looks locally attractive, but the real outcome depends on delayed reactions, hidden dependencies, or system adaptation.
The current plan optimizes one visible metric while leaving the downstream chain of effects unstated.
The decision needs a dependency map and a clear threshold where the local gain could flip into later system damage.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when second-order storytelling outruns evidence
failure_modes.description: Second-order storytelling can outrun evidence and turn sophistication into speculation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require second-order claims to cite a mechanism and a timeline: if the downstream effect cannot be specified concretely, it is speculation, not analysis.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when second-order storytelling outruns evidence

failure_modes.mode: First-Order Win, Second-Order Loss
failure_modes.description: A visible first-order win can hide a second-order loss caused by overloaded dependencies, delayed reactions, or metric protection.
failure_modes.mitigation: Map second-order effects explicitly: for every first-order win, list what breaks, overloads, or shifts in the layer below, and verify whether the net is still positive.
failure_modes.source_quote: First-Order Win, Second-Order Loss

failure_modes.mode: Local relief that teaches the wrong behavior
failure_modes.description: Local relief can teach surrounding teams the wrong behavior and make the next cycle slower, noisier, or harder to govern.
failure_modes.mitigation: Check behavioral spillovers: ask whether the local fix teaches surrounding teams a lesson that makes the next cycle easier or harder.
failure_modes.source_quote: Local relief that teaches the wrong behavior

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "Is the worst case bad enough?"

premortem_questions.description: How does what you're doing solve the problem?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "How does what you’re doing solve the problem?"

premortem_questions.description: What is the system that exists within this area of mastery?
premortem_questions.source_quote: "What is the system that exists within this area of mastery? What’s the nature of cause and effect? What are some analogies?"

heuristics.description: Map the dependency chain before commitment instead of approving the plan from the immediate gain alone.
heuristics.source_quote: Map the dependency chain this intervention assumes will hold

heuristics.description: Name the first threshold where delay, overload, or information-hiding would flip the outcome from gain to damage.
heuristics.source_quote: identify the first threshold where delay, overload, or information-hiding would cascade into a worse second-order outcome

heuristics.description: Test what behavior the intervention will teach surrounding teams to repeat on the next cycle.
heuristics.source_quote: If this intervention works locally, what behavior will it teach the surrounding teams to repeat

danger_when: Second-order language is outrunning evidence, so the chain of consequences is speculative rather than anchored in mechanism, actor reaction, or constraints.
The model is being used to avoid choosing at all instead of naming what threshold would justify acting under uncertainty.
second-order-thinking.name: Second Order Thinking
reframing_when: The question asks about the immediate outcome of a decision without considering what happens next — the real question is about the cascading consequences, not the first-order result.
The question frames the decision as a static snapshot when the situation is dynamic — actors will adapt, incentives will shift, and the second-order effects may reverse the first-order win.
The question optimizes a local metric that may degrade trust, coordination, resilience, or future option value downstream — the question needs a longer time horizon.
second-order-thinking.reframing_move: Extend the time horizon: ask 'and then what?' — push past the first-order answer to the second and third order consequences. The reframed question is about the trajectory, not the snapshot.

select_when: People already know the right move, but stress, temptation, distraction, or impulse keeps defeating execution.
Long-term goals are repeatedly losing to short-term urges or reactive time management.
The environment can be redesigned so behavior depends less on willpower and more on routines and prompts.
The bottleneck is follow-through, attention discipline, or emotional regulation rather than missing knowledge.

failure_modes.mode: Ego Depletion
failure_modes.description: Ego depletion makes good judgment harder once mental effort and attention are exhausted.
failure_modes.mitigation: Design the environment, not the willpower: when self-control is depleted, use defaults, checklists, and pre-commitments to protect good decisions from fatigue.
failure_modes.source_quote: Ego Depletion

failure_modes.mode: firefighter time management style
failure_modes.description: Reactive firefighting can crowd out important work even when priorities are already known.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect priority time structurally: block calendar time for important-not-urgent work and route reactive requests through a triage filter instead of relying on in-the-moment discipline.
failure_modes.source_quote: firefighter time management style

failure_modes.mode: Design Problem
failure_modes.description: Self-control misfires when it becomes a character judgment instead of a design problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Reframe self-control failures as system failures: when people consistently fail to follow through, redesign the process, incentives, or workload rather than blaming character.
failure_modes.source_quote: Design Problem

premortem_questions.description: Am I rested enough to make this choice deliberately instead of impulsively?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Am I physically and mentally rested enough

premortem_questions.description: Is this task important, or am I just putting out a fire?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Important vs. Urgent

premortem_questions.description: What minimum viable standard is good enough to act without perfectionism?
premortem_questions.source_quote: good enough

heuristics.description: Pause and ask what is happening before acting on the first impulse.
heuristics.source_quote: What's happening?

heuristics.description: Use the Eisenhower matrix to protect important work from reactive drift.
heuristics.source_quote: Eisenhower Matrix

heuristics.description: Shape behavior through routines and prompts instead of relying on raw willpower alone.
heuristics.source_quote: systems rather than pure willpower

danger_when: Failure is being treated as a moral flaw even though sleep, incentives, or environment design are the real issue.
The team is using self-control rhetoric to justify endless deliberation instead of choosing and acting.
self-control.name: Self Control

select_when: The visible performance problem is really a motivation-architecture problem around autonomy, competence, or relatedness.
People can technically do the work, but ownership, initiative, or creativity remain weak.
Leaders can redesign conditions such as decision latitude, feedback quality, or growth path rather than just adding pressure.
Compliance is possible, but commitment is missing because the environment feels controlling rather than enabling.

failure_modes.mode: Thwarting Autonomy with Micro-management
failure_modes.description: Micro-management can throttle commitment by reducing autonomy at the moment people most need ownership.
failure_modes.mitigation: Protect autonomy within guardrails: give people meaningful choice over how they accomplish goals while keeping the goal and quality standard non-negotiable.
failure_modes.source_quote: Thwarting Autonomy with Micro-management

failure_modes.mode: not valuing the activity, not feeling competent, or not expecting a desired outcome
failure_modes.description: Amotivation is often misread as laziness when the real issue is low competence, low value, or low expected outcome.
failure_modes.mitigation: Diagnose amotivation before judging effort: check whether the person lacks competence, sees no value in the task, or expects no chance of success — each requires a different intervention.
failure_modes.source_quote: not valuing the activity, not feeling competent, or not expecting a desired outcome

failure_modes.mode: Autonomy Talk Masks Missing Standards
failure_modes.description: Empowerment language can become empty when leaders refuse to set clear quality bars or accountability.
failure_modes.mitigation: Back empowerment language with real accountability: pair autonomy with clear quality bars, feedback loops, and consequences so empowerment does not become abandonment.
failure_modes.source_quote: Autonomy Talk Masks Missing Standards

premortem_questions.description: Which of autonomy, competence, or relatedness is actually missing here?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Autonomy, Competence, and Relatedness

premortem_questions.description: Are people failing because they cannot do the work, or because the environment is suppressing commitment?
premortem_questions.source_quote: motivation architecture is the real bottleneck

premortem_questions.description: What would the team have to believe for this motivation design to feel self-directed rather than controlled?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.description: Think autonomy, not control, by setting the outcome while allowing room in the method.
heuristics.source_quote: Think autonomy, not control

heuristics.description: Build competence with tasks that are challenging enough to support mastery without inducing helplessness.
heuristics.source_quote: The Path to Mastery

heuristics.description: Align low-level goals to a meaningful top-level purpose so effort feels internally coherent.
heuristics.source_quote: Aligning Purpose

danger_when: Autonomy language is being used to avoid setting standards, quality bars, or accountability.
The model is being treated as a reason to ignore compensation or necessary structure for routine work.
self-determination-theory.name: Self Determination Theory

select_when: Useful coordination can emerge from local rules, incentives, and feedback without heavy central scripting.
The challenge is to shape interaction conditions so order can arise from distributed behavior.
A system needs enough autonomy and signal flow to organize itself rather than wait for constant top-down control.
Teams are managing an adaptive network where local behavior creates the larger pattern.

failure_modes.mode: Order can emerge from local interactions rather than heavy central control.
failure_modes.description: Distributed systems underperform when local actors have no clear rules, signal flow, or feedback from which order can emerge.
failure_modes.mitigation: Define the minimal rules, signal flows, and feedback loops that local actors need before expecting useful emergent coordination.
failure_modes.source_quote: Order can emerge from local interactions rather than heavy central control.

failure_modes.mode: The model explains how coherent order can arise without top-down command of every step.
failure_modes.description: Leaders can smother adaptive coordination by over-scripting every local move that should be solved in place.
failure_modes.mitigation: Identify which decisions should be local and which need central coordination: over-scripting kills adaptation, but under-specifying kills coherence.
failure_modes.source_quote: The model explains how coherent order can arise without top-down command of every step.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when self-organization is treated as permission for no design or no accountability.
failure_modes.description: Self-organization becomes an excuse for weak ownership when teams remove structure instead of designing the right local constraints.
failure_modes.mitigation: Pair self-organization with explicit ownership: when removing top-down structure, replace it with local accountability and visible decision rights, not a vacuum.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when self-organization is treated as permission for no design or no accountability.

premortem_questions.description: Which local rules or incentives are producing the large-scale pattern we keep observing?
premortem_questions.source_quote: The outcome results from local interactions among parts of the system.

premortem_questions.description: What minimum structure must stay in place so distributed coordination does not collapse into noise or drift?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Danger when the focus on emergence is misapplied as making it up as you go along.

premortem_questions.description: Where would central control help less than better feedback among local actors?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Order arises through interaction of parts within the system.

heuristics.description: Design local rules, incentives, and feedback so the larger pattern can organize itself.
heuristics.source_quote: Order emerges from the interaction of simpler parts within a system.

heuristics.description: Do not centralize decisions that the local environment can solve faster with the right signals.
heuristics.source_quote: The whole comes into existence through the integration and synthesis of many components.

heuristics.description: Keep self-organization paired with explicit feedback and accountability instead of romanticizing spontaneity.
heuristics.source_quote: Danger when self-organization is treated as permission for no design or no accountability.

danger_when: Self-organization is being used to excuse weak ownership, absent constraints, or missing feedback channels.
The problem requires centralized sequencing or compliance discipline rather than distributed adaptation.
self-organization-and-emergent-order.name: Self Organization And Emergent Order

select_when: A strategy, offer, or position needs to communicate credibility under uncertainty, and the current message may not align with the actual underlying quality.
The real risk is that appearance, framing, or symbolic gestures are substituting for truth-aligned capability or commitment.
A team needs to make the intended action legible to others without relying on vague mission language or intellectual laziness.
The next move should clarify what behavior, proof, or costly commitment will make the message trustworthy.

failure_modes.mode: Cheap symbolic signals can substitute for real capability, accountability, or truth-aligned behavior...
failure_modes.description: Cheap symbolic signals can substitute for real capability, accountability, or truth-aligned behavior.
failure_modes.mitigation: Audit signals for substance: require every symbolic claim to be backed by a verifiable capability, commitment, or track record.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when cheap symbolic signals are allowed to substitute for real capability, accountability, or truth-aligned behavior.

failure_modes.mode: Message optimization can hide a weak underlying offer when impression management outruns substance.
failure_modes.description: Message optimization can hide a weak underlying offer when impression management outruns substance.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test whether the message would survive due diligence: before sending a signal, ask what a skeptical recipient would check and whether the underlying substance would hold up.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the message is optimized for impression management while the underlying offer, reasoning, or execution standard remains weak.

failure_modes.mode: The Problem with Accuracy
failure_modes.description: Signaling fails when a message is accurate in the abstract but too useless to guide concrete action.
failure_modes.mitigation: Make signals actionable: translate abstract positioning into concrete guidance that a recipient can use to make a decision or take a next step.
failure_modes.source_quote: The Problem with Accuracy

premortem_questions.description: What costly behavior would prove this signal is real rather than symbolic?
premortem_questions.source_quote: cheap symbolic signals

premortem_questions.description: If the underlying offer stayed the same, would this message still persuade mainly because it looks good?
premortem_questions.source_quote: optimized for impression management

premortem_questions.description: What concrete decision or action should this signal change for the receiver?
premortem_questions.source_quote: it fails to help the recipient make decisions

heuristics.description: Use signals that are costly enough or behaviorally concrete enough to separate appearance from substance.
heuristics.source_quote: cheap symbolic signals

heuristics.description: Translate strategy into action-legible communication so the receiver knows what to do, not just what to admire.
heuristics.source_quote: Bridging the What and How

heuristics.description: Treat impression quality as a diagnostic clue, not as proof that the underlying capability is real.
heuristics.source_quote: appearance-trustworthy behavior rather than be-trustworthy behavior

danger_when: The work is mostly impression management, and better signaling would only make a weak underlying offer look cleaner.
The team already has clear truthful proof and now mainly needs execution rather than another round of message engineering.
signaling.name: Signaling

select_when: Complexity is blocking action, alignment, or recall more than it is improving the underlying decision.
The team needs the essential governing idea, not the full story of every analysis branch that produced it.
A recommendation must survive handoff to operators who need a clear rule, intent, or checkpoint rather than expert nuance.

failure_modes.mode: Mistaking the Tool for the Problem
failure_modes.description: Maslow's hammer can turn simplification into forcing the nearest easy frame onto the wrong problem.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test frame fit before simplifying: verify that the simplified model shares the structural features of the actual problem, not just surface similarity.
failure_modes.source_quote: Mistaking the Tool for the Problem

failure_modes.mode: Premature Abstraction
failure_modes.description: Premature abstraction can make the message elegant while stripping away the concrete guidance operators actually need.
failure_modes.mitigation: Preserve operational specificity: simplify the explanation, but keep the concrete guidance, boundary conditions, and caveats that operators need to act safely.
failure_modes.source_quote: Premature Abstraction

failure_modes.mode: Oversimplifying risk can remove the caveat or boundary that makes the recommendation safe to use.
failure_modes.description: Oversimplifying risk can remove the caveat or boundary that makes the recommendation safe to use.
failure_modes.mitigation: Flag removed caveats explicitly: when simplifying, note what was stripped away and under what conditions the simplified version becomes dangerous or misleading.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when simplification removes the very caveat, boundary, or uncertainty that makes the decision safe to use in reality.

premortem_questions.description: What's your main core point?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What's your main (core) point?

premortem_questions.description: If the audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?
premortem_questions.source_quote: If the audience remembers only one thing, what should it be?

premortem_questions.description: Is the worst case bad enough?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the Worst Case Bad Enough?

heuristics.description: Find the core before carrying the full edifice of analysis into the handoff.
heuristics.source_quote: Finding the Core

heuristics.description: Use the rule of three to give the audience a durable verbal roadmap rather than a bag of details.
heuristics.source_quote: The Rule of Three

heuristics.description: Use the Feynman technique to expose blind spots before compressing the explanation for someone else.
heuristics.source_quote: The Feynman Technique

danger_when: Simplification would remove the caveat, boundary, or uncertainty that makes the decision safe to use in reality.
The case depends on expert nuance or accuracy levels that cannot be safely compressed into a short governing rule.
simplification.name: Simplification

select_when: A team needs to separate competing modes of thought so risk review, creativity, evidence checking, and synthesis do not jam each other.
Meetings are stuck in unstructured argument and would benefit from deliberate perspective rotation.
The same group must examine one issue from several angles without mistaking the first dominant frame for the whole problem.
Role separation would reduce cognitive load and improve objectivity in a complex review.

failure_modes.mode: Most useful when a team needs to separate competing modes of thought.
failure_modes.description: Unstructured argument can jam evidence, risk review, creativity, and synthesis together until none of them gets done well.
failure_modes.mitigation: Sequence thinking modes deliberately: separate evidence gathering, risk assessment, creative generation, and evaluation into distinct rounds with clear transitions.
failure_modes.source_quote: Most useful when a team needs to separate competing modes of thought.

failure_modes.mode: Danger when the roles become performative labels.
failure_modes.description: Perspective rotation can become empty performance when people adopt the labels without changing the evidence standard or cognitive task.
failure_modes.mitigation: Require genuine mode shifts: when switching hats, change the evidence standard and question type, not just the label — each mode should produce different outputs.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when the roles become performative labels.
